functions.name: thermo.phase_identification.vapor_score_Tpc
documentation:
Compute a vapor score representing how vapor-like a phase is
(higher, above zero = more vapor like) using the following criteria

$$
T - \sum_i z_i T_{c,i}
$$

# Parameters

T

: Temperature, \[K\]

Tcs

: Critical temperatures of all species, \[K\]

zs

: Mole fractions of the phase being identified, \[-\]

# Returns

score

: Vapor like score, \[-\]

# Examples

A flash of equimolar CO2/n-hexane at 300 K and 1 MPa is computed, and there
is a two phase solution. The phase must be identified for each result:

Liquid-like phase:

```pycon
>>> vapor_score_Tpc(T=300.0, Tcs=[304.2, 507.6], zs=[0.21834418746784942, 0.7816558125321506])
-163.18879226903942
```

Vapor-like phase:

```pycon
>>> vapor_score_Tpc(T=300.0, Tcs=[304.2, 507.6], zs=[0.9752234962374878, 0.024776503762512052])
-9.239540865294941
```

In this result, the vapor phase is not identified as a gas at all! It has
a mass density of ~ 20 kg/m^3, which would usually be called a gas by most
people.

# Notes

functions.name: thermo.phase_identification.vapor_score_Vpc
documentation:
Compute a vapor score representing how vapor-like a phase is
(higher, above zero = more vapor like) using the following criteria

$$
V - \sum_i z_i V_{c,i}
$$

# Parameters

V

: Molar volume, \[m^3/mol\]

Vcs

: Critical molar volumes of all species, \[m^3/mol\]

zs

: Mole fractions of the phase being identified, \[-\]

# Returns

score

: Vapor like score, \[-\]

# Examples

A flash of equimolar CO2/n-hexane at 300 K and 1 MPa is computed, and there
is a two phase solution. The phase must be identified for each result:

Liquid-like phase:

```pycon
>>> vapor_score_Vpc(V=0.00011316308855449715, Vcs=[9.4e-05, 0.000368], zs=[0.21834418746784942, 0.7816558125321506])
-0.000195010604079
```

Vapor-like phase:

```pycon
>>> vapor_score_Vpc(V=0.0023406573328250335, Vcs=[9.4e-05, 0.000368], zs=[0.9752234962374878, 0.024776503762512052])
0.002239868570
```

# Notes

functions.name: thermo.phase_identification.vapor_score_Tpc_weighted
documentation:
Compute a vapor score representing how vapor-like a phase is
(higher, above zero = more vapor like) using the following criteria, said
to be implemented in ECLIPSE [^footnote-1]:

$$
T - T_{pc}
$$

$$
T_{p,c} = r_1 \frac{\sum_j x_j V_{c,j}T_{c,j}}{\sum_j x_j V_{c,j}}
$$

# Parameters

T

: Temperature, \[K\]

Tcs

: Critical temperatures of all species, \[K\]

Vcs

: Critical molar volumes of all species, \[m^3/mol\]

zs

: Mole fractions of the phase being identified, \[-\]

r1

: Tuning factor, \[-\]

# Returns

score

: Vapor like score, \[-\]

# Examples

A flash of equimolar CO2/n-hexane at 300 K and 1 MPa is computed, and there
is a two phase solution. The phase must be identified for each result:

Liquid-like phase:

```pycon
>>> vapor_score_Tpc_weighted(T=300.0, Tcs=[304.2, 507.6], Vcs=[9.4e-05, 0.000368], zs=[0.21834418746784942, 0.7816558125321506])
-194.0535694431
```

Vapor-like phase:

```pycon
>>> vapor_score_Tpc_weighted(T=300.0, Tcs=[304.2, 507.6], Vcs=[9.4e-05, 0.000368], zs=[0.9752234962374878, 0.024776503762512052])
-22.60037521107
```

As can be seen, the CO2-phase is incorrectly identified as a liquid.

# Notes

# References

[^footnote-1]: Bennett, Jim, and Kurt A. G. Schmidt. "Comparison of Phase
    Identification Methods Used in Oil Industry Flow Simulations." Energy &
    Fuels 31, no. 4 (April 20, 2017): 3370-79.
    <https://doi.org/10.1021/acs.energyfuels.6b02316>.

functions.name: thermo.phase_identification.vapor_score_Tpc_Vpc
documentation:
Compute a vapor score representing how vapor-like a phase is
(higher, above zero = more vapor like) using the following criteria, said
to be implemented in Multiflash [^footnote-1]:

$$
VT^2 - V_{pc} T_{pc}^2
$$

# Parameters

T

: Temperature, \[K\]

V

: Molar volume, \[m^3/mol\]

Tcs

: Critical temperatures of all species, \[K\]

Vcs

: Critical molar volumes of all species, \[m^3/mol\]

zs

: Mole fractions of the phase being identified, \[-\]

# Returns

score

: Vapor like score, \[-\]

# Examples

A flash of equimolar CO2/n-hexane at 300 K and 1 MPa is computed, and there
is a two phase solution. The phase must be identified for each result:

Liquid-like phase:

```pycon
>>> vapor_score_Tpc_Vpc(T=300.0, V=0.00011316308855449715, Tcs=[304.2, 507.6], Vcs=[9.4e-05, 0.000368], zs=[0.21834418746784942, 0.7816558125321506])
-55.932094761
```

Vapor-like phase:

```pycon
>>> vapor_score_Tpc_Vpc(T=300.0, V=0.0023406573328250335, Tcs=[304.2, 507.6], Vcs=[9.4e-05, 0.000368], zs=[0.9752234962374878, 0.024776503762512052])
201.020821992
```

# Notes

# References

[^footnote-1]: Bennett, Jim, and Kurt A. G. Schmidt. "Comparison of Phase
    Identification Methods Used in Oil Industry Flow Simulations." Energy &
    Fuels 31, no. 4 (April 20, 2017): 3370-79.
    <https://doi.org/10.1021/acs.energyfuels.6b02316>.

functions.name: thermo.phase_identification.vapor_score_Wilson
documentation:
Compute a vapor score representing how vapor-like a phase is
(higher, above zero = more vapor like) using the Rachford-Rice Wilson
method of Perschke [^footnote-1].

After calculating Wilson's K values, the following expression is evaluated
at $\frac{V}{F} = 0.5$; the result is the score.

$$
\sum_i \frac{z_i(K_i-1)}{1 + \frac{V}{F}(K_i-1)}
$$

# Parameters

T

: Temperature, \[K\]

P

: Pressure, \[Pa\]

zs

: Mole fractions of the phase being identified, \[-\]

Tcs

: Critical temperatures of all species, \[K\]

Pcs

: Critical pressures of all species, \[Pa\]

omegas

: Acentric factors of all species, \[-\]

# Returns

score

: Vapor like score, \[-\]

# Examples

A flash of equimolar CO2/n-hexane at 300 K and 1 MPa is computed, and there
is a two phase solution. The phase must be identified for each result:

Liquid-like phase:

```pycon
>>> vapor_score_Wilson(T=300.0, P=1e6, zs=[.218, .782], Tcs=[304.2, 507.6], Pcs=[7376460.0, 3025000.0], omegas=[0.2252, 0.2975])
-1.16644793
```

Vapor-like phase:

```pycon
>>> vapor_score_Wilson(T=300.0, P=1e6, zs=[.975, .025], Tcs=[304.2, 507.6], Pcs=[7376460.0, 3025000.0], omegas=[0.2252, 0.2975])
1.397678492
```

This method works well in many conditions, like the Wilson equation itself,
but fundamentally it cannot do a great job because it is not tied to the
phase model itself.

A dew point flash at P = 100 Pa for the same mixture shows both phases
being identified as vapor-like:

```pycon
>>> T_dew = 206.40935716944634
>>> P = 100.0
>>> vapor_score_Wilson(T=T_dew, P=P, zs=[0.5, 0.5], Tcs=[304.2, 507.6], Pcs=[7376460.0, 3025000.0], omegas=[0.2252, 0.2975])
1.074361930956633
>>> vapor_score_Wilson(T=T_dew, P=P, zs=[0.00014597910182360052, 0.9998540208981763], Tcs=[304.2, 507.6], Pcs=[7376460.0, 3025000.0], omegas=[0.2252, 0.2975])
0.15021784286075726
```

# Notes

# References

[^footnote-1]: Chang, Yih-Bor. "Development and Application of an Equation of State
    Compositional Simulator," 1990.
    <https://repositories.lib.utexas.edu/handle/2152/80585>.

functions.name: thermo.phase_identification.vapor_score_Poling
documentation:
Compute a vapor score representing how vapor-like a phase is
(higher, above zero = more vapor like) using the isothermal compressibility
`kappa` concept by Poling [^footnote-1].

$$
\text{score} = (\kappa - 0.005 \text{atm}^{-1})
$$

# Parameters

kappa

: Isothermal coefficient of compressibility, \[1/Pa\]

# Returns

score

: Vapor like score, \[-\]

# Examples

CO2 vapor properties computed with Peng-Robinson at 300 K and 1 bar:

```pycon
>>> vapor_score_Poling(1.0054239121594122e-05)
1.013745778995
```

n-hexane liquid properties computed with Peng-Robinson at 300 K and 10 bar:

```pycon
>>> vapor_score_Poling(2.121777078782957e-09)
-0.00478501093
```

# Notes

A second criteria which is not implemented as it does not fit with the
scoring concept is for liquids:

$$
\frac{0.9}{P} < \beta < \frac{3}{P}
$$

# References

[^footnote-1]: Poling, Bruce E., Edward A. Grens, and John M. Prausnitz.
    "Thermodynamic Properties from a Cubic Equation of State: Avoiding
    Trivial Roots and Spurious Derivatives." Industrial & Engineering
    Chemistry Process Design and Development 20, no. 1 (January 1, 1981):
    127-30. <https://doi.org/10.1021/i200012a019>.

functions.name: thermo.phase_identification.vapor_score_PIP
documentation:
Compute a vapor score representing how vapor-like a phase is
(higher, above zero = more vapor like) using the PIP concept.

$$
\text{score} = -(\Pi - 1)
$$

$$
\Pi = V \left[\frac{\frac{\partial^2 P}{\partial V \partial T}} {\frac{\partial P }{\partial T}}- \frac{\frac{\partial^2 P}{\partial V^2}}{\frac{\partial P}{\partial V}} \right]
$$

# Parameters

V

: Molar volume at `T` and `P`, \[m^3/mol\]

dP_dT

: Derivative of `P` with respect to `T`, \[Pa/K\]

dP_dV

: Derivative of `P` with respect to `V`, \[Pa\*mol/m^3\]

d2P_dV2

: Second derivative of `P` with respect to `V`, \[Pa\*mol^2/m^6\]

d2P_dVdT

: Second derivative of `P` with respect to both `V` and `T`, \[Pa\*mol/m^3/K\]

# Returns

score

: Vapor like score, \[-\]

# Examples

CO2 vapor properties computed with Peng-Robinson at 300 K and 1 bar:

```pycon
>>> vapor_score_PIP(0.024809176851423774, 337.0119286073647, -4009021.959558917, 321440573.3615088, -13659.63987996052)
0.016373735005
```

n-hexane liquid properties computed with Peng-Robinson at 300 K and 10 bar:

```pycon
>>> vapor_score_PIP(0.00013038156684574785, 578477.8796379718, -3614798144591.8984, 4.394997991022487e+17, -20247865009.795322)
-10.288635225
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Venkatarathnam, G., and L. R. Oellrich. "Identification of the Phase
    of a Fluid Using Partial Derivatives of Pressure, Volume, and
    Temperature without Reference to Saturation Properties: Applications in
    Phase Equilibria Calculations." Fluid Phase Equilibria 301, no. 2
    (February 25, 2011): 225-33. doi:10.1016/j.fluid.2010.12.001.

functions.name: thermo.phase_identification.vapor_score_Bennett_Schmidt
documentation:
Compute a vapor score representing how vapor-like a phase is
(higher, above zero = more vapor like) using the Bennet-Schmidt
temperature derivative of isobaric expansion suggestion.

$$
\text{score} = -\left(\frac{\partial \beta}{\partial T}\right)
$$

# Parameters

dbeta_dT

: Temperature derivative of isobaric coefficient of a thermal
  expansion, \[1/K^2\]

# Returns

score

: Vapor like score, \[-\]

# Examples

CO2 vapor properties computed with Peng-Robinson at 300 K and 1 bar:

```pycon
>>> vapor_score_Bennett_Schmidt(-1.1776172267959163e-05)
1.1776172267959163e-05
```

n-hexane liquid properties computed with Peng-Robinson at 300 K and 10 bar:

```pycon
>>> vapor_score_Bennett_Schmidt(7.558572848883679e-06)
-7.558572848883679e-06
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Bennett, Jim, and Kurt A. G. Schmidt. "Comparison of Phase
    Identification Methods Used in Oil Industry Flow Simulations." Energy &
    Fuels 31, no. 4 (April 20, 2017): 3370-79.
    <https://doi.org/10.1021/acs.energyfuels.6b02316>.

functions.name: thermo.phase_identification.vapor_score_traces
documentation:
Compute a vapor score representing how vapor-like a phase is
(higher, above zero = more vapor like) using the concept of which phase has
the most of the lightest compound. This nicely sidesteps issues in many
other methods, at the expense that it cannot be applied when there is only
one phase and it is not smart enough to handle liquid-liquid cases.

If no trace components are present, the component with the lowest critical
temperature's concentration is returned. Because of the way this is
implemented, the score is always larger than 1.0.

# Parameters

zs

: Mole fractions of the phase being identified, \[-\]

CASs

: CAS numbers of all components, \[-\]

Tcs

: Critical temperatures of all species, \[K\]

trace_CASs

: Trace components to use for identification; if more than one component
  is given, the first component present in both `CASs` and `trace_CASs`
  is the one used, \[-\]

min_trace

: Minimum concentration to make a phase appear vapor-like; subtracted
  from the concentration which would otherwise be returned, \[-\]

# Returns

score

: Vapor like score, \[-\]

# Examples

A flash of equimolar CO2/n-hexane at 300 K and 1 MPa is computed, and there
is a two phase solution. The phase must be identified for each result:

Liquid-like phase:

```pycon
>>> vapor_score_traces(zs=[.218, .782], Tcs=[304.2, 507.6], CASs=['124-38-9', '110-54-3'])
0.218
```

Vapor-like phase:

```pycon
>>> vapor_score_traces(zs=[.975, .025], Tcs=[304.2, 507.6], CASs=['124-38-9', '110-54-3'])
0.975
```

# Notes

functions.name: thermo.phase_identification.score_phases_S
documentation:
Score all phases according to how wolid they appear given the
provided parameters and a selected method.

A score above zero indicates a solid. More than one phase may have
a score above zero. A score under zero means the phase is a liquid or gas.

# Parameters

phases

: Phases to be identified and sorted, \[-\]

constants

: Constants used in the identification, \[-\]

correlations

: Correlations used in the identification, \[-\]

method

: Setting configuring how the scoring is performed; one of
  ('d2P_dVdT',), \[-\]

S_ID_settings

: Additional configuration options for solid-liquid phase ID, \[-\]

# Returns

scores

: Scores for the phases in the order provided, \[-\]

functions.name: thermo.phase_identification.score_phases_VL
documentation:
Score all phases given the provided parameters and a selected method.

A score above zero indicates a potential gas. More than one phase may have
a score above zero, in which case the highest scoring phase is the gas,
and the other is a liquid.

# Parameters

phases

: Phases to be identified and sorted, \[-\]

constants

: Constants used in the identification, \[-\]

correlations

: Correlations used in the identification, \[-\]

method

: Setting configuring how the scoring is performed; one of
  'Tpc', 'Vpc', 'Tpc Vpc weighted', 'Tpc Vpc', 'Wilson', 'Poling',
  'PIP', 'Bennett-Schmidt', 'Traces', \[-\]

# Returns

scores

: Scores for the phases in the order provided, \[-\]

# Notes

# Examples

```pycon
>>> from thermo import ChemicalConstantsPackage, PropertyCorrelationsPackage, CEOSGas, CEOSLiquid, PRMIX, HeatCapacityGas
>>> constants = ChemicalConstantsPackage(CASs=['124-38-9', '110-54-3'], Vcs=[9.4e-05, 0.000368], MWs=[44.0095, 86.17536], names=['carbon dioxide', 'hexane'], omegas=[0.2252, 0.2975], Pcs=[7376460.0, 3025000.0], Tbs=[194.67, 341.87], Tcs=[304.2, 507.6], Tms=[216.65, 178.075])
>>> correlations = PropertyCorrelationsPackage(constants=constants, skip_missing=True, HeatCapacityGases=[HeatCapacityGas(poly_fit=(50.0, 1000.0, [-3.1115474168865828e-21, 1.39156078498805e-17, -2.5430881416264243e-14, 2.4175307893014295e-11, -1.2437314771044867e-08, 3.1251954264658904e-06, -0.00021220221928610925, 0.000884685506352987, 29.266811602924644])), HeatCapacityGas(poly_fit=(200.0, 1000.0, [1.3740654453881647e-21, -8.344496203280677e-18, 2.2354782954548568e-14, -3.4659555330048226e-11, 3.410703030634579e-08, -2.1693611029230923e-05, 0.008373280796376588, -1.356180511425385, 175.67091124888998]))])
>>> T, P, zs = 300.0, 1e6, [.5, .5]
>>> eos_kwargs = {'Pcs': constants.Pcs, 'Tcs': constants.Tcs, 'omegas': constants.omegas}
>>> gas = CEOSGas(PRMIX, eos_kwargs, HeatCapacityGases=correlations.HeatCapacityGases, T=T, P=P, zs=zs)
>>> liq = CEOSLiquid(PRMIX, eos_kwargs, HeatCapacityGases=correlations.HeatCapacityGases, T=T, P=P, zs=zs)
```

A sampling of different phase identification methods is below:

```pycon
>>> score_phases_VL([gas, liq], constants, correlations, method='PIP')
[1.6409446310, -7.5692120928]
>>> score_phases_VL([gas, liq], constants, correlations, method='Vpc')
[0.00144944049, -0.0001393075288]
>>> score_phases_VL([gas, liq], constants, correlations, method='Tpc Vpc')
[113.181283525, -29.806038704]
>>> score_phases_VL([gas, liq], constants, correlations, method='Bennett-Schmidt')
[0.0003538299416, -2.72255439503e-05]
>>> score_phases_VL([gas, liq], constants, correlations, method='Poling')
[0.1767828268, -0.004516837897]
```

functions.name: thermo.phase_identification.identity_phase_states
documentation:
Identify and the actial phase of all the given phases given the
provided settings.

# Parameters

phases

: Phases to be identified and sorted, \[-\]

constants

: Constants used in the identification, \[-\]

correlations

: Correlations used in the identification, \[-\]

VL_method

: One of {obj}`VL_ID_METHODS`, \[-\]

S_method

: One of {obj}`S_ID_METHODS`, \[-\]

VL_ID_settings

: Additional configuration options for vapor-liquid phase ID, \[-\]

S_ID_settings

: Additional configuration options for solid-liquid phase ID, \[-\]

skip_solids

: Set this to True if no phases are provided which can represent a solid phase, \[-\]

# Returns

gas

: Gas phase, if one was identified, \[-\]

liquids

: Liquids that were identified and sorted, \[-\]

solids

: Solids that were identified and sorted, \[-\]

functions.name: thermo.phase_identification.mini_sort_phases
documentation: return phases

functions.name: thermo.phase_identification.sort_phases
documentation:
Identify and sort all phases given the provided parameters.
This is not a thermodynamic concept; it is just a convinience
method to make the results of the flash more consistent, because
the flash algorithms don't care about density or ordering
the phases.

# Parameters

liquids

: Liquids that were identified, \[-\]

solids

: Solids that were identified, \[-\]

constants

: Constants used in the identification, \[-\]

correlations

: Correlations used in the identification, \[-\]

settings

: Settings object controlling the phase sorting, \[-\]

# Returns

liquids

: Liquids that were identified and sorted, \[-\]

solids

: Solids that were identified and sorted, \[-\]

# Notes

The settings object uses the preferences
`liquid_sort_method`, `liquid_sort_prop`,
`liquid_sort_cmps`, `liquid_sort_cmps_neg`,
and `phase_sort_higher_first`.

# Examples

functions.name: thermo.phase_identification.identify_sort_phases
documentation:
Identify and sort all phases given the provided parameters.

# Parameters

phases

: Phases to be identified and sorted, \[-\]

betas

: Phase molar fractions, \[-\]

constants

: Constants used in the identification, \[-\]

correlations

: Correlations used in the identification, \[-\]

settings

: Settings object controlling the phase ID, \[-\]

skip_solids

: Set this to True if no phases are provided which can represent a solid phase, \[-\]

# Returns

gas

: Gas phase, if one was identified, \[-\]

liquids

: Liquids that were identified and sorted, \[-\]

solids

: Solids that were identified and sorted, \[-\]

betas

: Sorted phase molar fractions, in order (gas, liquids..., solids...) \[-\]

# Notes

This step is very important as although phase objects are
designed to represent a single phase, cubic equations of state
can be switched back and forth by the flash algorithms.
Thermodynamics doesn't care about gases, liquids, or solids;
it just cares about minimizing Gibbs energy!

# Examples

A butanol-water-ethanol flash yields three phases. For brevity we skip
the flash and initialize our `gas`, `liq0`, and `liq1` object with the
correct phase composition. Then we identify the phases into liquid,
gas, and solid.

```pycon
>>> from thermo import ChemicalConstantsPackage, PropertyCorrelationsPackage, HeatCapacityGas, SRKMIX, CEOSGas, CEOSLiquid
>>> constants = ChemicalConstantsPackage(Tcs=[563.0, 647.14, 514.0], Vcs=[0.000274, 5.6e-05, 0.000168], Pcs=[4414000.0, 22048320.0, 6137000.0], omegas=[0.59, 0.344, 0.635], MWs=[74.1216, 18.01528, 46.06844], CASs=['71-36-3', '7732-18-5', '64-17-5'])
>>> properties = PropertyCorrelationsPackage(constants=constants, skip_missing=True,
...                                     HeatCapacityGases=[HeatCapacityGas(load_data=False, poly_fit=(50.0, 1000.0, [-3.787200194613107e-20, 1.7692887427654656e-16, -3.445247207129205e-13, 3.612771874320634e-10, -2.1953250181084466e-07, 7.707135849197655e-05, -0.014658388538054169, 1.5642629364740657, -7.614560475001724])),
...                                     HeatCapacityGas(load_data=False, poly_fit=(50.0, 1000.0, [5.543665000518528e-22, -2.403756749600872e-18, 4.2166477594350336e-15, -3.7965208514613565e-12, 1.823547122838406e-09, -4.3747690853614695e-07, 5.437938301211039e-05, -0.003220061088723078, 33.32731489750759])),
...                                     HeatCapacityGas(load_data=False, poly_fit=(50.0, 1000.0, [-1.162767978165682e-20, 5.4975285700787494e-17, -1.0861242757337942e-13, 1.1582703354362728e-10, -7.160627710867427e-08, 2.5392014654765875e-05, -0.004732593693568646, 0.5072291035198603, 20.037826650765965])),], )
>>> eos_kwargs = dict(Tcs=constants.Tcs, Pcs=constants.Pcs, omegas=constants.omegas)
>>> gas = CEOSGas(SRKMIX, eos_kwargs, HeatCapacityGases=properties.HeatCapacityGases)
>>> liq = CEOSLiquid(SRKMIX, eos_kwargs, HeatCapacityGases=properties.HeatCapacityGases)
>>> T, P = 361, 1e5
>>> gas = gas.to(T=T, P=P, zs=[0.2384009970908655, 0.5786839935180925, 0.1829150093910419])
>>> liq0 = liq.to(T=T, P=P, zs=[7.619975052238032e-05, 0.9989622883894993, 0.0009615118599781474])
>>> liq1 = liq.to(T=T, P=P, zs=[0.6793120076703771, 0.19699746328631124, 0.12369052904331178])
>>> res = identity_phase_states(phases=[liq0, liq1, gas], constants=constants, correlations=properties, VL_method='PIP')
>>> res[0] is gas, res[1][0] is liq0, res[1][1] is liq1, res[2]
(True, True, True, [])
```

modules.name: thermo.vectorized
documentation:
Chemical Engineering Design Library (ChEDL). Utilities for process modeling.
Copyright (C) 2021, Caleb Bell \<<mailto:Caleb.Andrew.Bell@gmail.com>>

Permission is hereby granted, free of charge, to any person obtaining a copy
of this software and associated documentation files (the "Software"), to deal
in the Software without restriction, including without limitation the rights
to use, copy, modify, merge, publish, distribute, sublicense, and/or sell
copies of the Software, and to permit persons to whom the Software is
furnished to do so, subject to the following conditions:

The above copyright notice and this permission notice shall be included in all
copies or substantial portions of the Software.

THE SOFTWARE IS PROVIDED "AS IS", WITHOUT WARRANTY OF ANY KIND, EXPRESS OR
IMPLIED, INCLUDING BUT NOT LIMITED TO THE WARRANTIES OF MERCHANTABILITY,
FITNESS FOR A PARTICULAR PURPOSE AND NONINFRINGEMENT. IN NO EVENT SHALL THE
AUTHORS OR COPYRIGHT HOLDERS BE LIABLE FOR ANY CLAIM, DAMAGES OR OTHER
LIABILITY, WHETHER IN AN ACTION OF CONTRACT, TORT OR OTHERWISE, ARISING FROM,
OUT OF OR IN CONNECTION WITH THE SOFTWARE OR THE USE OR OTHER DEALINGS IN THE
SOFTWARE.

Basic module which wraps all thermo functions with numpy's np.vectorize
function.

All other object - dicts, classes, etc - are not wrapped. Supports star
imports; so the same objects exported when importing from the main library
will be imported from here.

```pycon
>>> from thermo.vectorized import *
>>> Antoine(np.linspace(100, 200, 5), A=8.95894, B=510.595, C=-15.95)
array([7.65674361e+02, 1.89116754e+04, 1.41237759e+05, 5.60609191e+05,
       1.53010431e+06])
```

Inputs do not need to be numpy arrays; they can be any iterable:

```pycon
>>> import thermo.vectorized
>>> thermo.vectorized.Tc(['108-88-3', '7732-18-5'])
array([591.75, 647.14])
```

:::{warning}
This module does not replace the functions in the `thermo` module; it
copies all the functions into the `thermo.vectorized` module and makes
them vectorized there.

For example by importing `thermo.vectorized`,
`thermo.Antoine` won't become vectorized,
but `thermo.vectorized.Antoine` will become available and is vectorized.
:::

:::{warning}
`np.vectorize` does not use NumPy to accelerate any computations;
it is a convenience wrapper. If you are working on a problem large enough for
speed to be an issue and Numba is compatible with your version of Python,
an interface to that library is available at {obj}`thermo.numba` which does
accelerate NumPy array computations and is normally faster than using numpy
directly.
:::

OUT OF OR IN CONNECTION WITH THE SOFTWARE OR THE USE OR OTHER DEALINGS IN THE

This module contains an object designed to store the result of a flash
calculation and provide convinient access to all properties of the calculated
phases and bulks.

For reporting bugs, adding feature requests, or submitting pull requests,
please use the [GitHub issue tracker](https://github.com/CalebBell/thermo/).

```{contents}
:local: true
```


methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.__hash__
documentation:
Basic method to calculate a hash of the state.

Note that the hashes should only be compared on the same system running
in the same process!

Returns
-------
hash : int
    Hash of the state including the phases making it up [-]

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.__init__
documentation: __str__ = __repr__

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.phase
documentation:
Method to calculate and return a string representing the phase of
the mixture. The return string uses 'V' to represent the gas phase,
'L' to represent a liquid phase, and 'S' to represent a solid phase
(always in that order).

A state with three liquids, two solids, and a gas would return
'VLLLSS'.

Returns
-------
phase : str
    Phase string, [-]

Notes
-----

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.VF
documentation:
Method to return the vapor fraction of the equilibrium state.
If no vapor/gas is present, 0 is always returned.

Returns
-------
VF : float
    Vapor molar fraction, [-]

Notes
-----

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.LF
documentation:
Method to return the liquid fraction of the equilibrium state.
If no liquid is present, 0 is always returned.

Returns
-------
LF : float
    Liquid molar fraction, [-]

Notes
-----

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.quality
documentation:
Method to return the mass vapor fraction of the equilibrium state.
If no vapor/gas is present, 0 is always returned. This is normally
called the quality.

Returns
-------
quality : float
    Vapor mass fraction, [-]

Notes
-----

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.betas_liquids
documentation:
Method to calculate and return the fraction of the liquid phase
that each liquid phase is, by molar phase fraction.
If the system is VLLL with phase fractions of 0.125 vapor, and
[.25, .125, .5] for the three liquids phases respectively, the return
value would be [0.28571428, 0.142857142, 0.57142857].

Returns
-------
betas_liquids : list[float]
    Molar phase fractions of the overall liquid phase, [-]

Notes
-----

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.betas_mass_liquids
documentation:
Method to calculate and return the fraction of the liquid phase
that each liquid phase is, by mass phase fraction.
If the system is VLLL with mass phase fractions of 0.125 vapor, and
[.25, .125, .5] for the three liquids phases respectively, the return
value would be [0.28571428, 0.142857142, 0.57142857].

Returns
-------
betas_mass_liquids : list[float]
    Mass phase fractions of the overall liquid phase, [-]

Notes
-----

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.betas_volume_liquids
documentation:
Method to calculate and return the fraction of the liquid phase
that each liquid phase is, by volume phase fraction.
If the system is VLLL with volume phase fractions of 0.125 vapor, and
[.25, .125, .5] for the three liquids phases respectively, the return
value would be [0.28571428, 0.142857142, 0.57142857].

Returns
-------
betas_volume_liquids : list[float]
    Volume phase fractions of the overall liquid phase, [-]

Notes
-----

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.V_liquids_ref
documentation:
Method to calculate and return the liquid reference molar volumes

V_liquids_ref

# Notes

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.V_liquid_ref
documentation:
Method to calculate and return the liquid reference molar volume
according to the temperature variable `T_liquid_volume_ref` of
{obj}`thermo.bulk.BulkSettings` and the composition of the phase.

$$
V = \sum_i z_i V_i
$$

# Returns

V_liquid_ref

: Liquid molar volume at the reference condition, \[m^3/mol\]

# Notes

: Liquid mass density at the reference condition, \[kg/m^3\]

# Notes

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.Vfls
documentation:
Method to calculate and return the ideal-liquid volume fractions of
the components of the phase, using the standard liquid densities at
the temperature variable `T_liquid_volume_ref` of
{obj}`thermo.bulk.BulkSettings` and the composition of the phase.

# Returns

Vfls

: Ideal-liquid volume fractions of the components of the phase, \[-\]

# Notes

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.Vfgs
documentation:
Method to calculate and return the ideal-gas volume fractions of
the components of the phase. This is the same as the mole fractions.

Returns
-------
Vfgs : list[float]
    Ideal-gas volume fractions of the components of the phase, [-]

Notes
-----

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.MW
documentation:
Method to calculate and return the molecular weight of the phase.

$$
\text{MW} = \sum_i z_i \text{MW}_{i}
$$

# Returns

MW

: Molecular weight of the phase, \[g/mol\]

# Notes

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.pseudo_Tc
documentation:
Method to calculate and return the pseudocritical temperature
calculated using Kay's rule (linear mole fractions):

$$
T_{c, pseudo} = \sum_i z_i T_{c,i}
$$

# Returns

pseudo_Tc

: Pseudocritical temperature of the phase, \[K\]

# Notes

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.pseudo_Pc
documentation:
Method to calculate and return the pseudocritical pressure
calculated using Kay's rule (linear mole fractions):

$$
P_{c, pseudo} = \sum_i z_i P_{c,i}
$$

# Returns

pseudo_Pc

: Pseudocritical pressure of the phase, \[Pa\]

# Notes

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.pseudo_Vc
documentation:
Method to calculate and return the pseudocritical volume
calculated using Kay's rule (linear mole fractions):

$$
V_{c, pseudo} = \sum_i z_i V_{c,i}
$$

# Returns

pseudo_Vc

: Pseudocritical volume of the phase, \[m^3/mol\]

# Notes

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.pseudo_Zc
documentation:
Method to calculate and return the pseudocritical compressibility
calculated using Kay's rule (linear mole fractions):

$$
Z_{c, pseudo} = \sum_i z_i Z_{c,i}
$$

# Returns

pseudo_Zc

: Pseudocritical compressibility of the phase, \[-\]

# Notes

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.pseudo_omega
documentation:
Method to calculate and return the pseudocritical acentric factor
calculated using Kay's rule (linear mole fractions):

$$
\omega_{pseudo} = \sum_i z_i \omega_{i}
$$

# Returns

pseudo_omega

: Pseudo acentric factor of the phase, \[-\]

# Notes

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.Cp_ideal_gas
documentation:
Method to calculate and return the ideal-gas heat capacity of the
phase.

$$
C_p^{ig} = \sum_i z_i {C_{p,i}^{ig}}
$$

# Returns

Cp

: Ideal gas heat capacity, \[J/(mol\*K)\]

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.H_dep
documentation:
Method to calculate and return the difference between the actual
`H` and the ideal-gas enthalpy of the phase.

$$
H^{dep} = H - H^{ig}
$$

# Returns

H_dep

: Departure enthalpy, \[J/(mol)\]

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.S_dep
documentation:
Method to calculate and return the difference between the actual
`S` and the ideal-gas entropy of the phase.

$$
S^{dep} = S - S^{ig}
$$

# Returns

S_dep

: Departure entropy, \[J/(mol\*K)\]

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.Cp_dep
documentation:
Method to calculate and return the difference between the actual
`Cp` and the ideal-gas heat
capacity $C_p^{ig}$ of the phase.

$$
C_p^{dep} = C_p - C_p^{ig}
$$

# Returns

Cp_dep

: Departure ideal gas heat capacity, \[J/(mol\*K)\]

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.Cv_dep
documentation:
Method to calculate and return the difference between the actual
`Cv` and the ideal-gas constant volume heat
capacity $C_v^{ig}$ of the phase.

$$
C_v^{dep} = C_v - C_v^{ig}
$$

# Returns

Cv_dep

: Departure ideal gas constant volume heat capacity, \[J/(mol\*K)\]

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.H_dep_flow
documentation:
Method to return the flow rate of the difference between the
ideal-gas energy of this phase and the actual energy of the phase
This method is only
available when the phase is linked to an EquilibriumStream.

Returns
-------
H_dep_flow : float
    Flow rate of departure energy, [J/s]

Notes
-----

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.S_dep_flow
documentation:
Method to return the flow rate of the difference between the
ideal-gas entropy of this phase and the actual entropy of the phase
This method is only
available when the phase is linked to an EquilibriumStream.

Returns
-------
S_dep_flow : float
    Flow rate of departure entropy, [J/(K*s)]

Notes
-----

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.U_dep_flow
documentation:
Method to return the flow rate of the difference between the
ideal-gas internal energy of this phase and the actual internal energy of the phase
This method is only
available when the phase is linked to an EquilibriumStream.

Returns
-------
U_dep_flow : float
    Flow rate of departure internal energy, [J/s]

Notes
-----

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.A_dep_flow
documentation:
Method to return the flow rate of the difference between the
ideal-gas Helmholtz energy of this phase and the Helmholtz energy of the phase
This method is only
available when the phase is linked to an EquilibriumStream.

Returns
-------
A_dep_flow : float
    Flow rate of departure Helmholtz energy, [J/s]

Notes
-----

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.G_dep_flow
documentation:
Method to return the flow rate of the difference between the
ideal-gas Gibbs free energy of this phase and the actual Gibbs free energy of the phase
This method is only
available when the phase is linked to an EquilibriumStream.

Returns
-------
G_dep_flow : float
    Flow rate of departure Gibbs energy, [J/s]

Notes
-----

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.H_ideal_gas
documentation:
Method to calculate and return the ideal-gas enthalpy of the phase.

$$
H^{ig} = \sum_i z_i {H_{i}^{ig}}
$$

# Returns

H

: Ideal gas enthalpy, \[J/(mol)\]

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.S_ideal_gas
documentation:
Method to calculate and return the ideal-gas entropy of the phase.

$$
S^{ig} = \sum_i z_i S_{i}^{ig} - R\ln\left(\frac{P}{P_{ref}}\right) - R\sum_i z_i \ln(z_i)
$$

# Returns

S

: Ideal gas molar entropy, \[J/(mol\*K)\]

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.Cv_ideal_gas
documentation:
Method to calculate and return the ideal-gas constant volume heat
capacity of the phase.

$$
C_v^{ig} = \sum_i z_i {C_{p,i}^{ig}} - R
$$

# Returns

Cv

: Ideal gas constant volume heat capacity, \[J/(mol\*K)\]

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.Cp_Cv_ratio_ideal_gas
documentation:
Method to calculate and return the ratio of the ideal-gas heat
capacity to its constant-volume heat capacity.

$$
\frac{C_p^{ig}}{C_v^{ig}}
$$

# Returns

Cp_Cv_ratio_ideal_gas

: Cp/Cv for the phase as an ideal gas, \[-\]

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.G_ideal_gas
documentation:
Method to calculate and return the ideal-gas Gibbs free energy of
the phase.

$$
G^{ig} = H^{ig} - T S^{ig}
$$

# Returns

G_ideal_gas

: Ideal gas free energy, \[J/(mol)\]

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.U_ideal_gas
documentation:
Method to calculate and return the ideal-gas internal energy of
the phase.

$$
U^{ig} = H^{ig} - P V^{ig}
$$

# Returns

U_ideal_gas

: Ideal gas internal energy, \[J/(mol)\]

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.A_ideal_gas
documentation:
Method to calculate and return the ideal-gas Helmholtz energy of
the phase.

$$
A^{ig} = U^{ig} - T S^{ig}
$$

# Returns

A_ideal_gas

: Ideal gas Helmholtz free energy, \[J/(mol)\]

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.V_ideal_gas
documentation:
Method to calculate and return the ideal-gas molar volume of the
phase.

$$
V^{ig} = \frac{RT}{P}
$$

# Returns

V

: Ideal gas molar volume, \[m^3/mol\]

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.H_formation_ideal_gas
documentation:
Method to calculate and return the ideal-gas enthalpy of formation
of the phase (as if the phase was an ideal gas).

$$
H_{reactive}^{ig} = \sum_i z_i {H_{f,i}}
$$

# Returns

H_formation_ideal_gas

: Enthalpy of formation of the phase on a reactive basis
  as an ideal gas, \[J/mol\]

# Notes

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.S_formation_ideal_gas
documentation:
Method to calculate and return the ideal-gas entropy of formation
of the phase (as if the phase was an ideal gas).

$$
S_{reactive}^{ig} = \sum_i z_i {S_{f,i}}
$$

# Returns

S_formation_ideal_gas

: Entropy of formation of the phase on a reactive basis
  as an ideal gas, \[J/(mol\*K)\]

# Notes

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.G_formation_ideal_gas
documentation:
Method to calculate and return the ideal-gas Gibbs free energy of
formation of the phase (as if the phase was an ideal gas).

$$
G_{reactive}^{ig} = H_{reactive}^{ig} - T_{ref}^{ig} S_{reactive}^{ig}
$$

# Returns

G_formation_ideal_gas

: Gibbs free energy of formation of the phase on a reactive basis
  as an ideal gas, \[J/(mol)\]

# Notes

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.U_formation_ideal_gas
documentation:
Method to calculate and return the ideal-gas internal energy of
formation of the phase (as if the phase was an ideal gas).

$$
U_{reactive}^{ig} = H_{reactive}^{ig} - P_{ref}^{ig} V^{ig}
$$

# Returns

U_formation_ideal_gas

: Internal energy of formation of the phase on a reactive basis
  as an ideal gas, \[J/(mol)\]

# Notes

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.A_formation_ideal_gas
documentation:
Method to calculate and return the ideal-gas Helmholtz energy of
formation of the phase (as if the phase was an ideal gas).

$$
A_{reactive}^{ig} = U_{reactive}^{ig} - T_{ref}^{ig} S_{reactive}^{ig}
$$

# Returns

A_formation_ideal_gas

: Helmholtz energy of formation of the phase on a reactive basis
  as an ideal gas, \[J/(mol)\]

# Notes

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.nu
documentation:
Method to calculate and return the kinematic viscosity of the
equilibrium state.

$$
\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho}
$$

# Returns

nu

: Kinematic viscosity, \[m^2/s\]

# Notes

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.SG
documentation:
Method to calculate and return the standard liquid specific gravity
of the phase, using constant liquid pure component densities not
calculated by the phase object, at 60 °F.

Returns
-------
SG : float
    Specific gravity of the liquid, [-]

Notes
-----
The reference density of water is from the IAPWS-95 standard -
999.0170824078306 kg/m^3.

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.SG_gas
documentation:
Method to calculate and return the specific gravity of the phase
with respect to a gas reference density.

Returns
-------
SG_gas : float
    Specific gravity of the gas, [-]

Notes
-----
The reference molecular weight of air used is 28.9586 g/mol.

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.V_gas_standard
documentation:
Method to calculate and return the ideal-gas molar volume of the
phase at the standard temperature and pressure,  according to the
temperature variable `T_standard` and pressure variable `P_standard`
of the {obj}`thermo.bulk.BulkSettings`.

$$
V^{ig} = \frac{RT_{std}}{P_{std}}
$$

# Returns

V_gas_standard

: Ideal gas molar volume at standard temperature and pressure,
  \[m^3/mol\]
rho_mass = self.rho_mass(phase)
return SG(rho_mass, rho_ref=1.2)

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.V_gas_normal
documentation:
Method to calculate and return the ideal-gas molar volume of the
phase at the normal temperature and pressure,  according to the
temperature variable `T_normal` and pressure variable `P_normal`
of the {obj}`thermo.bulk.BulkSettings`.

$$
V^{ig} = \frac{RT_{norm}}{P_{norm}}
$$

# Returns

V_gas_normal

: Ideal gas molar volume at normal temperature and pressure,
  \[m^3/mol\]

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.V_gas
documentation:
Method to calculate and return the ideal-gas molar volume of the
phase at the chosen reference temperature and pressure,  according to the
temperature variable `T_gas_ref` and pressure variable `P_gas_ref`
of the {obj}`thermo.bulk.BulkSettings`.

$$
V^{ig} = \frac{RT_{ref}}{P_{ref}}
$$

# Returns

V_gas

: Ideal gas molar volume at the reference temperature and pressure,
  \[m^3/mol\]

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.rho_gas_standard
documentation:
Method to calculate and return the ideal-gas molar density of the
phase at the standard temperature and pressure,  according to the
temperature variable `T_standard` and pressure variable `P_standard`
of the {obj}`thermo.bulk.BulkSettings`.

# Returns

rho_gas_standard

: Ideal gas molar density at standard temperature and pressure,
  \[mol/m^3\]

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.rho_gas_normal
documentation:
Method to calculate and return the ideal-gas molar density of the
phase at the normal temperature and pressure,  according to the
temperature variable `T_normal` and pressure variable `P_normal`
of the {obj}`thermo.bulk.BulkSettings`.

# Returns

rho_gas_normal

: Ideal gas molar density at normal temperature and pressure,
  \[mol/m^3\]

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.rho_gas
documentation:
Method to calculate and return the ideal-gas molar density of the
phase at the chosen reference temperature and pressure,  according to the
temperature variable `T_gas_ref` and pressure variable `P_gas_ref`
of the {obj}`thermo.bulk.BulkSettings`.

# Returns

rho_gas

: Ideal gas molar density at the reference temperature and pressure,
  \[mol/m^3\]

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.rho_mass_gas_standard
documentation:
Method to calculate and return the ideal-gas mass density of the
phase at the standard temperature and pressure,  according to the
temperature variable `T_standard` and pressure variable `P_standard`
of the {obj}`thermo.bulk.BulkSettings`.

# Returns

rho_mass_gas_standard

: Ideal gas molar density at standard temperature and pressure,
  \[kg/m^3\]

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.rho_mass_gas_normal
documentation:
Method to calculate and return the ideal-gas mass density of the
phase at the normal temperature and pressure,  according to the
temperature variable `T_normal` and pressure variable `P_normal`
of the {obj}`thermo.bulk.BulkSettings`.

# Returns

rho_mass_gas_normal

: Ideal gas molar density at normal temperature and pressure,
  \[kg/m^3\]

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.rho_mass_gas
documentation:
Method to calculate and return the ideal-gas mass density of the
phase at the chosen reference temperature and pressure,  according to the
temperature variable `T_gas_ref` and pressure variable `P_gas_ref`
of the {obj}`thermo.bulk.BulkSettings`.

# Returns

rho_mass_gas

: Ideal gas molar density at the reference temperature and pressure,
  \[kg/m^3\]

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.H_C_ratio
documentation:
Method to calculate and return the atomic ratio of hydrogen atoms
to carbon atoms, based on the current composition of the phase.

Returns
-------
H_C_ratio : float
    H/C ratio on a molar basis, [-]

Notes
-----
None is returned if no species are present that have carbon atoms.

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.H_C_ratio_mass
documentation:
Method to calculate and return the mass ratio of hydrogen atoms
to carbon atoms, based on the current composition of the phase.

Returns
-------
H_C_ratio_mass : float
    H/C ratio on a mass basis, [-]

Notes
-----
None is returned if no species are present that have carbon atoms.

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.lightest_liquid
documentation:
The liquid-like phase with the lowest mass density, [-]

Returns
-------
lightest_liquid : Phase or None
    Phase with the lowest mass density or None if there are no liquid
    like phases, [-]

Notes
-----

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.heaviest_liquid
documentation:
The liquid-like phase with the highest mass density, [-]

Returns
-------
heaviest_liquid : Phase or None
    Phase with the highest mass density or None if there are no liquid
    like phases, [-]

Notes
-----

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.water_phase_index
documentation:
The liquid-like phase with the highest mole fraction of water, \[-\]

# Returns

water_phase_index

: Index into the attribute {obj}`EquilibriumState.liquids` which
  refers to the liquid-like phase with the highest water mole
  fraction, \[-\]

# Notes

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.water_phase
documentation:
The liquid-like phase with the highest water mole fraction, [-]

Returns
-------
water_phase : Phase or None
    Phase with the highest water mole fraction or None if there are no
    liquid like phases with water, [-]

Notes
-----

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.water_index
documentation:
The index of the component water in the components. None if water
is not present. Water is recognized by its CAS number.

Returns
-------
water_index : int
    The index of the component water, [-]

Notes
-----

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.humidity_ratio
documentation:
Method to calculate and return the humidity ratio of the phase;
normally defined as the kg water/kg dry air, the definition here is
kg water/(kg rest of the phase) \[-\]

$$
\text{humidity ratio} = \text{HR} = \frac{w_{H2O}}{1 - w_{H2O}}
$$

# Returns

humidity_ratio

: Humidity ratio, \[-\]

# Notes

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.zs_no_water
documentation:
Method to calculate and return the mole fractions of all species
in the phase, normalized to a water-free basis (the mole fraction of
water returned is zero).

Returns
-------
zs_no_water : list[float]
    Mole fractions on a water free basis, [-]

Notes
-----

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.ws_no_water
documentation:
Method to calculate and return the mass fractions of all species
in the phase, normalized to a water-free basis (the mass fraction of
water returned is zero).

Returns
-------
ws_no_water : list[float]
    Mass fractions on a water free basis, [-]

Notes
-----

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.Ks
documentation:
Method to calculate and return the K-values of each phase.
These are NOT just liquid-vapor K values; these are thermodynamic K
values. The reference phase can be specified with `ref_phase`, and then
the K-values will be with respect to that phase.

$$
K_i = \frac{z_{i, \text{phase}}}{z_{i, \text{ref phase}}}
$$

If no reference phase is provided, the following criteria is used to
select one:

> - If the flash algorithm provided a reference phase, use that
> - Otherwise use the liquid0 phase if one is present
> - Otherwise use the solid0 phase if one is present
> - Otherwise use the gas phase if one is present

# Returns

Ks

: Equilibrium K values, \[-\]

# Notes

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.Hc
documentation:
Method to calculate and return the molar ideal-gas higher heat of
combustion of the object, [J/mol]

Returns
-------
Hc : float
    Molar higher heat of combustion, [J/(mol)]

Notes
-----

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.Hc_mass
documentation:
Method to calculate and return the mass ideal-gas higher heat of
combustion of the object, [J/mol]

Returns
-------
Hc_mass : float
    Mass higher heat of combustion, [J/(kg)]

Notes
-----

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.Hc_normal
documentation:
Method to calculate and return the volumetric ideal-gas higher heat
of combustion of the object using the normal gas volume, [J/m^3]

Returns
-------
Hc_normal : float
    Volumetric (normal) higher heat of combustion, [J/(m^3)]

Notes
-----

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.Hc_standard
documentation:
Method to calculate and return the volumetric ideal-gas higher heat
of combustion of the object using the standard gas volume, [J/m^3]

Returns
-------
Hc_normal : float
    Volumetric (standard) higher heat of combustion, [J/(m^3)]

Notes
-----

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.Hc_lower
documentation:
Method to calculate and return the molar ideal-gas lower heat of
combustion of the object, [J/mol]

Returns
-------
Hc_lower : float
    Molar lower heat of combustion, [J/(mol)]

Notes
-----

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.Hc_lower_mass
documentation:
Method to calculate and return the mass ideal-gas lower heat of

Returns
-------
Hc_lower_mass : float
    Mass lower heat of combustion, [J/(kg)]

Notes
-----

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.Hc_lower_normal
documentation:
Method to calculate and return the volumetric ideal-gas lower heat
of combustion of the object using the normal gas volume, [J/m^3]

Returns
-------
Hc_lower_normal : float
    Volumetric (normal) lower heat of combustion, [J/(m^3)]

Notes
-----

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.Hc_lower_standard
documentation:
Method to calculate and return the volumetric ideal-gas lower heat
of combustion of the object using the standard gas volume, [J/m^3]

Returns
-------
Hc_lower_standard : float
    Volumetric (standard) lower heat of combustion, [J/(m^3)]

-----

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.Wobbe_index
documentation:
Method to calculate and return the molar Wobbe index of the object,
\[J/mol\].

$$
I_W = \frac{H_{comb}^{higher}}{\sqrt{\text{SG}}}
$$

# Returns

Wobbe_index

: Molar Wobbe index, \[J/(mol)\]

# Notes

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.Wobbe_index_mass
documentation:
Method to calculate and return the mass Wobbe index of the object,
\[J/kg\].

$$
I_W = \frac{H_{comb}^{higher}}{\sqrt{\text{SG}}}
$$

# Returns

Wobbe_index_mass

: Mass Wobbe index, \[J/(kg)\]

# Notes

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.Wobbe_index_lower
documentation:
Method to calculate and return the molar lower Wobbe index of the

: object, \[J/mol\].

$$
I_W = \frac{H_{comb}^{lower}}{\sqrt{\text{SG}}}
$$

# Returns

Wobbe_index_lower

: Molar lower Wobbe index, \[J/(mol)\]

# Notes

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.Wobbe_index_lower_mass
documentation:
Method to calculate and return the lower mass Wobbe index of the
object, \[J/kg\].

$$
I_W = \frac{H_{comb}^{lower}}{\sqrt{\text{SG}}}
$$

# Returns

Wobbe_index_lower_mass

: Mass lower Wobbe index, \[J/(kg)\]

# Notes

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.Wobbe_index_standard
documentation:
Method to calculate and return the volumetric standard Wobbe index
of the object, \[J/m^3\]. The standard gas volume is used in this
calculation.

$$
I_W = \frac{H_{comb}^{higher}}{\sqrt{\text{SG}}}
$$

# Returns

Wobbe_index_standard

: Volumetric standard Wobbe index, \[J/(m^3)\]

# Notes

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.Wobbe_index_normal
documentation:
Method to calculate and return the volumetric normal Wobbe index
of the object, \[J/m^3\]. The normal gas volume is used in this
calculation.

$$
I_W = \frac{H_{comb}^{higher}}{\sqrt{\text{SG}}}
$$

# Returns

Wobbe_index

: Volumetric normal Wobbe index, \[J/(m^3)\]

# Notes

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.Wobbe_index_lower_standard
documentation:
Method to calculate and return the volumetric standard lower Wobbe
index of the object, \[J/m^3\]. The standard gas volume is used in this
calculation.

$$
I_W = \frac{H_{comb}^{lower}}{\sqrt{\text{SG}}}
$$

# Returns

Wobbe_index_lower_standard

: Volumetric standard lower Wobbe index, \[J/(m^3)\]

# Notes

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.Wobbe_index_lower_normal
documentation:
Method to calculate and return the volumetric normal lower Wobbe
index of the object, \[J/m^3\]. The normal gas volume is used in this
calculation.

$$
I_W = \frac{H_{comb}^{lower}}{\sqrt{\text{SG}}}
$$

# Returns

Wobbe_index_lower_normal

: Volumetric normal lower Wobbe index, \[J/(m^3)\]

# Notes

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.Psats
documentation:
Method to calculate and return the pure-component vapor pressures
of each species from the {obj}`thermo.vapor_pressure.VaporPressure`
objects.

# Returns

Psats

: Vapor pressures, \[Pa\]

# Notes

:::{warning}
This is not necessarily consistent with the saturation pressure
calculated by a flash algorithm.
:::
T dependent property correlations only - separate from phases

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.Psubs
documentation:
Method to calculate and return the pure-component sublimation
of each species from the {obj}`thermo.vapor_pressure.SublimationPressure`
objects.

# Returns

Psubs

: Sublimation pressures, \[Pa\]

# Notes

:::{warning}
This is not necessarily consistent with the saturation pressure
calculated by a flash algorithm.
:::

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.Hsubs
documentation:
Method to calculate and return the pure-component enthalpy of sublimation
of each species from the {obj}`thermo.phase_change.EnthalpySublimation`
objects.

# Returns

Hsubs

: Sublimation enthalpies, \[J/mol\]

# Notes

:::{warning}
This is not necessarily consistent with the saturation
enthalpy change calculated by a flash algorithm.
:::

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.Hvaps
documentation:
Method to calculate and return the pure-component enthalpy of vaporization
of each species from the {obj}`thermo.phase_change.EnthalpyVaporization`
objects.

# Returns

Hvaps

: Enthalpies of vaporization, \[J/mol\]

# Notes

:::{warning}
This is not necessarily consistent with the saturation
enthalpy change calculated by a flash algorithm.
:::

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.sigmas
documentation:
Method to calculate and return the pure-component surface tensions
of each species from the {obj}`thermo.interface.SurfaceTension`
objects.

# Returns

sigmas

: Surface tensions, \[N/m\]

# Notes

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.Cpgs
documentation:
Method to calculate and return the pure-component ideal gas heat capacities
of each species from the {obj}`thermo.heat_capacity.HeatCapacityGas`
objects.

# Returns

Cpgs

: Ideal gas pure component heat capacities, \[J/(mol\*K)\]

# Notes

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.Cpls
documentation:
Method to calculate and return the pure-component liquid
temperature-dependent heat capacities
of each species from the {obj}`thermo.heat_capacity.HeatCapacityLiquid`
objects.

Note that some correlation methods for liquid heat capacity are at
low pressure, and others are along the saturation line. There is a
large difference in values.

# Returns

Cpls

: Pure component liquid heat capacities, \[J/(mol\*K)\]

# Notes

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.Cpss
documentation:
Method to calculate and return the pure-component solid heat capacities
of each species from the {obj}`thermo.heat_capacity.HeatCapacitySolid`
objects.

# Returns

Cpss

: Pure component solid heat capacities, \[J/(mol\*K)\]

# Notes

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.kls
documentation:
Method to calculate and return the pure-component liquid
temperature-dependent thermal conductivity
of each species from the {obj}`thermo.thermal_conductivity.ThermalConductivityLiquid`
objects.

These values are normally at low pressure, not along the saturation line.

# Returns

kls

: Pure component temperature dependent liquid thermal conductivities,
  \[W/(m\*K)\]

# Notes

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.kss
documentation:
Method to calculate and return the pure-component solid
temperature-dependent thermal conductivity
of each species from the {obj}`thermo.thermal_conductivity.ThermalConductivitySolid`
objects.

# Returns

kss

: Pure component temperature dependent solid thermal conductivities,
  \[W/(m\*K)\]

# Notes

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.kgs
documentation:
Method to calculate and return the pure-component gas
temperature-dependent thermal conductivity
of each species from the {obj}`thermo.thermal_conductivity.ThermalConductivityGas`
objects.

These values are normally at low pressure, not along the saturation line.

# Returns

kgs

: Pure component temperature dependent gas thermal conductivities,
  \[W/(m\*K)\]

# Notes

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.value
documentation:
Method to retrieve a property from a string. This more or less
wraps `getattr`, but also allows for the property to be returned for a
specific phase if `phase` is provided.

`name` could be a python property like 'Tms' or a callable method
like 'H'; and if the property is on a per-phase basis like 'betas_mass',
a phase object can be provided as the second argument and only the
value for that phase will be returned.

# Parameters

name

: String representing the property, \[-\]

phase

: Phase to retrieve the property for only (if specified), \[-\]

# Returns

value

: Value specified, \[various\]

# Notes

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.IDs
documentation: Alias of CASs.

methods.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.API
documentation:
Method to calculate and return the API of the phase.

$$
\text{API gravity} = \frac{141.5}{\text{SG}} - 131.5
$$

# Returns

API

: API of the fluid \[-\]

class_variables.name: thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState.vectorized
documentation: not supported yet

Class to represent a thermodynamic equilibrium state with one or more
phases in it. This object is designed to be the output of the
{obj}`thermo.flash.Flash` interface and to provide easy acess to all
properties of the mixture.

Properties like {obj}`Cp <EquilibriumState.Cp>` are calculated using the
mixing rules configured by the
{obj}`BulkSettings <thermo.bulk.BulkSettings>` object. For states with a
single phase, this will always reduce to the properties of that phase.

This interface allows calculation of thermodynamic properties,
and transport properties. Both molar and mass outputs are provided, as
separate calls (ex. {obj}`Cp <EquilibriumState.Cp>` and
{obj}`Cp_mass <EquilibriumState.Cp_mass>`).

# Parameters

T

: Temperature of state, \[K\]

P

: Pressure of state, \[Pa\]

zs

: Overall mole fractions of all species in the state, \[-\]

gas

: The calcualted gas phase object, if one was found, \[-\]

liquids

: A list of liquid phase objects, if any were found, \[-\]

solids

: A list of solid phase objects, if any were found, \[-\]

betas

: Molar phase fractions of every phase, ordered \[`gas beta`,
  `liquid beta0`, `liquid beta1`, ..., `solid beta0`, `solid beta1`, ...\]

flash_specs

: A dictionary containing the specifications for the flash calculations,
  \[-\]

flash_convergence

: A dictionary containing the convergence results for the flash
  calculations; this is to help support development of the library only
  and the contents of this dictionary is subject to change, \[-\]

constants

: Package of chemical constants; all cases these properties are
  accessible as attributes of this object, \[-\]
  {obj}`EquilibriumState <thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState>` object, \[-\]

correlations

: Package of chemical T-dependent properties; these properties are
  accessible as attributes of this object object, \[-\]

flasher

: This reference can be provided to this object to allow the object to
  return properties which are themselves calculated from results of flash
  calculations, \[-\]

settings

: Object containing settings for calculating bulk and transport
  properties, \[-\]

# Examples

The following sample shows a flash for the CO2-n-hexane system with all
constants provided, using no data from thermo.

```pycon
>>> from thermo import *
>>> constants = ChemicalConstantsPackage(names=['carbon dioxide', 'hexane'], CASs=['124-38-9', '110-54-3'], MWs=[44.0095, 86.17536], omegas=[0.2252, 0.2975], Pcs=[7376460.0, 3025000.0], Tbs=[194.67, 341.87], Tcs=[304.2, 507.6], Tms=[216.65, 178.075])
>>> correlations = PropertyCorrelationsPackage(constants=constants, skip_missing=True,
...                                            HeatCapacityGases=[HeatCapacityGas(poly_fit=(50.0, 1000.0, [-3.1115474168865828e-21, 1.39156078498805e-17, -2.5430881416264243e-14, 2.4175307893014295e-11, -1.2437314771044867e-08, 3.1251954264658904e-06, -0.00021220221928610925, 0.000884685506352987, 29.266811602924644])),
...                                                               HeatCapacityGas(poly_fit=(200.0, 1000.0, [1.3740654453881647e-21, -8.344496203280677e-18, 2.2354782954548568e-14, -3.4659555330048226e-11, 3.410703030634579e-08, -2.1693611029230923e-05, 0.008373280796376588, -1.356180511425385, 175.67091124888998]))])
>>> eos_kwargs = {'Pcs': constants.Pcs, 'Tcs': constants.Tcs, 'omegas': constants.omegas}
>>> gas = CEOSGas(PRMIX, eos_kwargs, HeatCapacityGases=correlations.HeatCapacityGases)
>>> liq = CEOSLiquid(PRMIX, eos_kwargs, HeatCapacityGases=correlations.HeatCapacityGases)
>>> flasher = FlashVL(constants, correlations, liquid=liq, gas=gas)
>>> state = flasher.flash(P=1e5, T=196.0, zs=[0.5, 0.5])
>>> type(state) is EquilibriumState
True
>>> state.phase_count
2
>>> state.bulk.Cp()
108.3164692
>>> state.flash_specs
{'zs': [0.5, 0.5], 'T': 196.0, 'P': 100000.0}
>>> state.Tms
[216.65, 178.075]
>>> state.liquid0.H()
-34376.4853
>>> state.gas.H()
-3608.0551
```

# Attributes

gas_count

: Number of gas phases present (0 or 1), \[-\]

liquid_count

: Number of liquid phases present, \[-\]

solid_count

: Number of solid phases present, \[-\]

phase_count

: Number of phases present, \[-\]

gas_beta

: Molar phase fraction of the gas phase; 0 if no gas phase is present,
  \[-\]

liquids_betas

: Liquid molar phase fractions, \[-\]

solids_betas

: Solid molar phase fractions, \[-\]

liquid_zs

: Overall mole fractions of each component in the overall liquid phase,
  \[-\]

liquid_bulk

: Liquid phase bulk, \[-\]

solid_zs

: Overall mole fractions of each component in the overall solid phase,
  \[-\]

solid_bulk

: Solid phase bulk, \[-\]

bulk

: Overall phase bulk, \[-\]

classes.name: thermo.phases.gibbs_excess.GibbsExcessLiquid

methods.name: thermo.phases.gibbs_excess.GibbsExcessLiquid.__repr__
documentation:
Method to create a string representation of the phase object, with
the goal of making it easy to obtain standalone code which reproduces
the current state of the phase. This is extremely helpful in creating
new test cases.

Returns
-------
recreation : str
    String which is valid Python and recreates the current state of
    the object if ran, [-]

methods.name: thermo.phases.gibbs_excess.GibbsExcessLiquid.__init__
documentation:
It is quite possible to introduce a PVT relation ship for liquid
density and remain thermodynamically consistent. However, must be
applied on a per-component basis! This class cannot have an
equation-of-state or VolumeLiquidMixture for a liquid MIXTURE!

(it might still be nice to generalize the handling; maybe even allow)
pure EOSs to be used too, and as a form/template for which functions to
use).

In conclusion, you have
1) The standard H/S model
2) The H/S model with all pressure correction happening at P
3) The inconsistent model which has no pressure dependence whatsover in H/S
   This model is required due to its popularity, not its consistency (but still volume dependency)

All mixture volumetric properties have to be averages of the pure
components properties and derivatives. A Multiphase will be needed to
allow flashes with different properties from different phases.

methods.name: thermo.phases.gibbs_excess.GibbsExcessLiquid.lnHenry_matrix
documentation:
Method to calculate and return the matrix of log Henry's law constants
as required by the traditional mixing rule, \[-\].

$$
\ln \text{H}_{i,j} = A_{ij}+\frac{B_{ij}}{T}+C_{ij}\ln T + D_{ij}T + \frac{E_{ij}}{T^2} + F_{ij}{T^2}
$$

# Returns

lnHenry_matrix

: Henry's law interaction parameters, \[log(Pa)\]

# Notes

Solvent/solvent and gas/gas values are all 0.

documentation:
Method to calculate and return the first temperature derivative
of the matrix of log Henry's law constants
as required by the traditional mixing rule, [-].

Returns
-------
dlnHenry_matrix_dT : list[list[float]]

    parameters, [log(Pa)/K]

Notes
-----

methods.name: thermo.phases.gibbs_excess.GibbsExcessLiquid.Poyntings
documentation:
Method to calculate and return the Poynting pressure correction
factors of the phase, \[-\].

$$
\text{Poynting}_i = \exp\left(\frac{V_{m,i}(P-P_{sat})}{RT}\right)
$$

# Returns

Poyntings

: Poynting pressure correction factors, \[-\]

# Notes

The above formula is correct for pressure-independent molar volumes.
When the volume does depend on pressure, the full expression is:

$$
\text{Poynting} = \exp\left[\frac{\int_{P_i^{sat}}^P V_i^l dP}{RT}\right]
$$

When a specified model e.g. the Tait equation is used, an analytical
integral of this term is normally available.

methods.name: thermo.phases.gibbs_excess.GibbsExcessLiquid.dPoyntings_dP
documentation:
from sympy import *
R, T, P, zi = symbols('R, T, P, zi')
Vml = symbols('Vml', cls=Function)
cse(diff(exp(Vml(T)*(P - Psati(T))/(R*T)), P), optimizations='basic')

methods.name: thermo.phases.gibbs_excess.GibbsExcessLiquid.d2Poyntings_dPdT
documentation:
from sympy import *
R, T, P = symbols('R, T, P')
Vml, Psat = symbols('Vml, Psat', cls=Function)
RT_inv = 1/(R*T)
Poy = exp(Vml(T)*(P-Psat(T))*RT_inv)
Poyf = symbols('Poyf')
cse(diff(Poy, T, P).subs(Poy, Poyf), optimizations='basic')

methods.name: thermo.phases.gibbs_excess.GibbsExcessLiquid.phis_sat
documentation:
Method to calculate and return the saturation fugacity coefficient
correction factors of the phase, \[-\].

These are calculated from the
provided pure-component equations of state. This term should only be
used with a consistent vapor-phase cubic equation of state.

# Returns

phis_sat

: Saturation fugacity coefficient correction factors, \[-\]

# Notes

:::{warning}
This factor cannot be calculated when a compound is supercritical,
as there is no longer any vapor-liquid pure-component equilibrium
(by definition).
:::

methods.name: thermo.phases.gibbs_excess.GibbsExcessLiquid.phis
documentation:
Method to calculate the fugacity coefficients of the
GibbsExcessLiquid phase. Depending on the settings of the phase, can
include the effects of activity coefficients `gammas`, pressure
correction terms `Poyntings`, and pure component saturation fugacities
`phis_sat` as well as the pure component vapor pressures.

$$
\phi_i = \frac{\gamma_i P_{i}^{sat} \phi_i^{sat} \text{Poynting}_i} {P}
$$

# Returns

phis

: Fugacity coefficients of all components in the phase, \[-\]

# Notes

Poyntings, gammas, and pure component saturation phis default to 1.

methods.name: thermo.phases.gibbs_excess.GibbsExcessLiquid.dphis_dT
documentation: def fugacities(self, T, P, zs):
# DO NOT EDIT _ CORRECT
gammas = self.gammas(T, zs)
Psats = self._Psats(T=T)
if self.use_phis_sat:
phis = self.phis(T=T, zs=zs)
else:
phis = [1.0]*self.N
if self.use_Poynting:
Poyntings = self.Poyntings(T=T, P=P, Psats=Psats)
else:
Poyntings = [1.0]*self.N
return [zs[i]*gammas[i]*Psats[i]*Poyntings[i]*phis[i]
for i in range(self.N)]

methods.name: thermo.phases.gibbs_excess.GibbsExcessLiquid.dlnphis_dP
documentation:
Method to calculate the pressure derivative of log fugacity
coefficients of the phase. Depending on the settings of the phase, can
include the effects of activity coefficients `gammas`, pressure
correction terms `Poyntings`, and pure component saturation fugacities
`phis_sat` as well as the pure component vapor pressures.

$$
\frac{\partial \ln \phi_i}{\partial P} = \frac{\frac{\partial \text{Poynting}_i}{\partial P}} {\text{Poynting}_i} - \frac{1}{P}
$$

# Returns

dlnphis_dP

: Pressure derivative of log fugacity coefficients of all components
  in the phase, \[1/Pa\]

# Notes

Poyntings, gammas, and pure component saturation phis default to 1. For
that case, $\frac{\partial \ln \phi_i}{\partial P}=\frac{1}{P}$.

methods.name: thermo.phases.gibbs_excess.GibbsExcessLiquid.gammas
documentation:
Method to calculate and return the activity coefficients of the
phase, \[-\]. This is a direct call to
{obj}`GibbsExcess.gammas <thermo.activity.GibbsExcess.gammas>`.

# Returns

gammas

: Activity coefficients, \[-\]

methods.name: thermo.phases.gibbs_excess.GibbsExcessLiquid.dgammas_dT
documentation:
Method to calculate and return the temperature derivative of
activity coefficients of the phase, \[-\].

This is a direct call to

# Returns

dgammas_dT

: First temperature derivative of the activity coefficients, \[1/K\]

methods.name: thermo.phases.gibbs_excess.GibbsExcessLiquid.H
documentation:
Method to calculate the enthalpy of the
{obj}`GibbsExcessLiquid` phase. Depending on the settings of the phase, this can
include the effects of activity coefficients
{obj}`gammas <GibbsExcessLiquid.gammas>`, pressure correction terms
{obj}`Poyntings <GibbsExcessLiquid.Poyntings>`, and pure component
saturation fugacities {obj}`phis_sat <GibbsExcessLiquid.phis_sat>`
as well as the pure component vapor pressures.

When `caloric_basis` is 'Poynting&PhiSat':

$$
H = H_{\text{excess}} + \sum_i z_i \left[-RT^2\left( \frac{\frac{\partial \phi_{\text{sat},i}}{\partial T}}{\phi_{\text{sat},i}} + \frac{\frac{\partial P_{\text{sat},i}}{\partial T}}{P_{\text{sat},i}} + \frac{\frac{\text{Poynting}}{\partial T}}{\text{Poynting}} \right) + \int_{T,ref}^T C_{p,ig} dT \right]
$$

When `caloric_basis` is 'PhiSat':

$$
H = H_{\text{excess}} + \sum_i z_i \left[-RT^2\left( \frac{\frac{\partial \phi_{\text{sat},i}}{\partial T}}{\phi_{\text{sat},i}} + \frac{\frac{\partial P_{\text{sat},i}}{\partial T}}{P_{\text{sat},i}} \right) + \int_{T,ref}^T C_{p,ig} dT \right]
$$

When `caloric_basis` is 'Poynting':

$$
H = H_{\text{excess}} + \sum_i z_i \left[-RT^2\left( + \frac{\frac{\partial P_{\text{sat},i}}{\partial T}}{P_{\text{sat},i}} + \frac{\frac{\text{Poynting}}{\partial T}}{\text{Poynting}} \right) + \int_{T,ref}^T C_{p,ig} dT \right]
$$

When `caloric_basis` is 'Psat':

$$
H = H_{\text{excess}} + \sum_i z_i \left[-RT^2\left( + \frac{\frac{\partial P_{\text{sat},i}}{\partial T}}{P_{\text{sat},i}}  \right) + \int_{T,ref}^T C_{p,ig} dT \right]
$$

When `caloric_basis` is 'Hvap':

$$
H = H_{\text{excess}} + \sum_i z_i\left[-H_{vap,i} + \int_{T,ref}^T C_{p,ig} dT \right]
$$

# Returns

H

: Enthalpy of the phase, \[J/(mol)\]

# Notes

methods.name: thermo.phases.gibbs_excess.GibbsExcessLiquid.S_old
documentation:
from scipy.integrate import *
from CoolProp.CoolProp import PropsSI

fluid = 'decane'
T = 400
Psat = PropsSI('P', 'T', T, 'Q', 0, fluid)
P2 = Psat*100
dP = P2 - Psat
Vm = 1/PropsSI('DMOLAR', 'T', T, 'Q', 0, fluid)
Vm2 = 1/PropsSI('DMOLAR', 'T', T, 'P', P2, fluid)
dH = PropsSI('HMOLAR', 'T', T, 'P', P2, fluid) - PropsSI('HMOLAR', 'T', T, 'Q', 0, fluid)
dS = PropsSI('SMOLAR', 'T', T, 'P', P2, fluid) - PropsSI('SMOLAR', 'T', T, 'Q', 0, fluid)
def to_int2(P):
    Vm = 1/PropsSI('DMOLAR', 'T', T, 'P', P, fluid)
    alpha = PropsSI('ISOBARIC_EXPANSION_COEFFICIENT', 'T', T, 'P', P, fluid)
    return -alpha*Vm
quad(to_int2, Psat, P2, epsabs=1.49e-14, epsrel=1.49e-14)[0]/dS

methods.name: thermo.phases.gibbs_excess.GibbsExcessLiquid.S
documentation:
Method to calculate the entropy of the
{obj}`GibbsExcessLiquid` phase. Depending on the settings of the phase, this can
include the effects of activity coefficients
{obj}`gammas <GibbsExcessLiquid.gammas>`, pressure correction terms
{obj}`Poyntings <GibbsExcessLiquid.Poyntings>`, and pure component
saturation fugacities {obj}`phis_sat <GibbsExcessLiquid.phis_sat>`
as well as the pure component vapor pressures.

When `caloric_basis` is 'Poynting&PhiSat':

$$
S = S_{\text{excess}} - R\sum_i z_i\ln z_i - R\ln\left(\frac{P}{P_{ref}}\right) - \sum_i z_i\left[R\left( T \frac{\frac{\partial \phi_{\text{sat},i}}{\partial T}}{\phi_{\text{sat},i}} + T\frac{\frac{\partial P_{\text{sat},i}}{\partial T}}{P_{\text{sat},i}} + T\frac{\frac{\text{Poynting}}{\partial T}}{\text{Poynting}} + \ln(P_{\text{sat},i}) + \ln\left(\frac{\text{Poynting}\cdot\phi_{\text{sat},i}}{P}\right) \right) - \int_{T,ref}^T \frac{C_{p,ig,i}}{T} dT \right]
$$

When `caloric_basis` is 'PhiSat':

$$
S = S_{\text{excess}} - R\sum_i z_i\ln z_i - R\ln\left(\frac{P}{P_{ref}}\right) - \sum_i z_i\left[R\left( T \frac{\frac{\partial \phi_{\text{sat},i}}{\partial T}}{\phi_{\text{sat},i}} + T\frac{\frac{\partial P_{\text{sat},i}}{\partial T}}{P_{\text{sat},i}} + \ln(P_{\text{sat},i}) + \ln\left(\frac{\phi_{\text{sat},i}}{P}\right) \right) - \int_{T,ref}^T \frac{C_{p,ig,i}}{T} dT \right]
$$

When `caloric_basis` is 'Poynting':

$$
S = S_{\text{excess}} - R\sum_i z_i\ln z_i - R\ln\left(\frac{P}{P_{ref}}\right) - \sum_i z_i\left[R\left( T\frac{\frac{\partial P_{\text{sat},i}}{\partial T}}{P_{\text{sat},i}} + T\frac{\frac{\text{Poynting}}{\partial T}}{\text{Poynting}} + \ln(P_{\text{sat},i}) + \ln\left(\frac{\text{Poynting}}{P}\right) \right) - \int_{T,ref}^T \frac{C_{p,ig,i}}{T} dT \right]
$$

When `caloric_basis` is 'Psat':

$$
S = S_{\text{excess}} - R\sum_i z_i\ln z_i - R\ln\left(\frac{P}{P_{ref}}\right) - \sum_i z_i\left[R\left( T\frac{\frac{\partial P_{\text{sat},i}}{\partial T}}{P_{\text{sat},i}} + \ln(P_{\text{sat},i}) + \ln\left(\frac{1}{P}\right) \right) - \int_{T,ref}^T \frac{C_{p,ig,i}}{T} dT \right]
$$

When `caloric_basis` is 'Hvap':

$$
S = S_{\text{excess}} - R\sum_i z_i\ln z_i - R\ln\left(\frac{P}{P_{ref}}\right) - \sum_i z_i\left[R\left(\ln P_{\text{sat},i} + \ln\left(\frac{1}{P}\right)\right) + \frac{H_{vap,i}}{T} - \int_{T,ref}^T \frac{C_{p,ig,i}}{T} dT \right]
$$

# Returns

S

: Entropy of the phase, \[J/(mol\*K)\]

# Notes
