This file contains a number of overflow methods for EOSs which for various
reasons are better implemented as functions.
Documentation is not provided for this file and no methods are intended to be
used outside this library.

For reporting bugs, adding feature requests, or submitting pull requests,
please use the [GitHub issue tracker](https://github.com/CalebBell/thermo/).

```{contents}
:local: true
```

# Alpha Function Mixing Rules

These are where the bulk of the time is spent in solving the equation of state.
For that reason, these functional forms often duplicate functionality but have
different performance characteristics.

Implementations which store N^2 matrices for other calculations:

```
.. autofunction:: a_alpha_aijs_composition_independent
```

```
.. autofunction:: a_alpha_and_derivatives_full
```

Compute only the alpha term itself:

```
.. autofunction:: a_alpha_and_derivatives
```

Faster implementations which do not store N^2 matrices:

```
.. autofunction:: a_alpha_quadratic_terms
```

```
.. autofunction:: a_alpha_and_derivatives_quadratic_terms
```

functions.name: thermo.eos_mix_methods.a_alpha_aijs_composition_independent
documentation:
Calculates the matrix $(a\alpha)_{ij}$ as well as the array
$\sqrt{(a\alpha)_{i}}$ and the matrix
$\frac{1}{\sqrt{(a\alpha)_{i}}\sqrt{(a\alpha)_{j}}}$.

$$
(a\alpha)_{ij} = (1-k_{ij})\sqrt{(a\alpha)_{i}(a\alpha)_{j}}
$$

This routine is efficient in both numba and PyPy, but it is generally
better to avoid calculating and storing **any** N^2 matrices. However,
this particular calculation only depends on `T` so in some circumstances
this can be feasible.

# Parameters

a_alphas

: EOS attractive terms, \[J^2/mol^2/Pa\]

one_minus_kijs

: One minus the constant kijs, \[-\]

a_alpha_ijs

: Optional output array, \[J^2/mol^2/Pa\]

a_alpha_roots

: Optional output array, \[J/mol/Pa^0.5\]

a_alpha_ij_roots_inv

: Optional output array, \[mol^2\*Pa/J^2\]

# Returns

a_alpha_ijs

: Matrix of $(1-k_{ij})\sqrt{(a\alpha)_{i}(a\alpha)_{j}}$, \[J^2/mol^2/Pa\]

a_alpha_roots

: Array of $\sqrt{(a\alpha)_{i}}$ values, \[J/mol/Pa^0.5\]

a_alpha_ij_roots_inv

: Matrix of $\frac{1}{\sqrt{(a\alpha)_{i}}\sqrt{(a\alpha)_{j}}}$,
  \[mol^2\*Pa/J^2\]

# Notes

# Examples

```pycon
>>> kijs = [[0,.083],[0.083,0]]
>>> one_minus_kijs = [[1.0 - kij for kij in row] for row in kijs]
>>> a_alphas = [0.2491099357671155, 0.6486495863528039]
>>> a_alpha_ijs, a_alpha_roots, a_alpha_ij_roots_inv = a_alpha_aijs_composition_independent(a_alphas, one_minus_kijs)
>>> a_alpha_ijs
[[0.249109935767, 0.36861239374], [0.36861239374, 0.64864958635]]
>>> a_alpha_roots
[0.49910914213, 0.80538784840]
>>> a_alpha_ij_roots_inv
[[4.0142919105, 2.487707997796], [2.487707997796, 1.54166443799]]
```

functions.name: thermo.eos_mix_methods.a_alpha_and_derivatives_full
documentation:
Calculates the `a_alpha` term, and its first two temperature
derivatives, for an equation of state along with the
matrix quantities calculated in the process.

$$
a \alpha = \sum_i \sum_j z_i z_j {(a\alpha)}_{ij}
$$

$$
\frac{\partial (a\alpha)}{\partial T} = \sum_i \sum_j z_i z_j \frac{\partial (a\alpha)_{ij}}{\partial T}
$$

$$
\frac{\partial^2 (a\alpha)}{\partial T^2} = \sum_i \sum_j z_i z_j \frac{\partial^2 (a\alpha)_{ij}}{\partial T^2}
$$

$$
(a\alpha)_{ij} = (1-k_{ij})\sqrt{(a\alpha)_{i}(a\alpha)_{j}}
$$

$$
\frac{\partial (a\alpha)_{ij}}{\partial T} = \frac{\sqrt{\operatorname{a\alpha_{i}}{\left(T \right)} \operatorname{a\alpha_{j}} {\left(T \right)}} \left(1 - k_{ij}\right) \left(\frac{\operatorname{a\alpha_{i}} {\left(T \right)} \frac{d}{d T} \operatorname{a\alpha_{j}}{\left(T \right)}}{2} + \frac{\operatorname{a\alpha_{j}}{\left(T \right)} \frac{d}{d T} \operatorname{ a\alpha_{i}}{\left(T \right)}}{2}\right)}{\operatorname{a\alpha_{i}}{\left(T \right)} \operatorname{a\alpha_{j}}{\left(T \right)}}
$$

$$
\frac{\partial^2 (a\alpha)_{ij}}{\partial T^2} = - \frac{\sqrt{\operatorname{a\alpha_{i}}{\left(T \right)} \operatorname{a\alpha_{j}} {\left(T \right)}} \left(k_{ij} - 1\right) \left(\frac{\left(\operatorname{ a\alpha_{i}}{\left(T \right)} \frac{d}{d T} \operatorname{a\alpha_{j}}{\left(T \right)} + \operatorname{a\alpha_{j}}{\left(T \right)} \frac{d}{d T} \operatorname{a\alpha_{i}} {\left(T \right)}\right)^{2}}{4 \operatorname{a\alpha_{i}}{\left(T \right)} \operatorname{a\alpha_{j}}{\left(T \right)}} - \frac{\left(\operatorname{a\alpha_{i}} {\left(T \right)} \frac{d}{d T} \operatorname{a\alpha_{j}}{\left(T \right)} + \operatorname{a\alpha_{j}}{\left(T \right)} \frac{d}{d T} \operatorname{a\alpha_{i}}{\left(T \right)}\right) \frac{d}{d T} \operatorname{a\alpha_{j}}{\left(T \right)}}{2 \operatorname{a\alpha_{j}} {\left(T \right)}} - \frac{\left(\operatorname{a\alpha_{i}}{\left(T \right)} \frac{d}{d T} \operatorname{a\alpha_{j}}{\left(T \right)} + \operatorname{a\alpha_{j}}{\left(T \right)} \frac{d}{d T} \operatorname{a\alpha_{i}}{\left(T \right)}\right) \frac{d}{d T} \operatorname{a\alpha_{i}}{\left(T \right)}}{2 \operatorname{a\alpha_{i}} {\left(T \right)}} + \frac{\operatorname{a\alpha_{i}}{\left(T \right)} \frac{d^{2}}{d T^{2}} \operatorname{a\alpha_{j}}{\left(T \right)}}{2} + \frac{\operatorname{a\alpha_{j}}{\left(T \right)} \frac{d^{2}}{d T^{2}} \operatorname{a\alpha_{i}}{\left(T \right)}}{2} + \frac{d}{d T} \operatorname{a\alpha_{i}}{\left(T \right)} \frac{d}{d T} \operatorname{a\alpha_{j}}{\left(T \right)}\right)} {\operatorname{a\alpha_{i}}{\left(T \right)} \operatorname{a\alpha_{j}} {\left(T \right)}}
$$

# Parameters

a_alphas

: EOS attractive terms, \[J^2/mol^2/Pa\]

da_alpha_dTs

: Temperature derivative of coefficient calculated by EOS-specific
  method, \[J^2/mol^2/Pa/K\]

d2a_alpha_dT2s

: Second temperature derivative of coefficient calculated by
  EOS-specific method, \[J^2/mol^2/Pa/K\*\*2\]

T

: Temperature, not used, \[K\]

zs

: Mole fractions of each species

one_minus_kijs

: One minus the constant kijs, \[-\]

a_alpha_ijs

: Matrix of $(1-k_{ij})\sqrt{(a\alpha)_{i}(a\alpha)_{j}}$, \[J^2/mol^2/Pa\]

a_alpha_roots

: Array of $\sqrt{(a\alpha)_{i}}$ values, \[J/mol/Pa^0.5\]

a_alpha_ij_roots_inv

: Matrix of $\frac{1}{\sqrt{(a\alpha)_{i}}\sqrt{(a\alpha)_{j}}}$,
  \[mol^2\*Pa/J^2\]

# Returns

a_alpha

: EOS attractive term, \[J^2/mol^2/Pa\]

da_alpha_dT

: Temperature derivative of coefficient calculated by EOS-specific
  method, \[J^2/mol^2/Pa/K\]

d2a_alpha_dT2

: Second temperature derivative of coefficient calculated by
  EOS-specific method, \[J^2/mol^2/Pa/K\*\*2\]

a_alpha_ijs

: Matrix of $(1-k_{ij})\sqrt{(a\alpha)_{i}(a\alpha)_{j}}$,
  \[J^2/mol^2/Pa\]

da_alpha_dT_ijs

: Matrix of $\frac{\partial (a\alpha)_{ij}}{\partial T}$,
  \[J^2/mol^2/Pa/K\]

d2a_alpha_dT2_ijs

: Matrix of $\frac{\partial^2 (a\alpha)_{ij}}{\partial T^2}$,
  \[J^2/mol^2/Pa/K^2\]

# Notes

# Examples

```pycon
>>> kijs = [[0,.083],[0.083,0]]
>>> one_minus_kijs = [[1.0 - kij for kij in row] for row in kijs]
>>> zs = [0.1164203, 0.8835797]
>>> a_alphas = [0.2491099357671155, 0.6486495863528039]
>>> da_alpha_dTs = [-0.0005102028006086241, -0.0011131153520304886]
>>> d2a_alpha_dT2s = [1.8651128859234162e-06, 3.884331923127011e-06]
>>> a_alpha, da_alpha_dT, d2a_alpha_dT2, a_alpha_ijs, da_alpha_dT_ijs, d2a_alpha_dT2_ijs = a_alpha_and_derivatives_full(a_alphas=a_alphas, da_alpha_dTs=da_alpha_dTs, d2a_alpha_dT2s=d2a_alpha_dT2s, T=299.0, zs=zs, one_minus_kijs=one_minus_kijs)
>>> a_alpha, da_alpha_dT, d2a_alpha_dT2
(0.58562139582, -0.001018667672, 3.56669817856e-06)
>>> a_alpha_ijs
[[0.2491099357, 0.3686123937], [0.36861239374, 0.64864958635]]
>>> da_alpha_dT_ijs
[[-0.000510202800, -0.0006937567844], [-0.000693756784, -0.00111311535]]
>>> d2a_alpha_dT2_ijs
[[1.865112885e-06, 2.4734471244e-06], [2.4734471244e-06, 3.8843319e-06]]
```

functions.name: thermo.eos_mix_methods.a_alpha_quadratic_terms
documentation:
Calculates the `a_alpha` term for an equation of state along with the
vector quantities needed to compute the fugacities of the mixture. This
routine is efficient in both numba and PyPy.

$$
a \alpha = \sum_i \sum_j z_i z_j {(a\alpha)}_{ij}
$$

$$
(a\alpha)_{ij} = (1-k_{ij})\sqrt{(a\alpha)_{i}(a\alpha)_{j}}
$$

The secondary values are as follows:

$$
\sum_i y_i(a\alpha)_{ij}
$$

# Parameters

a_alphas

: EOS attractive terms, \[J^2/mol^2/Pa\]

a_alpha_roots

: Square roots of `a_alphas`; provided for speed \[J/mol/Pa^0.5\]

T

: Temperature, not used, \[K\]

zs

: Mole fractions of each species

one_minus_kijs

: One minus the constant kijs, \[-\]

a_alpha_j_rows

: EOS attractive term row destimation vector (does not need
  to be zeroed, should be provided to prevent allocations),
  \[J^2/mol^2/Pa\]

vec0

: Empty vector, used in internal calculations, provide to avoid
  the allocations; does not need to be zeroed, \[-\]

# Returns

a_alpha

: EOS attractive term, \[J^2/mol^2/Pa\]

a_alpha_j_rows

: EOS attractive term row sums, \[J^2/mol^2/Pa\]

# Notes

Tried moving the i=j loop out, no difference in speed, maybe got a bit slower
in PyPy.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> kijs = [[0,.083],[0.083,0]]
>>> one_minus_kijs = [[1.0 - kij for kij in row] for row in kijs]
>>> zs = [0.1164203, 0.8835797]
>>> a_alphas = [0.2491099357671155, 0.6486495863528039]
>>> a_alpha_roots = [i**0.5 for i in a_alphas]
>>> a_alpha, a_alpha_j_rows = a_alpha_quadratic_terms(a_alphas, a_alpha_roots, 299.0, zs, one_minus_kijs)
>>> a_alpha, a_alpha_j_rows
(0.58562139582, [0.35469988173, 0.61604757237])
```

functions.name: thermo.eos_mix_methods.a_alpha_and_derivatives_quadratic_terms
documentation:
Calculates the `a_alpha` term, and its first two temperature
derivatives, for an equation of state along with the
vector quantities needed to compute the fugacitie and temperature
derivatives of fugacities of the mixture. This
routine is efficient in both numba and PyPy.

$$
a \alpha = \sum_i \sum_j z_i z_j {(a\alpha)}_{ij}
$$

$$
\frac{\partial (a\alpha)}{\partial T} = \sum_i \sum_j z_i z_j \frac{\partial (a\alpha)_{ij}}{\partial T}
$$

$$
\frac{\partial^2 (a\alpha)}{\partial T^2} = \sum_i \sum_j z_i z_j \frac{\partial^2 (a\alpha)_{ij}}{\partial T^2}
$$

$$
(a\alpha)_{ij} = (1-k_{ij})\sqrt{(a\alpha)_{i}(a\alpha)_{j}}
$$

$$
\frac{\partial (a\alpha)_{ij}}{\partial T} = \frac{\sqrt{\operatorname{a\alpha_{i}}{\left(T \right)} \operatorname{a\alpha_{j}} {\left(T \right)}} \left(1 - k_{ij}\right) \left(\frac{\operatorname{a\alpha_{i}} {\left(T \right)} \frac{d}{d T} \operatorname{a\alpha_{j}}{\left(T \right)}}{2} + \frac{\operatorname{a\alpha_{j}}{\left(T \right)} \frac{d}{d T} \operatorname{ a\alpha_{i}}{\left(T \right)}}{2}\right)}{\operatorname{a\alpha_{i}}{\left(T \right)} \operatorname{a\alpha_{j}}{\left(T \right)}}
$$

$$
\frac{\partial^2 (a\alpha)_{ij}}{\partial T^2} = - \frac{\sqrt{\operatorname{a\alpha_{i}}{\left(T \right)} \operatorname{a\alpha_{j}} {\left(T \right)}} \left(k_{ij} - 1\right) \left(\frac{\left(\operatorname{ a\alpha_{i}}{\left(T \right)} \frac{d}{d T} \operatorname{a\alpha_{j}}{\left(T \right)} + \operatorname{a\alpha_{j}}{\left(T \right)} \frac{d}{d T} \operatorname{a\alpha_{i}} {\left(T \right)}\right)^{2}}{4 \operatorname{a\alpha_{i}}{\left(T \right)} \operatorname{a\alpha_{j}}{\left(T \right)}} - \frac{\left(\operatorname{a\alpha_{i}} {\left(T \right)} \frac{d}{d T} \operatorname{a\alpha_{j}}{\left(T \right)} + \operatorname{a\alpha_{j}}{\left(T \right)} \frac{d}{d T} \operatorname{a\alpha_{i}}{\left(T \right)}\right) \frac{d}{d T} \operatorname{a\alpha_{j}}{\left(T \right)}}{2 \operatorname{a\alpha_{j}} {\left(T \right)}} - \frac{\left(\operatorname{a\alpha_{i}}{\left(T \right)} \frac{d}{d T} \operatorname{a\alpha_{j}}{\left(T \right)} + \operatorname{a\alpha_{j}}{\left(T \right)} \frac{d}{d T} \operatorname{a\alpha_{i}}{\left(T \right)}\right) \frac{d}{d T} \operatorname{a\alpha_{i}}{\left(T \right)}}{2 \operatorname{a\alpha_{i}} {\left(T \right)}} + \frac{\operatorname{a\alpha_{i}}{\left(T \right)} \frac{d^{2}}{d T^{2}} \operatorname{a\alpha_{j}}{\left(T \right)}}{2} + \frac{\operatorname{a\alpha_{j}}{\left(T \right)} \frac{d^{2}}{d T^{2}} \operatorname{a\alpha_{i}}{\left(T \right)}}{2} + \frac{d}{d T} \operatorname{a\alpha_{i}}{\left(T \right)} \frac{d}{d T} \operatorname{a\alpha_{j}}{\left(T \right)}\right)} {\operatorname{a\alpha_{i}}{\left(T \right)} \operatorname{a\alpha_{j}} {\left(T \right)}}
$$

The secondary values are as follows:

$$
\sum_i y_i(a\alpha)_{ij}
$$

$$
\sum_i y_i \frac{\partial (a\alpha)_{ij}}{\partial T}
$$

# Parameters

a_alphas

: EOS attractive terms, \[J^2/mol^2/Pa\]

a_alpha_roots

: Square roots of `a_alphas`; provided for speed \[J/mol/Pa^0.5\]

da_alpha_dTs

: Temperature derivative of coefficient calculated by EOS-specific
  method, \[J^2/mol^2/Pa/K\]

d2a_alpha_dT2s

: Second temperature derivative of coefficient calculated by
  EOS-specific method, \[J^2/mol^2/Pa/K\*\*2\]

T

: Temperature, not used, \[K\]

zs

: Mole fractions of each species

one_minus_kijs

: One minus the constant kijs, \[-\]

# Returns

a_alpha

: EOS attractive term, \[J^2/mol^2/Pa\]

da_alpha_dT

: Temperature derivative of coefficient calculated by EOS-specific
  method, \[J^2/mol^2/Pa/K\]

d2a_alpha_dT2

: Second temperature derivative of coefficient calculated by
  EOS-specific method, \[J^2/mol^2/Pa/K\*\*2\]

a_alpha_j_rows

: EOS attractive term row sums, \[J^2/mol^2/Pa\]

da_alpha_dT_j_rows

: Temperature derivative of EOS attractive term row sums, \[J^2/mol^2/Pa/K\]

# Notes

# Examples

```pycon
>>> kijs = [[0,.083],[0.083,0]]
>>> one_minus_kijs = [[1.0 - kij for kij in row] for row in kijs]
>>> zs = [0.1164203, 0.8835797]
>>> a_alphas = [0.2491099357671155, 0.6486495863528039]
>>> a_alpha_roots = [i**0.5 for i in a_alphas]
>>> da_alpha_dTs = [-0.0005102028006086241, -0.0011131153520304886]
>>> d2a_alpha_dT2s = [1.8651128859234162e-06, 3.884331923127011e-06]
>>> a_alpha_and_derivatives_quadratic_terms(a_alphas, a_alpha_roots, da_alpha_dTs, d2a_alpha_dT2s, 299.0, zs, one_minus_kijs)
(0.58562139582, -0.001018667672, 3.56669817856e-06, [0.35469988173, 0.61604757237], [-0.000672387374, -0.001064293501])
```

This module contains classes for storing data and objects which are necessary
for doing thermodynamic calculations. The intention for these classes is to
serve as an in-memory storage layer between the disk and methods which do
full thermodynamic calculations.

For reporting bugs, adding feature requests, or submitting pull requests,
please use the [GitHub issue tracker](https://github.com/CalebBell/thermo/).

```{contents}
:local: true
```

methods.name: thermo.chemical_package.ChemicalConstantsPackage.as_json
documentation:
Method to create a JSON friendly serialization of the chemical constants
package which can be stored, and reloaded later.

# Returns

json_repr

: Json friendly representation, \[-\]

# Notes

# Examples

```pycon
>>> import json
>>> constants = ChemicalConstantsPackage(MWs=[18.01528, 106.165], names=['water', 'm-xylene'])
>>> string = json.dumps(constants.as_json())
```

methods.name: thermo.chemical_package.ChemicalConstantsPackage.from_json
documentation:
Method to create a ChemicalConstantsPackage from a JSON
serialization of another ChemicalConstantsPackage.

# Parameters

json_repr

: Json representation, \[-\]

# Returns

constants

: Newly created object from the json serialization, \[-\]

# Notes

It is important that the input be in the same format as that
created by {obj}`ChemicalConstantsPackage.as_json`.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> import json
>>> constants = ChemicalConstantsPackage(MWs=[18.01528, 106.165], names=['water', 'm-xylene'])
>>> string = json.dumps(constants.as_json())
>>> new_constants  = ChemicalConstantsPackage.from_json(json.loads(string))
>>> assert hash(new_constants) == hash(constants)
```

methods.name: thermo.chemical_package.ChemicalConstantsPackage.with_new_constants
documentation:
Method to construct a new ChemicalConstantsPackage that replaces or
adds one or more properties for all components.

# Parameters

kwargs

: Properties specified by name \[various\]

# Returns

new_constants

: Object with new and/or replaced properties, \[-\]

# Notes

# Examples

```pycon
>>> base = ChemicalConstantsPackage(MWs=[18.01528, 106.165, 106.165, 106.165], names=['water', 'o-xylene', 'p-xylene', 'm-xylene'], omegas=[0.344, 0.3118, 0.324, 0.331], Pcs=[22048320.0, 3732000.0, 3511000.0, 3541000.0], Tcs=[647.14, 630.3, 616.2, 617.0])
>>> base.with_new_constants(Tms=[40.0, 20.0, 10.0, 30.0], omegas=[0.0, 0.1, 0.2, 0.3])
ChemicalConstantsPackage(MWs=[18.01528, 106.165, 106.165, 106.165], names=['water', 'o-xylene', 'p-xylene', 'm-xylene'], omegas=[0.0, 0.1, 0.2, 0.3], Pcs=[22048320.0, 3732000.0, 3511000.0, 3541000.0], Tcs=[647.14, 630.3, 616.2, 617.0], Tms=[40.0, 20.0, 10.0, 30.0])
```

methods.name: thermo.chemical_package.ChemicalConstantsPackage.subset
documentation:
Method to construct a new ChemicalConstantsPackage that removes
all components not specified in the `idxs` argument. Although this
class has a great many attributes, it is often sufficient to work with
a subset of those properties; and if a list of properties is provided,
only those properties will be added to the new object as well.

# Parameters

idxs

: Indexes of components that should be included; if None, all
  components will be included , \[-\]

properties

: List of properties to be included; all properties will be included
  if this is not specified

# Returns

subset_consts

: Object with reduced properties and or components, \[-\]

# Notes

It is not intended for properties to be edited in this object!
One optimization is that all entirely empty properties use the same
list-of-Nones.

All properties should have been specified before constructing the first
ChemicalConstantsPackage.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> base = ChemicalConstantsPackage(MWs=[18.01528, 106.165, 106.165, 106.165], names=['water', 'o-xylene', 'p-xylene', 'm-xylene'], omegas=[0.344, 0.3118, 0.324, 0.331], Pcs=[22048320.0, 3732000.0, 3511000.0, 3541000.0], Tcs=[647.14, 630.3, 616.2, 617.0])
>>> base.subset([0])
ChemicalConstantsPackage(MWs=[18.01528], names=['water'], omegas=[0.344], Pcs=[22048320.0], Tcs=[647.14])
>>> base.subset(slice(1,4))
ChemicalConstantsPackage(MWs=[106.165, 106.165, 106.165], names=['o-xylene', 'p-xylene', 'm-xylene'], omegas=[0.3118, 0.324, 0.331], Pcs=[3732000.0, 3511000.0, 3541000.0], Tcs=[630.3, 616.2, 617.0])
>>> base.subset(idxs=[0, 3], properties=('names', 'MWs'))
ChemicalConstantsPackage(MWs=[18.01528, 106.165], names=['water', 'm-xylene'])
```

methods.name: thermo.chemical_package.ChemicalConstantsPackage.compound_index
documentation:
Method to retrieve the index of a compound given one of the
optional identifiers for the compound.

Parameters
----------
CAS : str, optional
    The CAS number of the compound, [-]
name : str, optional
    The (provided) name of the compound, [-]
smiles : str, optional
    Smiles identifier, [-]
InChI : str, optional
    InChI identifier, [-]
InChI_Key : str, optional
    InChI key identifier, [-]
PubChem : int, optional
    PubChem identifier, [-]

Returns
-------
index : int
    The index of the component (if found), [-]

Raises
------
ValueError
    If no identifier is provided for any argument, this is raised
IndexError
    If no match is found for the provided identifier, this is raised

documentation:
Method to construct a new `ChemicalConstantsPackage` with loaded
parameters from the [chemicals library](https://github.com/CalebBell/chemicals),
using whatever default methods and values happen to be in that library.
Expect values to change over time.

# Parameters

IDs

: Identifying strings for each compound;
  most identifiers are accepted and all inputs are documented in

methods.name: thermo.chemical_package.PropertyCorrelationsPackage.subset
documentation:
Method to construct a new PropertyCorrelationsPackage that removes
all components not specified in the `idxs` argument.

Parameters
----------
idxs : list[int] or Slice or None
    Indexes of components that should be included; if None, all
    components will be included , [-]

Returns
-------
subset_correlations : PropertyCorrelationsPackage
    Object with components, [-]

Notes
-----

Class for creating and storing `T` and `P` and `zs` dependent chemical
property objects. All parameters are also attributes.

This object can be used either to hold already-created property objects;
or to create new ones and hold them.

# Parameters

constants

: Object holding all constant properties, \[-\]

VaporPressures

: Objects holding vapor pressure data and methods, \[-\]

SublimationPressures

: Objects holding sublimation pressure data and methods, \[-\]

VolumeGases

: Objects holding gas volume data and methods, \[-\]

VolumeLiquids

: Objects holding liquid volume data and methods, \[-\]

VolumeSolids

: Objects holding solid volume data and methods, \[-\]

HeatCapacityGases

: Objects holding gas heat capacity data and methods, \[-\]

HeatCapacityLiquids

: Objects holding liquid heat capacity data and methods, \[-\]

HeatCapacitySolids

: Objects holding solid heat capacity data and methods, \[-\]

ViscosityGases

: Objects holding gas viscosity data and methods, \[-\]

ViscosityLiquids

: Objects holding liquid viscosity data and methods, \[-\]

ThermalConductivityGases

: Objects holding gas thermal conductivity data and methods, \[-\]

ThermalConductivityLiquids

: Objects holding liquid thermal conductivity data and methods, \[-\]

ThermalConductivitySolids

: Objects holding solid thermal conductivity data and methods, \[-\]

EnthalpyVaporizations

: Objects holding enthalpy of vaporization data and methods, \[-\]

EnthalpySublimations

: Objects holding enthalpy of sublimation data and methods, \[-\]

SurfaceTensions

: Objects holding surface tension data and methods, \[-\]

PermittivityLiquids

: Objects holding permittivity data and methods, \[-\]

skip_missing

: If False, any properties not provided will have objects created; if
  True, no extra objects will be created.

VolumeSolidMixture

: Predictor object for the volume of solid mixtures, \[-\]

VolumeLiquidMixture

: Predictor object for the volume of liquid mixtures, \[-\]

VolumeGasMixture

: Predictor object for the volume of gas mixtures, \[-\]

HeatCapacityLiquidMixture

: Predictor object for the heat capacity of liquid mixtures, \[-\]

HeatCapacityGasMixture

: Predictor object for the heat capacity of gas mixtures, \[-\]

HeatCapacitySolidMixture

: Predictor object for the heat capacity of solid mixtures, \[-\]

ViscosityLiquidMixture

: Predictor object for the viscosity of liquid mixtures, \[-\]

ViscosityGasMixture

: Predictor object for the viscosity of gas mixtures, \[-\]

ThermalConductivityLiquidMixture

: Predictor object for the thermal conductivity of liquid mixtures, \[-\]

ThermalConductivityGasMixture

: Predictor object for the thermal conductivity of gas mixtures, \[-\]

SurfaceTensionMixture

: Predictor object for the surface tension of liquid mixtures, \[-\]

# Attributes

pure_correlations

: List of all pure component property objects, \[-\]

# Examples

Create a package from CO2 and n-hexane, with ideal-gas heat capacities
provided while excluding all other properties:

```pycon
>>> constants = ChemicalConstantsPackage(CASs=['124-38-9', '110-54-3'], MWs=[44.0095, 86.17536], names=['carbon dioxide', 'hexane'], omegas=[0.2252, 0.2975], Pcs=[7376460.0, 3025000.0], Tbs=[194.67, 341.87], Tcs=[304.2, 507.6], Tms=[216.65, 178.075])
>>> correlations = PropertyCorrelationsPackage(constants=constants, skip_missing=True, HeatCapacityGases=[HeatCapacityGas(poly_fit=(50.0, 1000.0, [-3.1115474168865828e-21, 1.39156078498805e-17, -2.5430881416264243e-14, 2.4175307893014295e-11, -1.2437314771044867e-08, 3.1251954264658904e-06, -0.00021220221928610925, 0.000884685506352987, 29.266811602924644])), HeatCapacityGas(poly_fit=(200.0, 1000.0, [1.3740654453881647e-21, -8.344496203280677e-18, 2.2354782954548568e-14, -3.4659555330048226e-11, 3.410703030634579e-08, -2.1693611029230923e-05, 0.008373280796376588, -1.356180511425385, 175.67091124888998]))])
```

Create a package from various data files, creating all property objects:

```pycon
>>> correlations = PropertyCorrelationsPackage(constants=constants, skip_missing=False)
```

functions.name: thermo.group_contribution.fedors.Fedors
documentation:
Estimate the critical volume of a molecule
using the Fedors [^footnote-1] method, which is a basic
group contribution method that also uses certain
bond count features and the number of different
types of rings.

# Parameters

mol

: Smiles string representing a chemical or a rdkit molecule, \[-\]

# Returns

Vc

: Estimated critical volume, \[m^3/mol\]

status

: A string holding an explanation of why the molecule failed to be
  fragmented, if it fails; 'OK' if it suceeds, \[-\]

unmatched_atoms

: Whether or not all atoms in the molecule were matched successfully;
  if this is True, the results should not be trusted, \[-\]

unrecognized_bond

: Whether or not all bonds in the molecule were matched successfully;
  if this is True, the results should not be trusted, \[-\]

unrecognized_ring_size

: Whether or not all rings in the molecule were matched successfully;
  if this is True, the results should not be trusted, \[-\]

# Notes

Raises an exception if rdkit is not installed, or `smi` or `rdkitmol` is
not defined.

# Examples

Example for sec-butanol in [^footnote-2]:

```pycon
>>> Vc, status, _, _, _ = Fedors('CCC(C)O') # doctest:+SKIP
>>> Vc, status # doctest:+SKIP
(0.000274024, 'OK')
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Fedors, R. F. "A Method to Estimate Critical Volumes." AIChE
    Journal 25, no. 1 (1979): 202-202. <https://doi.org/10.1002/aic.690250129>.

[^footnote-2]: Green, Don, and Robert Perry. Perry's Chemical Engineers' Handbook,
    Eighth Edition. McGraw-Hill Professional, 2007.

composition `zs` dependent properties. These power the various interfaces for
each property.

This module contains an implementation of the Joback group-contribution method.
This functionality requires the RDKit library to work.

For submitting pull requests,
please use the [GitHub issue tracker](https://github.com/CalebBell/thermo/).

:::{warning}
The Joback class method does not contain all the groups for every chemical.
There are often multiple ways of fragmenting a chemical. Other times, the
fragmentation algorithm will fail. These limitations are present in both
the implementation and the method itself. You are welcome to seek to
improve this code but no to little help can be offered.
:::

```{contents}
:local: true
```

variables.name: thermo.group_contribution.joback.J_BIGGS_JOBACK_SMARTS
documentation:
Metadata for the Joback groups. The first element is the group name; the
second is the group symbol; and the third is the SMARTS matching string.

classes.name: thermo.group_contribution.joback.Joback

methods.name: thermo.group_contribution.joback.Joback.estimate
documentation:
Method to compute all available properties with the Joback method;
returns their results as a dict. For the tempearture dependent values
Cpig and mul, both the coefficients and objects to perform calculations
are returned.

methods.name: thermo.group_contribution.joback.Joback.Tb
documentation:
Estimates the normal boiling temperature of an organic compound
using the Joback method as a function of chemical structure only.

$$
T_b = 198.2 + \sum_i {T_{b,i}}
$$

For 438 compounds tested by Joback, the absolute average error was
12.91 K  and standard deviation was 17.85 K; the average relative error
was 3.6%.

# Parameters

counts

: Dictionary of Joback groups present (numerically indexed) and their
  counts, \[-\]

# Returns

Tb

: Estimated normal boiling temperature, \[K\]

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Joback.Tb({1: 2, 24: 1})
322.11
```

methods.name: thermo.group_contribution.joback.Joback.Tm
documentation:
Estimates the melting temperature of an organic compound using the
Joback method as a function of chemical structure only.

$$
T_m = 122.5 + \sum_i {T_{m,i}}
$$

For 388 compounds tested by Joback, the absolute average error was
22.6 K  and standard deviation was 24.68 K; the average relative error
was 11.2%.

# Parameters

counts

: Dictionary of Joback groups present (numerically indexed) and their
  counts, \[-\]

# Returns

Tm

: Estimated melting temperature, \[K\]

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Joback.Tm({1: 2, 24: 1})
173.5
```

methods.name: thermo.group_contribution.joback.Joback.Tc
documentation:
Estimates the critcal temperature of an organic compound using the
Joback method as a function of chemical structure only, or optionally
improved by using an experimental boiling point. If the experimental
boiling point is not provided it will be estimated with the Joback
method as well.

$$
T_c = T_b \left[0.584 + 0.965 \sum_i {T_{c,i}} - \left(\sum_i {T_{c,i}}\right)^2 \right]^{-1}
$$

For 409 compounds tested by Joback, the absolute average error was
4.76 K  and standard deviation was 6.94 K; the average relative error
was 0.81%.

Appendix BI of Joback's work lists 409 estimated critical temperatures.

# Parameters

counts

: Dictionary of Joback groups present (numerically indexed) and their
  counts, \[-\]

Tb

: Experimental normal boiling temperature, \[K\]

# Returns

Tc

: Estimated critical temperature, \[K\]

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Joback.Tc({1: 2, 24: 1}, Tb=322.11)
500.5590049525365
```

methods.name: thermo.group_contribution.joback.Joback.Pc
documentation:
Estimates the critcal pressure of an organic compound using the
Joback method as a function of chemical structure only. This
correlation was developed using the actual number of atoms forming
the molecule as well.

$$
P_c = \left [0.113 + 0.0032N_A - \sum_i {P_{c,i}}\right ]^{-2}
$$

In the above equation, critical pressure is calculated in bar; it is
converted to Pa here.

392 compounds were used by Joback in this determination, with an
absolute average error of 2.06 bar, standard devaition 3.2 bar, and
AARE of 5.2%.

# Parameters

counts

: Dictionary of Joback groups present (numerically indexed) and their
  counts, \[-\]

atom_count

: Total number of atoms (including hydrogens) in the molecule, \[-\]

# Returns

Pc

: Estimated critical pressure, \[Pa\]

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Joback.Pc({1: 2, 24: 1}, 10)
4802499.604994407
```

methods.name: thermo.group_contribution.joback.Joback.Vc
documentation:
Estimates the critcal volume of an organic compound using the
Joback method as a function of chemical structure only.

$$
V_c = 17.5 + \sum_i {V_{c,i}}
$$

In the above equation, critical volume is calculated in cm^3/mol; it
is converted to m^3/mol here.

310 compounds were used by Joback in this determination, with an
absolute average error of 7.54 cm^3/mol, standard devaition 13.16
cm^3/mol, and AARE of 2.27%.

# Parameters

counts

: Dictionary of Joback groups present (numerically indexed) and their
  counts, \[-\]

# Returns

Vc

: Estimated critical volume, \[m^3/mol\]

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Joback.Vc({1: 2, 24: 1})
0.0002095
```

methods.name: thermo.group_contribution.joback.Joback.Hf
documentation:
Estimates the ideal-gas enthalpy of formation at 298.15 K of an
organic compound using the Joback method as a function of chemical
structure only.

$$
H_{formation} = 68.29 + \sum_i {H_{f,i}}
$$

In the above equation, enthalpy of formation is calculated in kJ/mol;
it is converted to J/mol here.

370 compounds were used by Joback in this determination, with an
absolute average error of 2.2 kcal/mol, standard devaition 2.0
kcal/mol, and AARE of 15.2%.

# Parameters

counts

: Dictionary of Joback groups present (numerically indexed) and their
  counts, \[-\]

# Returns

Hf

: Estimated ideal-gas enthalpy of formation at 298.15 K, \[J/mol\]

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Joback.Hf({1: 2, 24: 1})
-217829.99999999997
```

methods.name: thermo.group_contribution.joback.Joback.Gf
documentation:
Estimates the ideal-gas Gibbs energy of formation at 298.15 K of an
organic compound using the Joback method as a function of chemical
structure only.

$$
G_{formation} = 53.88 + \sum {G_{f,i}}
$$

In the above equation, Gibbs energy of formation is calculated in
kJ/mol; it is converted to J/mol here.

328 compounds were used by Joback in this determination, with an
absolute average error of 2.0 kcal/mol, standard devaition 4.37
kcal/mol, and AARE of 15.7%.

# Parameters

counts

: Dictionary of Joback groups present (numerically indexed) and their
  counts, \[-\]

# Returns

Gf

: Estimated ideal-gas Gibbs energy of formation at 298.15 K, \[J/mol\]

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Joback.Gf({1: 2, 24: 1})
-154540.00000000003
```

methods.name: thermo.group_contribution.joback.Joback.Hfus
documentation:
Estimates the enthalpy of fusion of an organic compound at its
melting point using the Joback method as a function of chemical
structure only.

$$
\Delta H_{fus} = -0.88 + \sum_i H_{fus,i}
$$

In the above equation, enthalpy of fusion is calculated in
kJ/mol; it is converted to J/mol here.

For 155 compounds tested by Joback, the absolute average error was
485.2 cal/mol  and standard deviation was 661.4 cal/mol; the average
relative error was 38.7%.

# Parameters

counts

: Dictionary of Joback groups present (numerically indexed) and their
  counts, \[-\]

# Returns

Hfus

: Estimated enthalpy of fusion of the compound at its melting point,
  \[J/mol\]

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Joback.Hfus({1: 2, 24: 1})
5125.0
```

methods.name: thermo.group_contribution.joback.Joback.Hvap
documentation:
Estimates the enthalpy of vaporization of an organic compound at
its normal boiling point using the Joback method as a function of
chemical structure only.

$$
\Delta H_{vap} = 15.30 + \sum_i H_{vap,i}
$$

In the above equation, enthalpy of fusion is calculated in
kJ/mol; it is converted to J/mol here.

For 368 compounds tested by Joback, the absolute average error was
303.5 cal/mol  and standard deviation was 429 cal/mol; the average
relative error was 3.88%.

# Parameters

counts

: Dictionary of Joback groups present (numerically indexed) and their
  counts, \[-\]

# Returns

Hvap

: Estimated enthalpy of vaporization of the compound at its normal
  boiling point, \[J/mol\]

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Joback.Hvap({1: 2, 24: 1})
29018.0
```

methods.name: thermo.group_contribution.joback.Joback.Cpig_coeffs
documentation:
Computes the ideal-gas polynomial heat capacity coefficients
of an organic compound using the Joback method as a function of
chemical structure only.

$$
C_p^{ig} = \sum_i a_i - 37.93 + \left[ \sum_i b_i + 0.210 \right] T + \left[ \sum_i c_i - 3.91 \cdot 10^{-4} \right] T^2 + \left[\sum_i d_i + 2.06 \cdot 10^{-7}\right] T^3
$$

288 compounds were used by Joback in this determination. No overall
error was reported.

The ideal gas heat capacity values used in developing the heat
capacity polynomials used 9 data points between 298 K and 1000 K.

# Parameters

counts

: Dictionary of Joback groups present (numerically indexed) and their
  counts, \[-\]

# Returns

coefficients

: Coefficients which will result in a calculated heat capacity in
  in units of J/mol/K, \[-\]

# Examples

```pycon
>>> c = Joback.Cpig_coeffs({1: 2, 24: 1})
>>> c
[7.520000000000003, 0.26084, -0.0001207, 1.545999999999998e-08]
>>> Cp = lambda T : c[0] + c[1]*T + c[2]*T**2 + c[3]*T**3
>>> Cp(300)
75.32642000000001
```

methods.name: thermo.group_contribution.joback.Joback.mul_coeffs
documentation:
Computes the liquid phase viscosity Joback coefficients
of an organic compound using the Joback method as a function of
chemical structure only.

$$
\mu_{liq} = \text{MW} \exp\left( \frac{ \sum_i \mu_a - 597.82}{T} + \sum_i \mu_b - 11.202 \right)
$$

288 compounds were used by Joback in this determination. No overall
error was reported.

The liquid viscosity data used was specified to be at "several
temperatures for each compound" only. A small and unspecified number
of compounds were used in this estimation.

# Parameters

counts

: Dictionary of Joback groups present (numerically indexed) and their
  counts, \[-\]

# Returns

coefficients

: Coefficients which will result in a liquid viscosity in
  in units of Pa\*s, \[-\]

# Examples

```pycon
>>> mu_ab = Joback.mul_coeffs({1: 2, 24: 1})
>>> mu_ab
[839.1099999999998, -14.99]
>>> MW = 58.041864812
>>> mul = lambda T : MW*exp(mu_ab[0]/T + mu_ab[1])
>>> mul(300)
0.0002940378347162687
```

methods.name: thermo.group_contribution.joback.Joback.Cpig
documentation:
Computes ideal-gas heat capacity at a specified temperature
of an organic compound using the Joback method as a function of
chemical structure only.

$$
C_p^{ig} = \sum_i a_i - 37.93 + \left[ \sum_i b_i + 0.210 \right] T + \left[ \sum_i c_i - 3.91 \cdot 10^{-4} \right] T^2 + \left[\sum_i d_i + 2.06 \cdot 10^{-7}\right] T^3
$$

# Parameters

T

: Temperature, \[K\]

# Returns

Cpig

: Ideal-gas heat capacity, \[J/mol/K\]

# Examples

```pycon
>>> J = Joback('CC(=O)C') # doctest:+SKIP
>>> J.Cpig(300) # doctest:+SKIP
75.32642000000001
```

methods.name: thermo.group_contribution.joback.Joback.mul
documentation:
Computes liquid viscosity at a specified temperature
of an organic compound using the Joback method as a function of
chemical structure only.

$$
\mu_{liq} = \text{MW} \exp\left( \frac{ \sum_i \mu_a - 597.82}{T} + \sum_i \mu_b - 11.202 \right)
$$

# Parameters

T

: Temperature, \[K\]

# Returns

mul

: Liquid viscosity, \[Pa\*s\]

# Examples

```pycon
>>> J = Joback('CC(=O)C') # doctest:+SKIP
>>> J.mul(300) # doctest:+SKIP
0.0002940378347162687
```

Class for performing chemical property estimations with the Joback
group contribution method as described in [^footnote-1] and [^footnote-2]. This is a very
common method with low accuracy but wide applicability. This routine can be
used with either its own automatic fragmentation routine, or user specified
groups. It is applicable to organic compounds only, and has only 41 groups
with no interactions between them. Each method's documentation describes
its accuracy. The automatic fragmentation routine is possible only because
of the development of SMARTS expressions to match the Joback groups by
Dr. Jason Biggs. The list of SMARTS expressions
was posted publically on the
[RDKit mailing list](https://www.mail-archive.com/rdkit-discuss@lists.sourceforge.net/msg07446.html).

# Parameters

mol

: Input molecule for the analysis, \[-\]

atom_count

: The total number of atoms including hydrogen in the molecule; this will
  be counted by rdkit if it not provided, \[-\]

MW

: Molecular weight of the molecule; this will be calculated by rdkit if
  not provided, \[g/mol\]

Tb

: An experimentally known boiling temperature for the chemical; this
  increases the accuracy of the calculated critical point if provided.
  \[K\]

# Notes

Be sure to check the status of the automatic fragmentation; not all
chemicals with the Joback method are applicable.

Approximately 68% of chemcials in the thermo database seem to be able to
be estimated with the Joback method.

If a group which was identified is missign a regressed contribution, the
estimated property will be None. However, if not all atoms of a molecule
are identified as particular groups, property estimation will go ahead
with heavily reduced accuracy. Check the `status` attribute to be sure
a molecule was properly fragmented.

# Examples

Analysis of Acetone:

```pycon
>>> J = Joback('CC(=O)C') # doctest:+SKIP
>>> J.Hfus(J.counts) # doctest:+SKIP
5125.0
>>> J.Cpig(350) # doctest:+SKIP
84.69109750000001
>>> J.status # doctest:+SKIP
'OK'
```

All properties can be obtained in one go with the `estimate` method:

```pycon
>>> J.estimate(callables=False) # doctest:+SKIP
{'Tb': 322.11, 'Tm': 173.5, 'Tc': 500.5590049525365, 'Pc': 4802499.604994407, 'Vc': 0.0002095, 'Hf': -217829.99999999997, 'Gf': -154540.00000000003, 'Hfus': 5125.0, 'Hvap': 29018.0, 'mul_coeffs': [839.1099999999998, -14.99], 'Cpig_coeffs': [7.520000000000003, 0.26084, -0.0001207, 1.545999999999998e-08]}
```

The results for propionic anhydride (if the status is not OK) should not be
used.

```pycon
>>> J = Joback('CCC(=O)OC(=O)CC') # doctest:+SKIP
>>> J.status # doctest:+SKIP
'Matched some atoms repeatedly: [4]'
>>> J.Cpig(300) # doctest:+SKIP
175.85999999999999
```

None of the routines need to use the automatic routine; they can be used
manually too:

```pycon
>>> Joback.Tb({1: 2, 24: 1})
322.11
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Joback, Kevin G. "A Unified Approach to Physical Property Estimation
    Using Multivariate Statistical Techniques." Thesis, Massachusetts
    Institute of Technology, 1984.

[^footnote-2]: Joback, K.G., and R.C. Reid. "Estimation of Pure-Component
    Properties from Group-Contributions." Chemical Engineering
    Communications 57, no. 1-6 (July 1, 1987): 233-43.
    doi:10.1080/00986448708960487.

functions.name: thermo.group_contribution.bondi.bondi_van_der_waals_surface_area_volume
documentation:
Calculate the van der Waals volume `V_vdw` and surface area `A_vdw`
for a given molecule using Bondi group contributions.

This function identifies the Bondi groups present in the molecule
using SMARTS-based fragmentation and computes the total van der Waals
volume and surface area from the bondi group contributions method.

# Parameters

rdkitmol

: RDKit molecule object representing the chemical structure of the molecule, \[-\]

# Returns

V_vdw

: Van der Waals volume, \[m^3/mol\]

A_vdw

: Van der Waals surface area, \[m^2/mol\]

# Notes

:::{warning}
The corrections have not been implemented and this function has not yet undergone
validation.
:::

# Examples

```pycon
>>> from rdkit import Chem # doctest:+SKIP
>>> from thermo import Chemical # doctest:+SKIP
>>> mol = Chemical('decane').rdkitmol # doctest:+SKIP
>>> bondi_van_der_waals_surface_area_volume(mol) # doctest:+SKIP
(0.00010918, 1504000.0)
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Bondi, A. "Van Der Waals Volumes and Radii." The Journal of Physical
    Chemistry 68, no. 3 (March 1, 1964): 441-451. <https://doi.org/10.1021/j100785a001>.

[^footnote-2]: Horvath, ARI L., ed. "Chapter 3 - Relationships between Structure and Properties."
    In Studies in Physical and Theoretical Chemistry, 75:575-860. Molecular Design.
    Elsevier, 1992. <https://doi.org/10.1016/B978-0-444-89217-1.50007-7>.

functions.name: thermo.group_contribution.bondi.R_from_Van_der_Waals_volume
documentation:
Calculates the UNIFAC R parameter from a species' Van der Waals molar volume.

$$
R_k = \frac{V_{wk}}{15.17}
$$

# Parameters

V_vdw

: Unnormalized Van der Waals volume, \[m^3/mol\]

# Returns

R

: R UNIFAC parameter (normalized Van der Waals Volume)  \[-\]

# Examples

```pycon
>>> R_from_Van_der_Waals_volume(6.826196599999999e-05)
4.4998
```

# Notes

This function is the inverse of Van_der_Waals_volume().

functions.name: thermo.group_contribution.bondi.Q_from_Van_der_Waals_area
documentation:
Calculates the UNIFAC Q parameter from a species' Van der Waals molar surface area.

$$
Q_k = \frac{A_{wk}}{2.5 \times 10^9}
$$

# Parameters

A_vdw

: Unnormalized Van der Waals surface area, \[m^2/mol\]

# Returns

Q

: Q UNIFAC parameter (normalized Van der Waals Area)  \[-\]

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Q_from_Van_der_Waals_area(964000.0)
3.856
```

# Notes

This function is the inverse of Van_der_Waals_area().

- Aw (float): Polarizability contribution in cm^3/mole * 10^9.

functions.name: thermo.group_contribution.wilson_jasperson.Wilson_Jasperson
documentation:
Estimate the critical temperature and pressure of a molecule using
the molecule itself, and a known or estimated boiling point
using the Wilson-Jasperson method.

# Parameters

mol

: Smiles string representing a chemical or a rdkit molecule, \[-\]

Tb

: Known or estimated boiling point, \[K\]

second_order

: Whether to use the first order method (False), or the second order
  method, \[-\]

# Returns

Tc

: Estimated critical temperature, \[K\]

Pc

: Estimated critical pressure, \[Pa\]

missing_Tc_increments

: Whether or not there were missing atoms for the `Tc` calculation, \[-\]

missing_Pc_increments

: Whether or not there were missing atoms for the `Pc` calculation, \[-\]

# Notes

Raises an exception if rdkit is not installed, or `smi` or `rdkitmol` is
not defined.

Calculated values were published in [^footnote-3] for 448 compounds, as calculated
by NIST TDE. There appear to be further modifications to the method in
NIST TDE, as ~25% of values have differences larger than 5 K.

# Examples

Example for 2-ethylphenol in [^footnote-2]:

```pycon
>>> Tc, Pc, _, _ = Wilson_Jasperson('CCC1=CC=CC=C1O', Tb=477.67) # doctest:+SKIP
>>> (Tc, Pc) # doctest:+SKIP
(693.567, 3743819.6667)
>>> Tc, Pc, _, _ = Wilson_Jasperson('CCC1=CC=CC=C1O', Tb=477.67, second_order=False) # doctest:+SKIP
>>> (Tc, Pc) # doctest:+SKIP
(702.883, 3794106.49)
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Wilson, G. M., and L. V. Jasperson. "Critical Constants Tc, Pc,
    Estimation Based on Zero, First and Second Order Methods." In
    Proceedings of the AIChE Spring Meeting, 21, 1996.

[^footnote-2]: Poling, Bruce E. The Properties of Gases and Liquids. 5th edition.
    New York: McGraw-Hill Professional, 2000.

[^footnote-3]: Yan, Xinjian, Qian Dong, and Xiangrong Hong. "Reliability Analysis
    of Group-Contribution Methods in Predicting Critical Temperatures of
    Organic Compounds." Journal of Chemical & Engineering Data 48, no. 2
    (March 1, 2003): 374-80. <https://doi.org/10.1021/je025596f>.

functions.name: thermo.group_contribution.group_contribution_base.group_assignment_to_str
documentation:
Take a group contribution dictionary, and turn it into a string.
The string is usually more memory efficient.

# Parameters

counts

: Dictionaty of integer counts of the found groups only, indexed by
  the `group` \[-\]

pair_separator

: The separator between pairs of group assignments, \[-\]

key_val_separator

: The separator between the group, and the amount of that group, \[-\]

# Returns

counts

: String of counts of the found groups only \[-\]

# Notes

# Examples

```pycon
>>> group_assignment_to_str({1: 5, 4: 1, 9: 5, 10: 1, 81: 1})
'1:5,4:1,9:5,10:1,81:1'
```

functions.name: thermo.group_contribution.group_contribution_base.str_group_assignment_to_dict
documentation:
Take a group contribution string, and turn it into a dictionary.

# Parameters

counts

: String of counts of the found groups only \[-\]

pair_separator

: The separator between pairs of group assignments, \[-\]

key_val_separator

: The separator between the group, and the amount of that group, \[-\]

# Returns

counts

: Dictionaty of integer counts of the found groups only, indexed by
  the `group` \[-\]

# Notes

# Examples

```pycon
>>> str_group_assignment_to_dict('1:5,4:1,9:5,10:1,81:1')
{1: 5, 4: 1, 9: 5, 10: 1, 81: 1}
>>> str_group_assignment_to_dict('')
{}
```

functions.name: thermo.group_contribution.group_contribution_base.smarts_fragment_priority
documentation:
Fragments a molecule into a set of unique groups and counts as
specified by the `catalog`, which is a list of objects containing
the attributes `smarts`, `group`, and `priority`.

The molecule can either be an rdkit
molecule object, or a smiles string which will be parsed by rdkit.
Returns a dictionary of groups and their counts according to the
priorities of the catalog provided, as well as some other information

Parameters
----------
catalog : list
    List of objects, [-]
rdkitmol : mol, optional
    Molecule as rdkit object, [-]
smi : str, optional
    Smiles string representing a chemical, [-]

Returns
-------
counts : dict
    Dictionaty of integer counts of the found groups only, indexed by
    the `group` [-]
group_assignments : dict[`group`: [(matched_atoms_0,), (matched_atoms_0,)]]
    A dictionary of which atoms were included in each of the groups identified, [-]
matched_atoms : set
    A set of all of the atoms which were matched, [-]
success : bool
    Whether or not molecule was fully fragmented, [-]
status : str
    A string holding an explanation of why the molecule failed to be
    fragmented, if it fails; 'OK' if it suceeds.

Notes
-----
Raises an exception if rdkit is not installed, or `smi` or `rdkitmol` is
not defined.

Examples
--------

functions.name: thermo.group_contribution.group_contribution_base.smarts_fragment
documentation:
Fragments a molecule into a set of unique groups and counts as
specified by the `catalog`. The molecule can either be an rdkit
molecule object, or a smiles string which will be parsed by rdkit.
Returns a dictionary of groups and their counts according to the
indexes of the catalog provided.

# Parameters

catalog

: Dictionary indexed by keys pointing to smarts strings, \[-\]

rdkitmol

: Molecule as rdkit object, \[-\]

smi

: Smiles string representing a chemical, \[-\]

# Returns

counts

: Dictionaty of integer counts of the found groups only, indexed by
  the same keys used by the catalog \[-\]

success

: Whether or not molecule was fully and uniquely fragmented, \[-\]

status

: A string holding an explanation of why the molecule failed to be
  fragmented, if it fails; 'OK' if it suceeds.

# Notes

Raises an exception if rdkit is not installed, or `smi` or `rdkitmol` is
not defined.

# Examples

Acetone:

```pycon
>>> smarts_fragment(catalog=J_BIGGS_JOBACK_SMARTS_id_dict, smi='CC(=O)C') # doctest:+SKIP
({1: 2, 24: 1}, True, 'OK')
```

Sodium sulfate, (Na2O4S):

```pycon
>>> smarts_fragment(catalog=J_BIGGS_JOBACK_SMARTS_id_dict, smi='[O-]S(=O)(=O)[O-].[Na+].[Na+]') # doctest:+SKIP
({29: 4}, False, 'Did not match all atoms present')
```

Propionic anhydride (C6H10O3):

```pycon
>>> smarts_fragment(catalog=J_BIGGS_JOBACK_SMARTS_id_dict, smi='CCC(=O)OC(=O)CC') # doctest:+SKIP
({1: 2, 2: 2, 28: 2}, False, 'Matched some atoms repeatedly: [4]')
```

This module contains implementations of {obj}`TDependentProperty <thermo.utils.TDependentProperty>`
representing liquid permittivity. A variety of estimation
and data methods are available as included in the `chemicals` library.

For reporting bugs, adding feature requests, or submitting pull requests,
please use the [GitHub issue tracker](https://github.com/CalebBell/thermo/).

```{contents}
:local: true
```

# Pure Liquid Permittivity

```
.. autoclass:: PermittivityLiquid
    :members: calculate, test_method_validity,
              name, property_max, property_min,
              units, Tmin, Tmax, ranked_methods
    :undoc-members:
    :show-inheritance:
    :exclude-members:
```

```
.. autodata:: permittivity_methods
```

variables.name: thermo.permittivity.permittivity_methods
documentation:
Holds all methods available for the {obj}`PermittivityLiquid` class, for use in
iterating over them.

classes.name: thermo.permittivity.PermittivityLiquid

methods.name: thermo.permittivity.PermittivityLiquid._method_indexes
documentation:
Returns a dictionary of method: index for all methods
that use data files to retrieve constants. The use of this function
ensures the data files are not loaded until they are needed.

methods.name: thermo.permittivity.PermittivityLiquid.load_all_methods
documentation:
Method which picks out coefficients for the specified chemical
from the various dictionaries and DataFrames storing it. All data is
stored as attributes. This method also sets {obj}`Tmin`, {obj}`Tmax`,
and {obj}`all_methods` as a set of methods for which the data exists for.

Called on initialization only. See the source code for the variables at
which the coefficients are stored. The coefficients can safely be
altered once the class is initialized. This method can be called again
to reset the parameters.

methods.name: thermo.permittivity.PermittivityLiquid.calculate
documentation:
Method to calculate permittivity of a liquid at temperature `T`
with a given method.

This method has no exception handling; see {obj}`T_dependent_property <thermo.utils.TDependentProperty.T_dependent_property>`
for that.

# Parameters

T

: Temperature at which to calculate relative permittivity, \[K\]

method

: Name of the method to use

# Returns

epsilon

: Relative permittivity of the liquid at T, \[-\]

methods.name: thermo.permittivity.PermittivityLiquid.test_method_validity
documentation:
Method to check the validity of a method. Follows the given
ranges for all coefficient-based methods. For tabular data,
extrapolation outside of the range is used if
{obj}`tabular_extrapolation_permitted` is set; if it is, the
extrapolation is considered valid for all temperatures.

It is not guaranteed that a method will work or give an accurate
prediction simply because this method considers the method valid.

# Parameters

T

: Temperature at which to test the method, \[K\]

method

: Name of the method to test

# Returns

validity

: Whether or not a method is valid

class_variables.name: thermo.permittivity.PermittivityLiquid.interpolation_T
documentation: No interpolation transformation by default.

class_variables.name: thermo.permittivity.PermittivityLiquid.interpolation_property
documentation: No interpolation transformation by default.

class_variables.name: thermo.permittivity.PermittivityLiquid.interpolation_property_inv
documentation: No interpolation transformation by default.

class_variables.name: thermo.permittivity.PermittivityLiquid.tabular_extrapolation_permitted
documentation: Allow tabular extrapolation by default.

class_variables.name: thermo.permittivity.PermittivityLiquid.property_min
documentation:
Relative permittivity must always be larger than 1; nothing is better
than a vacuum.

class_variables.name: thermo.permittivity.PermittivityLiquid.property_max
documentation: Maximum valid of permittivity; highest in the data available is ~240.

class_variables.name: thermo.permittivity.PermittivityLiquid.ranked_methods
documentation: Default rankings of the available methods.

class_variables.name: thermo.permittivity.PermittivityLiquid._fit_force_n
documentation:
Dictionary containing method: fit_n, for use in methods which should
only ever be fit to a specific `n` value

Class for dealing with liquid permittivity as a function of temperature.
Consists of one temperature-dependent simple expression, one constant
value source, and IAPWS.

# Parameters

CASRN

: The CAS number of the chemical

load_data

: If False, do not load property coefficients from data sources in files
  \[-\]

extrapolation

: None to not extrapolate; see
  {obj}`TDependentProperty <thermo.utils.TDependentProperty>`
  for a full list of all options, \[-\]

method

: If specified, use this method by default and do not use the ranked
  sorting; an exception is raised if this is not a valid method for the
  provided inputs, \[-\]

# Notes

To iterate over all methods, use the list stored in
{obj}`permittivity_methods`.

**CRC**:

: Simple polynomials for calculating permittivity over a specified
  temperature range only. The full expression is:

  $$
  \epsilon_r = A + BT + CT^2 + DT^3
  $$

  Not all chemicals use all terms; in fact, few do. Data is available
  for 759 liquids, from [^footnote-1].

**CRC_CONSTANT**:

: Constant permittivity values at specified temperatures only.
  Data is from [^footnote-1], and is available for 1303 liquids.

**IAPWS**:

: The IAPWS model for water permittivity as a liquid.

# References

[^footnote-1]: Haynes, W.M., Thomas J. Bruno, and David R. Lide. CRC Handbook of
    Chemistry and Physics. \[Boca Raton, FL\]: CRC press, 2014.

methods.name: thermo.chemical.Chemical.draw_2d
documentation:
Interface for drawing a 2D image of the molecule.
Requires an HTML5 browser, and the libraries RDKit and
IPython. An exception is raised if either of these libraries is
absent.

# Parameters

width

: Number of pixels wide for the view

height

: Number of pixels tall for the view

Hs

: Whether or not to show hydrogen

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Chemical('decane').draw_2d() # doctest: +ELLIPSIS
<PIL.PngImagePlugin.PngImageFile image mode=RGB size=300x300 at 0x...>
```

methods.name: thermo.chemical.Chemical.draw_3d
documentation:
Interface for drawing an interactive 3D view of the molecule.
Requires an HTML5 browser, and the libraries RDKit, pymol3D, and
IPython. An exception is raised if all three of these libraries are
not installed.

# Parameters

width

: Number of pixels wide for the view, \[pixels\]

height

: Number of pixels tall for the view, \[pixels\]

style

: One of 'stick', 'line', 'cross', or 'sphere', \[-\]

Hs

: Whether or not to show hydrogen, \[-\]

atom_labels

: Whether or not to label the atoms, \[-\]

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Chemical('cubane').draw_3d()
None
```

methods.name: thermo.chemical.Chemical.eos
documentation:
Equation of state object held by the chemical; used to calculate
excess thermodynamic quantities, and also provides a vapor pressure
curve, enthalpy of vaporization curve, fugacity, thermodynamic partial
derivatives, and more; see {obj}`thermo.eos` for a full listing.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Chemical('methane').eos.V_g
0.0244
```

methods.name: thermo.chemical.Chemical.Um
documentation:
Internal energy of the chemical at its current temperature and
pressure, in units of \[J/mol\].

This property requires that {obj}`thermo.chemical.set_thermo` ran
successfully to be accurate.
It also depends on the molar volume of the chemical at its current
conditions.

methods.name: thermo.chemical.Chemical.U
documentation:
Internal energy of the chemical at its current temperature and
pressure, in units of \[J/kg\].

This property requires that {obj}`thermo.chemical.set_thermo` ran
successfully to be accurate.
It also depends on the molar volume of the chemical at its current
conditions.

methods.name: thermo.chemical.Chemical.Am
documentation:
Helmholtz energy of the chemical at its current temperature and
pressure, in units of \[J/mol\].

This property requires that {obj}`thermo.chemical.set_thermo` ran
successfully to be accurate.
It also depends on the molar volume of the chemical at its current
conditions.

methods.name: thermo.chemical.Chemical.A
documentation:
Helmholtz energy of the chemical at its current temperature and
pressure, in units of \[J/kg\].

This property requires that {obj}`thermo.chemical.set_thermo` ran
successfully to be accurate.
It also depends on the molar volume of the chemical at its current
conditions.

documentation:
Charge of a chemical, computed with RDKit from a chemical's SMILES.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Chemical('sodium ion').charge
1
```
## Temperature independent properties - calculate lazily

methods.name: thermo.chemical.Chemical.rings
documentation:
Number of rings in a chemical, computed with RDKit from a
chemical's SMILES. If RDKit is not available, holds None.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Chemical('Paclitaxel').rings
7
```

methods.name: thermo.chemical.Chemical.aromatic_rings
documentation:
Number of aromatic rings in a chemical, computed with RDKit from a

methods.name: thermo.chemical.Chemical.rdkitmol
documentation:
RDKit object of the chemical, without hydrogen. If RDKit is not

Hill formula of a compound. For a description of the Hill system,
see {obj}`chemicals.elements.atoms_to_Hill`.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Chemical('furfuryl alcohol').Hill
'C5H6O2'
```

methods.name: thermo.chemical.Chemical.atom_fractions
documentation:
Dictionary of atom:fractional occurence of the elements in a
chemical. Useful when performing element balances. For mass-fraction
occurences, see {obj}`mass_fractions`.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Chemical('Ammonium aluminium sulfate').atom_fractions
{'Al': 0.0625, 'H': 0.25, 'N': 0.0625, 'O': 0.5, 'S': 0.125}
```

methods.name: thermo.chemical.Chemical.mass_fractions

Dictionary of atom:mass-weighted fractional occurence of elements.
Useful when performing mass balances. For atom-fraction occurences, see
{obj}`atom_fractions`.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Chemical('water').mass_fractions
{'H': 0.11189834407236524, 'O': 0.8881016559276347}
```

methods.name: thermo.chemical.Chemical.legal_status

methods.name: thermo.chemical.Chemical.R_specific

Specific gas constant, in units of \[J/kg/K\].
