methods.name: thermo.chemical.Chemical.Psat
documentation:
Vapor pressure of the chemical at its current temperature, in units
of \[Pa\]. For calculation of this property at other temperatures,
or specifying manually the method used to calculate it, and more - see
the object oriented interface {obj}`thermo.vapor_pressure.VaporPressure`;
each Chemical instance creates one to actually perform the calculations.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Chemical('water', T=320).Psat
10545.
>>> Chemical('water').VaporPressure.T_dependent_property(320)
10545.
>>> sorted(Chemical('water').VaporPressure.all_methods)
['AMBROSE_WALTON', 'ANTOINE_POLING', 'ANTOINE_WEBBOOK', 'BOILING_CRITICAL', 'COOLPROP', 'DIPPR_PERRY_8E', 'EDALAT', 'EOS', 'IAPWS', 'LEE_KESLER_PSAT', 'SANJARI', 'VDI_PPDS', 'WAGNER_MCGARRY']
```
## One phase properties - calculate lazily

methods.name: thermo.chemical.Chemical.Hvapm
documentation:
Enthalpy of vaporization of the chemical at its current temperature,
in units of \[J/mol\]. For calculation of this property at other
temperatures, or specifying manually the method used to calculate it,
and more - see the object oriented interface
{obj}`thermo.phase_change.EnthalpyVaporization`; each Chemical instance
creates one to actually perform the calculations.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Chemical('water', T=320).Hvapm
43048.
>>> Chemical('water').EnthalpyVaporization.T_dependent_property(320)
43048.
>>> sorted(Chemical('water').EnthalpyVaporization.all_methods)
['ALIBAKHSHI', 'CHEN', 'CLAPEYRON', 'COOLPROP', 'CRC_HVAP_298', 'CRC_HVAP_TB', 'DIPPR_PERRY_8E', 'LIU', 'MORGAN_KOBAYASHI', 'PITZER', 'RIEDEL', 'SIVARAMAN_MAGEE_KOBAYASHI', 'VDI_PPDS', 'VELASCO', 'VETERE']
```

methods.name: thermo.chemical.Chemical.Hvap
documentation:
Enthalpy of vaporization of the chemical at its current temperature,
in units of \[J/kg\].

This property uses the object-oriented interface
{obj}`thermo.phase_change.EnthalpyVaporization`, but converts its
results from molar to mass units.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Chemical('water', T=320).Hvap
2389540.
```

methods.name: thermo.chemical.Chemical.Cpsm
documentation:
Solid-phase heat capacity of the chemical at its current temperature,
in units of \[J/mol/K\]. For calculation of this property at other
temperatures, or specifying manually the method used to calculate it,
and more - see the object oriented interface
{obj}`thermo.heat_capacity.HeatCapacitySolid`; each Chemical instance
creates one to actually perform the calculations.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Chemical('palladium').Cpsm
24.9
>>> Chemical('palladium').HeatCapacitySolid.T_dependent_property(320)
25.
>>> sorted(Chemical('palladium').HeatCapacitySolid.all_methods)
['CRCSTD', 'LASTOVKA_S', 'PERRY151']
```

methods.name: thermo.chemical.Chemical.Cplm
documentation:
Liquid-phase heat capacity of the chemical at its current temperature,
in units of \[J/mol/K\]. For calculation of this property at other
temperatures, or specifying manually the method used to calculate it,
and more - see the object oriented interface
{obj}`thermo.heat_capacity.HeatCapacityLiquid`; each Chemical instance
creates one to actually perform the calculations.

# Notes

Some methods give heat capacity along the saturation line, some at
1 atm but only up to the normal boiling point, and some give heat
capacity at 1 atm up to the normal boiling point and then along the
saturation line. Real-liquid heat capacity is pressure dependent, but
this interface is not.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Chemical('water').Cplm
75.3
>>> Chemical('water').HeatCapacityLiquid.T_dependent_property(320)
75.2
>>> Chemical('water').HeatCapacityLiquid.T_dependent_property_integral(300, 320)
1505.
```

methods.name: thermo.chemical.Chemical.Cpgm
documentation:
Gas-phase ideal gas heat capacity of the chemical at its current
temperature, in units of \[J/mol/K\]. For calculation of this property at
other temperatures, or specifying manually the method used to calculate
it, and more - see the object oriented interface
{obj}`thermo.heat_capacity.HeatCapacityGas`; each Chemical instance
creates one to actually perform the calculations.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Chemical('water').Cpgm
33.58
>>> Chemical('water').HeatCapacityGas.T_dependent_property(320)
33.67
>>> Chemical('water').HeatCapacityGas.T_dependent_property_integral(300, 320)
672.
```

methods.name: thermo.chemical.Chemical.Cps
documentation:
Solid-phase heat capacity of the chemical at its current temperature,
in units of \[J/kg/K\]. For calculation of this property at other
temperatures, or specifying manually the method used to calculate it,
and more - see the object oriented interface
{obj}`thermo.heat_capacity.HeatCapacitySolid`; each Chemical instance
creates one to actually perform the calculations. Note that that
interface provides output in molar units.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Chemical('palladium', T=400).Cps
241.
>>> Pd = Chemical('palladium', T=400)
>>> Cpsms = [Pd.HeatCapacitySolid.T_dependent_property(T) for T in np.linspace(300, 500, 2)]
>>> [property_molar_to_mass(Cps, Pd.MW) for Cps in Cpsms]
[234., 248.]
```

methods.name: thermo.chemical.Chemical.Cpl
documentation:
Liquid-phase heat capacity of the chemical at its current temperature,
in units of \[J/kg/K\]. For calculation of this property at other
temperatures, or specifying manually the method used to calculate it,
and more - see the object oriented interface
{obj}`thermo.heat_capacity.HeatCapacityLiquid`; each Chemical instance
creates one to actually perform the calculations. Note that that
interface provides output in molar units.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Chemical('water', T=320).Cpl
4177.
```

Ideal entropy change of water from 280 K to 340 K, output converted
back to mass-based units of J/kg/K.

```pycon
>>> dSm = Chemical('water').HeatCapacityLiquid.T_dependent_property_integral_over_T(280, 340)
>>> property_molar_to_mass(dSm, Chemical('water').MW)
812.
```

methods.name: thermo.chemical.Chemical.Cpg
documentation:
Gas-phase heat capacity of the chemical at its current temperature,
in units of \[J/kg/K\]. For calculation of this property at other
temperatures, or specifying manually the method used to calculate it,
and more - see the object oriented interface
{obj}`thermo.heat_capacity.HeatCapacityGas`; each Chemical instance
creates one to actually perform the calculations. Note that that
interface provides output in molar units.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> w = Chemical('water', T=520)
>>> w.Cpg
1967.
```

methods.name: thermo.chemical.Chemical.Cvgm
documentation:
Gas-phase ideal-gas contant-volume heat capacity of the chemical at
its current temperature, in units of \[J/mol/K\]. Subtracts R from
the ideal-gas heat capacity; does not include pressure-compensation
from an equation of state.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> w = Chemical('water', T=520)
>>> w.Cvgm
27.1
```

methods.name: thermo.chemical.Chemical.Cvg
documentation:
Gas-phase ideal-gas contant-volume heat capacity of the chemical at
its current temperature, in units of \[J/kg/K\]. Subtracts R from
the ideal-gas heat capacity; does not include pressure-compensation
from an equation of state.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> w = Chemical('water', T=520)
>>> w.Cvg
1506.
```

methods.name: thermo.chemical.Chemical.isentropic_exponent
documentation:
Gas-phase ideal-gas isentropic exponent of the chemical at its
current temperature, \[dimensionless\]. Does not include
pressure-compensation from an equation of state.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Chemical('hydrogen').isentropic_exponent
1.40
```

methods.name: thermo.chemical.Chemical.Vms
documentation:
Solid-phase molar volume of the chemical at its current
temperature, in units of  \[m^3/mol\]. For calculation of this property at
other temperatures, or specifying manually the method used to calculate
it, and more - see the object oriented interface
{obj}`thermo.volume.VolumeSolid`; each Chemical instance
creates one to actually perform the calculations.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Chemical('iron').Vms
7.09e-06
```

methods.name: thermo.chemical.Chemical.Vml
documentation:
Liquid-phase molar volume of the chemical at its current
temperature and pressure, in units of \[m^3/mol\]. For calculation of this
property at other temperatures or pressures, or specifying manually the
method used to calculate it, and more - see the object oriented interface
{obj}`thermo.volume.VolumeLiquid`; each Chemical instance
creates one to actually perform the calculations.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Chemical('cyclobutane', T=225).Vml
7.42395423425395e-05
```

methods.name: thermo.chemical.Chemical.Vmg
documentation:
Gas-phase molar volume of the chemical at its current
temperature and pressure, in units of \[m^3/mol\]. For calculation of this
property at other temperatures or pressures, or specifying manually the
method used to calculate it, and more - see the object oriented interface
{obj}`thermo.volume.VolumeGas`; each Chemical instance
creates one to actually perform the calculations.

# Examples

Estimate the molar volume of the core of the sun, at 15 million K and
26.5 PetaPascals, assuming pure helium (actually 68% helium):

```pycon
>>> Chemical('helium', T=15E6, P=26.5E15).Vmg
4.7e-07
```

methods.name: thermo.chemical.Chemical.Vmg_ideal
documentation:
Gas-phase molar volume of the chemical at its current
temperature and pressure calculated with the ideal-gas law,
in units of \[m^3/mol\].

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Chemical('helium', T=300.0, P=1e5).Vmg_ideal
0.0249433878544
```

methods.name: thermo.chemical.Chemical.rhos
documentation:
Solid-phase mass density of the chemical at its current temperature,
in units of \[kg/m^3\]. For calculation of this property at
other temperatures, or specifying manually the method used
to calculate it, and more - see the object oriented interface
{obj}`thermo.volume.VolumeSolid`; each Chemical instance
creates one to actually perform the calculations. Note that that
interface provides output in molar units.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Chemical('iron').rhos
7870.
```

methods.name: thermo.chemical.Chemical.rhol
documentation:
Liquid-phase mass density of the chemical at its current
temperature and pressure, in units of \[kg/m^3\]. For calculation of this
property at other temperatures and pressures, or specifying manually
the method used to calculate it, and more - see the object oriented
interface {obj}`thermo.volume.VolumeLiquid`; each Chemical instance
creates one to actually perform the calculations. Note that that
interface provides output in molar units.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Chemical('o-xylene', T=297).rhol
876.9946785618097
```

methods.name: thermo.chemical.Chemical.rhog
documentation:
Gas-phase mass density of the chemical at its current temperature
and pressure, in units of \[kg/m^3\]. For calculation of this property at
other temperatures or pressures, or specifying manually the method used
to calculate it, and more - see the object oriented interface
{obj}`thermo.volume.VolumeGas`; each Chemical instance
creates one to actually perform the calculations. Note that that
interface provides output in molar units.

# Examples

Estimate the density of the core of the sun, at 15 million K and
26.5 PetaPascals, assuming pure helium (actually 68% helium):

```pycon
>>> Chemical('helium', T=15E6, P=26.5E15).rhog
8519.
```

Compared to a result on
[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_core) of 150000
kg/m^3, the fundamental equation of state performs poorly.

```pycon
>>> He = Chemical('helium', T=15E6, P=26.5E15)
>>> He.VolumeGas.method_P = 'IDEAL'
>>> He.rhog
850477.
```

The ideal-gas law performs somewhat better, but vastly overshoots
the density prediction.

methods.name: thermo.chemical.Chemical.rhosm
documentation:
Molar density of the chemical in the solid phase at the
current temperature and pressure, in units of \[mol/m^3\].

Utilizes the object oriented interface and
{obj}`thermo.volume.VolumeSolid` to perform the actual calculation of
molar volume.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Chemical('palladium').rhosm
112760.
```

methods.name: thermo.chemical.Chemical.rholm
documentation:
Molar density of the chemical in the liquid phase at the
current temperature and pressure, in units of \[mol/m^3\].

Utilizes the object oriented interface and
{obj}`thermo.volume.VolumeLiquid` to perform the actual calculation of
molar volume.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Chemical('nitrogen', T=70).rholm
29937.
```

methods.name: thermo.chemical.Chemical.rhogm
documentation:
Molar density of the chemical in the gas phase at the
current temperature and pressure, in units of \[mol/m^3\].

Utilizes the object oriented interface and
{obj}`thermo.volume.VolumeGas` to perform the actual calculation of
molar volume.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Chemical('tungsten hexafluoride').rhogm
42.
```

methods.name: thermo.chemical.Chemical.Zs
documentation:
Compressibility factor of the chemical in the solid phase at the
current temperature and pressure, \[dimensionless\].

Utilizes the object oriented interface and
{obj}`thermo.volume.VolumeSolid` to perform the actual calculation of
molar volume.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Chemical('palladium').Z
0.000362
```

methods.name: thermo.chemical.Chemical.Zl
documentation:
Compressibility factor of the chemical in the liquid phase at the
current temperature and pressure, \[dimensionless\].

Utilizes the object oriented interface and
{obj}`thermo.volume.VolumeLiquid` to perform the actual calculation of
molar volume.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Chemical('water').Zl
0.00073
```

methods.name: thermo.chemical.Chemical.Zg
documentation:
Compressibility factor of the chemical in the gas phase at the
current temperature and pressure, \[dimensionless\].

Utilizes the object oriented interface and
{obj}`thermo.volume.VolumeGas` to perform the actual calculation of
molar volume.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Chemical('sulfur hexafluoride', T=700, P=1E9).Zg
11.14
```

methods.name: thermo.chemical.Chemical.SGs
documentation:
Specific gravity of the solid phase of the chemical at the
specified temperature and pressure, \[dimensionless\].
The reference condition is water at 4 °C and 1 atm
(rho=999.017 kg/m^3). The SG varries with temperature and pressure
but only very slightly.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Chemical('iron').SGs
7.8
```

methods.name: thermo.chemical.Chemical.SGl
documentation:
Specific gravity of the liquid phase of the chemical at the
specified temperature and pressure, \[dimensionless\].
The reference condition is water at 4 °C and 1 atm
(rho=999.017 kg/m^3). For liquids, SG is defined that the reference
chemical's T and P are fixed, but the chemical itself varies with
the specified T and P.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Chemical('water', T=365).SGl
0.965
```

methods.name: thermo.chemical.Chemical.SGg
documentation:
Specific gravity of the gas phase of the chemical, \[dimensionless\].
The reference condition is air at 15.6 °C (60 °F) and 1 atm
(rho=1.223 kg/m^3). The definition for gases uses the compressibility
factor of the reference gas and the chemical both at the reference
conditions, not the conditions of the chemical.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Chemical('argon').SGg
1.3
```

methods.name: thermo.chemical.Chemical.API
documentation:
API gravity of the liquid phase of the chemical, \[degrees\].
The reference condition is water at 15.6 °C (60 °F) and 1 atm
(rho=999.016 kg/m^3, standardized).

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Chemical('water').API
10.
```

methods.name: thermo.chemical.Chemical.Bvirial
documentation:
Second virial coefficient of the gas phase of the chemical at its
current temperature and pressure, in units of \[mol/m^3\].

This property uses the object-oriented interface
{obj}`thermo.volume.VolumeGas`, converting its result with
{obj}`thermo.utils.B_from_Z`.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Chemical('water', T=500, P=1e5).Bvirial
-0.00017
```

methods.name: thermo.chemical.Chemical.isobaric_expansion_l
documentation:
Isobaric (constant-pressure) expansion of the liquid phase of the
chemical at its current temperature and pressure, in units of \[1/K\].

$$
\beta = \frac{1}{V}\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T} \right)_P
$$

Utilizes the temperature-derivative method of
{obj}`thermo.volume.VolumeLiquid` to perform the actual calculation.
The derivatives are all numerical.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Chemical('dodecane', T=400).isobaric_expansion_l
0.00116
```

methods.name: thermo.chemical.Chemical.isobaric_expansion_g
documentation:
Isobaric (constant-pressure) expansion of the gas phase of the
chemical at its current temperature and pressure, in units of \[1/K\].

$$
\beta = \frac{1}{V}\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T} \right)_P
$$

Utilizes the temperature-derivative method of
{obj}`thermo.VolumeGas` to perform the actual calculation.
The derivatives are all numerical.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Chemical('Hexachlorobenzene', T=900).isobaric_expansion_g
0.00115
```

methods.name: thermo.chemical.Chemical.mul
documentation:
Viscosity of the chemical in the liquid phase at its current
temperature and pressure, in units of \[Pa\*s\].

For calculation of this property at other temperatures and pressures,
or specifying manually the method used to calculate it, and more - see
the object oriented interface
{obj}`thermo.viscosity.ViscosityLiquid`; each Chemical instance
creates one to actually perform the calculations.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Chemical('water', T=320).mul
0.000576
```

methods.name: thermo.chemical.Chemical.mug
documentation:
Viscosity of the chemical in the gas phase at its current
temperature and pressure, in units of \[Pa\*s\].

For calculation of this property at other temperatures and pressures,
or specifying manually the method used to calculate it, and more - see
the object oriented interface
{obj}`thermo.viscosity.ViscosityGas`; each Chemical instance
creates one to actually perform the calculations.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Chemical('water', T=320, P=100).mug
1.04e-05
```

methods.name: thermo.chemical.Chemical.kl
documentation:
Thermal conductivity of the chemical in the liquid phase at its
current temperature and pressure, in units of \[W/m/K\].

For calculation of this property at other temperatures and pressures,
or specifying manually the method used to calculate it, and more - see
the object oriented interface
{obj}`thermo.thermal_conductivity.ThermalConductivityLiquid`; each
Chemical instance creates one to actually perform the calculations.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Chemical('water', T=320).kl
0.63
```

methods.name: thermo.chemical.Chemical.kg
documentation:
Thermal conductivity of the chemical in the gas phase at its
current temperature and pressure, in units of \[W/m/K\].

For calculation of this property at other temperatures and pressures,
or specifying manually the method used to calculate it, and more - see
the object oriented interface
{obj}`thermo.thermal_conductivity.ThermalConductivityGas`; each
Chemical instance creates one to actually perform the calculations.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Chemical('water', T=320, P=100).kg
0.02
```

methods.name: thermo.chemical.Chemical.ks
documentation:
Thermal conductivity of the chemical in the solid phase at its
current temperature and pressure, in units of \[W/m/K\].

For calculation of this property at other temperatures,
or specifying manually the method used to calculate it, and more - see
the object oriented interface
{obj}`thermo.thermal_conductivity.ThermalConductivitySolid`; each
Chemical instance creates one to actually perform the calculations.

methods.name: thermo.chemical.Chemical.sigma
documentation:
Surface tension of the chemical at its current temperature, in
units of \[N/m\].

For calculation of this property at other temperatures,
or specifying manually the method used to calculate it, and more - see
the object oriented interface {obj}`thermo.interface.SurfaceTension`;
each Chemical instance creates one to actually perform the calculations.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Chemical('water', T=320).sigma
0.068
>>> Chemical('water', T=320).SurfaceTension.solve_property(0.05)
417.2
```

methods.name: thermo.chemical.Chemical.permittivity
documentation:
Relative permittivity (dielectric constant) of the chemical at its
current temperature, \[dimensionless\].

For calculation of this property at other temperatures,
or specifying manually the method used to calculate it, and more - see
the object oriented interface {obj}`thermo.permittivity.PermittivityLiquid`;
each Chemical instance creates one to actually perform the calculations.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Chemical('toluene', T=250).permittivity
2.497
```

methods.name: thermo.chemical.Chemical.absolute_permittivity
documentation:
Absolute permittivity of the chemical at its current temperature,
in units of \[farad/meter\]. Those units are equivalent to
ampere^2\*second^4/kg/m^3.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Chemical('water', T=293.15).absolute_permittivity
7.1e-10
```

methods.name: thermo.chemical.Chemical.JTl
documentation:
Joule Thomson coefficient of the chemical in the liquid phase at
its current temperature and pressure, in units of \[K/Pa\].

$$
\mu_{JT} = \left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_H = \frac{1}{C_p} \left[T \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P - V\right] = \frac{V}{C_p}\left(\beta T-1\right)
$$

Utilizes the temperature-derivative method of
{obj}`thermo.volume.VolumeLiquid` and the temperature-dependent heat
capacity method {obj}`thermo.heat_capacity.HeatCapacityLiquid` to
obtain the properties required for the actual calculation.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Chemical('dodecane', T=400).JTl
-3.1e-07
```

methods.name: thermo.chemical.Chemical.JTg
documentation:
Joule Thomson coefficient of the chemical in the gas phase at
its current temperature and pressure, in units of \[K/Pa\].

$$
\mu_{JT} = \left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_H = \frac{1}{C_p} \left[T \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P - V\right] = \frac{V}{C_p}\left(\beta T-1\right)
$$

Utilizes the temperature-derivative method of
{obj}`thermo.volume.VolumeGas` and the temperature-dependent heat
capacity method {obj}`thermo.heat_capacity.HeatCapacityGas` to
obtain the properties required for the actual calculation.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Chemical('dodecane', T=400, P=1000).JTg
5.4e-05
```

methods.name: thermo.chemical.Chemical.nul
documentation:
Kinematic viscosity of the liquid phase of the chemical at its
current temperature and pressure, in units of \[m^2/s\].

$$
\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho}
$$

Utilizes the temperature and pressure dependent object oriented
interfaces {obj}`thermo.volume.VolumeLiquid`,
{obj}`thermo.viscosity.ViscosityLiquid`  to calculate the
actual properties.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Chemical('methane', T=110).nul
2.858e-07
```

methods.name: thermo.chemical.Chemical.nug
documentation:
Kinematic viscosity of the gas phase of the chemical at its
current temperature and pressure, in units of \[m^2/s\].

$$
\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho}
$$

Utilizes the temperature and pressure dependent object oriented
interfaces {obj}`thermo.volume.VolumeGas`,
{obj}`thermo.viscosity.ViscosityGas`  to calculate the
actual properties.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Chemical('methane', T=115).nug
2.5e-06
```

methods.name: thermo.chemical.Chemical.alphal
documentation:
Thermal diffusivity of the liquid phase of the chemical at its
current temperature and pressure, in units of \[m^2/s\].

$$
\alpha = \frac{k}{\rho Cp}
$$

Utilizes the temperature and pressure dependent object oriented
interfaces {obj}`thermo.volume.VolumeLiquid`,
{obj}`thermo.thermal_conductivity.ThermalConductivityLiquid`,
and {obj}`thermo.heat_capacity.HeatCapacityLiquid` to calculate the
actual properties.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Chemical('nitrogen', T=70).alphal
9.4e-08
```

methods.name: thermo.chemical.Chemical.alphag
documentation:
Thermal diffusivity of the gas phase of the chemical at its
current temperature and pressure, in units of \[m^2/s\].

$$
\alpha = \frac{k}{\rho Cp}
$$

Utilizes the temperature and pressure dependent object oriented
interfaces {obj}`thermo.volume.VolumeGas`,
{obj}`thermo.thermal_conductivity.ThermalConductivityGas`,
and {obj}`thermo.heat_capacity.HeatCapacityGas` to calculate the
actual properties.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Chemical('ammonia').alphag
1.69e-05
```

methods.name: thermo.chemical.Chemical.Prl
documentation:
Prandtl number of the liquid phase of the chemical at its
current temperature and pressure, \[dimensionless\].

$$
Pr = \frac{C_p \mu}{k}
$$

Utilizes the temperature and pressure dependent object oriented
interfaces {obj}`thermo.viscosity.ViscosityLiquid`,
{obj}`thermo.thermal_conductivity.ThermalConductivityLiquid`,
and {obj}`thermo.heat_capacity.HeatCapacityLiquid` to calculate the
actual properties.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Chemical('nitrogen', T=70).Prl
2.78
```

methods.name: thermo.chemical.Chemical.Prg
documentation:
Prandtl number of the gas phase of the chemical at its
current temperature and pressure, \[dimensionless\].

$$
Pr = \frac{C_p \mu}{k}
$$

Utilizes the temperature and pressure dependent object oriented
interfaces {obj}`thermo.viscosity.ViscosityGas`,
{obj}`thermo.thermal_conductivity.ThermalConductivityGas`,
and {obj}`thermo.heat_capacity.HeatCapacityGas` to calculate the
actual properties.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Chemical('NH3').Prg
0.84
```

methods.name: thermo.chemical.Chemical.solubility_parameter
documentation:
Solubility parameter of the chemical at its
current temperature and pressure, in units of \[Pa^0.5\].

$$
\delta = \sqrt{\frac{\Delta H_{vap} - RT}{V_m}}
$$

Calculated based on enthalpy of vaporization and molar volume.
Normally calculated at STP. For uses of this property, see
{obj}`thermo.solubility.solubility_parameter`.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Chemical('NH3', T=200).solubility_parameter
31712.
```

methods.name: thermo.chemical.Chemical.Parachor
documentation:
Parachor of the chemical at its
current temperature and pressure, in units of \[N^0.25\*m^2.75/mol\].

$$
P = \frac{\sigma^{0.25} MW}{\rho_L - \rho_V}
$$

Calculated based on surface tension, density of the liquid
phase, and molecular weight. For uses of this property, see
{obj}`thermo.utils.Parachor`.

The gas density is calculated using the ideal-gas law.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Chemical('octane').Parachor
6.2e-05
```

methods.name: thermo.chemical.Chemical.Cp
documentation:
Mass heat capacity of the chemical at its current phase and
temperature, in units of \[J/kg/K\].

Utilizes the object oriented interfaces
{obj}`thermo.heat_capacity.HeatCapacitySolid`,
{obj}`thermo.heat_capacity.HeatCapacityLiquid`,
and {obj}`thermo.heat_capacity.HeatCapacityGas` to perform the
actual calculation of each property. Note that those interfaces provide
output in molar units (J/mol/K).

# Examples

```pycon
>>> w = Chemical('water')
>>> w.Cp, w.phase
(4180., 'l')
>>> Chemical('palladium').Cp
234.
```
## Single-phase properties

methods.name: thermo.chemical.Chemical.Cpm
documentation:
Molar heat capacity of the chemical at its current phase and
temperature, in units of \[J/mol/K\].

Utilizes the object oriented interfaces
{obj}`thermo.heat_capacity.HeatCapacitySolid`,
{obj}`thermo.heat_capacity.HeatCapacityLiquid`,
and {obj}`thermo.heat_capacity.HeatCapacityGas` to perform the
actual calculation of each property.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Chemical('cubane').Cpm
137.
>>> Chemical('ethylbenzene', T=550, P=3E6).Cpm
294.
```

methods.name: thermo.chemical.Chemical.Vm
documentation:
Molar volume of the chemical at its current phase and
temperature and pressure, in units of \[m^3/mol\].

Utilizes the object oriented interfaces
{obj}`thermo.volume.VolumeSolid`,
{obj}`thermo.volume.VolumeLiquid`,
and {obj}`thermo.volume.VolumeGas` to perform the
actual calculation of each property.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Chemical('ethylbenzene', T=550, P=3E6).Vm
0.00017
```

methods.name: thermo.chemical.Chemical.rho
documentation:
Mass density of the chemical at its current phase and
temperature and pressure, in units of \[kg/m^3\].

Utilizes the object oriented interfaces
{obj}`thermo.volume.VolumeSolid`,
{obj}`thermo.volume.VolumeLiquid`,
and {obj}`thermo.volume.VolumeGas` to perform the
actual calculation of each property. Note that those interfaces provide
output in units of m^3/mol.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Chemical('decane', T=550, P=2E6).rho
498.
```

methods.name: thermo.chemical.Chemical.rhom
documentation:
Molar density of the chemical at its current phase and
temperature and pressure, in units of \[mol/m^3\].

Utilizes the object oriented interfaces
{obj}`thermo.volume.VolumeSolid`,
{obj}`thermo.volume.VolumeLiquid`,
and {obj}`thermo.volume.VolumeGas` to perform the
actual calculation of each property. Note that those interfaces provide
output in units of m^3/mol.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Chemical('1-hexanol').rhom
7986.
```

methods.name: thermo.chemical.Chemical.Z
documentation:
Compressibility factor of the chemical at its current phase and
temperature and pressure, \[dimensionless\].

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Chemical('MTBE', T=900, P=1E-2).Z
1.
```

methods.name: thermo.chemical.Chemical.SG
documentation:
Specific gravity of the chemical, \[dimensionless\].

For gas-phase conditions, this is calculated at 15.6 °C (60 °F) and 1
atm for the chemical and the reference fluid, air.
For liquid and solid phase conditions, this is calculated based on a
reference fluid of water at 4°C at 1 atm, but the with the liquid or
solid chemical's density at the currently specified conditions.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Chemical('MTBE').SG
0.73
```

methods.name: thermo.chemical.Chemical.isobaric_expansion
documentation:
Isobaric (constant-pressure) expansion of the chemical at its
current phase and temperature, in units of \[1/K\].

$$
\beta = \frac{1}{V}\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T} \right)_P
$$

# Examples

Radical change  in value just above and below the critical temperature
of water:

```pycon
>>> Chemical('water', T=647.1, P=22048320.0).isobaric_expansion
0.77
```

```pycon
>>> Chemical('water', T=647.2, P=22048320.0).isobaric_expansion
0.39
```

methods.name: thermo.chemical.Chemical.JT
documentation:
Joule Thomson coefficient of the chemical at its
current phase and temperature, in units of \[K/Pa\].

$$
\mu_{JT} = \left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_H = \frac{1}{C_p} \left[T \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P - V\right] = \frac{V}{C_p}\left(\beta T-1\right)
$$

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Chemical('water').JT
-2.21e-07
```

methods.name: thermo.chemical.Chemical.mu
documentation:
Viscosity of the chemical at its current phase, temperature, and
pressure in units of \[Pa\*s\].

Utilizes the object oriented interfaces
{obj}`thermo.viscosity.ViscosityLiquid` and
{obj}`thermo.viscosity.ViscosityGas` to perform the
actual calculation of each property.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Chemical('ethanol', T=300).mu
0.0010
>>> Chemical('ethanol', T=400).mu
1.18e-05
```

methods.name: thermo.chemical.Chemical.k
documentation:
Thermal conductivity of the chemical at its current phase,
temperature, and pressure in units of \[W/m/K\].

Utilizes the object oriented interfaces
{obj}`thermo.thermal_conductivity.ThermalConductivityLiquid` and
{obj}`thermo.thermal_conductivity.ThermalConductivityGas` to perform
the actual calculation of each property.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Chemical('ethanol', T=300).kl
0.16
>>> Chemical('ethanol', T=400).kg
0.026
```

methods.name: thermo.chemical.Chemical.nu
documentation:
Kinematic viscosity of the the chemical at its current temperature,
pressure, and phase in units of \[m^2/s\].

$$
\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho}
$$

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Chemical('argon').nu
1.38e-05
```

methods.name: thermo.chemical.Chemical.alpha
documentation:
Thermal diffusivity of the chemical at its current temperature,
pressure, and phase in units of \[m^2/s\].

$$
\alpha = \frac{k}{\rho Cp}
$$

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Chemical('furfural').alpha
8.7e-08
```

methods.name: thermo.chemical.Chemical.Pr
documentation:
Prandtl number of the chemical at its current temperature,
pressure, and phase; \[dimensionless\].

$$
Pr = \frac{C_p \mu}{k}
$$

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Chemical('acetone').Pr
4.1
```

methods.name: thermo.chemical.Chemical.Poynting
documentation:
Poynting correction factor \[dimensionless\] for use in phase
equilibria methods based on activity coefficients or other reference
states. Performs the shortcut calculation assuming molar volume is
independent of pressure.

$$
\text{Poy} =  \exp\left[\frac{V_l (P-P^{sat})}{RT}\right]
$$

The full calculation normally returns values very close to the
approximate ones. This property is defined in terms of
pure components only.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Chemical('pentane', T=300, P=2e6).Poynting
1.09
```

# Notes

The full equation shown below can be used as follows:

$$
\text{Poy} = \exp\left[\frac{\int_{P_i^{sat}}^P V_i^l dP}{RT}\right]
$$

```pycon
>>> from scipy.integrate import quad
>>> c = Chemical('pentane', T=300, P=2e6)
>>> exp(quad(lambda P : c.VolumeLiquid(c.T, P), c.Psat, c.P)[0]/R/c.T)
1.093
```

methods.name: thermo.chemical.Chemical.Reynolds
documentation: ## Convenience Dimensionless numbers

Creates a Chemical object which contains basic information such as
molecular weight and the structure of the species, as well as thermodynamic
and transport properties as a function of temperature and pressure.

# Parameters

ID
: One of the following \[-\]:
  : - Name, in IUPAC form or common form or a synonym registered in PubChem
    - InChI name, prefixed by 'InChI=1S/' or 'InChI=1/'
    - InChI key, prefixed by 'InChIKey='
    - PubChem CID, prefixed by 'PubChem='
    - SMILES (prefix with 'SMILES=' to ensure smiles parsing)
    - CAS number

T

: Temperature of the chemical (default 298.15 K), \[K\]

P

: Pressure of the chemical (default 101325 Pa) \[Pa\]

# Examples

Creating chemical objects:

```pycon
>>> Chemical('hexane')
<Chemical [hexane], T=298.15 K, P=101325 Pa>
```

```pycon
>>> Chemical('CCCCCCCC', T=500, P=1E7)
<Chemical [octane], T=500.00 K, P=10000000 Pa>
```

```pycon
>>> Chemical('7440-36-0', P=1000)
<Chemical [antimony], T=298.15 K, P=1000 Pa>
```

Getting basic properties:

```pycon
>>> N2 = Chemical('Nitrogen')
>>> N2.Tm, N2.Tb, N2.Tc # melting, boiling, and critical points [K]
(63.15, 77.355, 126.2)
>>> N2.Pt, N2.Pc # sublimation and critical pressure [Pa]
(12526.9697368421, 3394387.5)
>>> N2.CAS, N2.formula, N2.InChI, N2.smiles, N2.atoms # CAS number, formula, InChI string, smiles string, dictionary of atomic elements and their count
('7727-37-9', 'N2', 'N2/c1-2', 'N#N', {'N': 2})
```

Changing the T/P of the chemical, and gettign temperature-dependent
properties:

```pycon
>>> N2.Cp, N2.rho, N2.mu # Heat capacity [J/kg/K], density [kg/m^3], viscosity [Pa*s]
(1039., 1.14, 1.78e-05)
>>> N2.calculate(T=65, P=1E6) # set it to a liquid at 65 K and 1 MPa
>>> N2.phase
'l'
>>> N2.Cp, N2.rho, N2.mu # properties are now of the liquid phase
(2002., 861., 0.000285)
```

Molar units are also available for properties:

```pycon
>>> N2.Cpm, N2.Vm, N2.Hvapm # heat capacity [J/mol/K], molar volume [m^3/mol], enthalpy of vaporization [J/mol]
(56., 3.25e-05, 5982.)
```

A great deal of properties are available; for a complete list look at the
attributes list.

```pycon
>>> N2.alpha, N2.JT # thermal diffusivity [m^2/s], Joule-Thompson coefficient [K/Pa]
(9.87e-08, -4.0e-07)
```

```pycon
>>> N2.isentropic_exponent, N2.isobaric_expansion
(1.4, 0.0047)
```

For pure species, the phase is easily identified, allowing for properties
to be obtained without needing to specify the phase. However, the
properties are also available in the hypothetical gas phase (when under the
boiling point) and in the hypothetical liquid phase (when above the boiling
point) as these properties are needed to evaluate mixture properties.
Specify the phase of a property to be retrieved by appending 'l' or 'g' or
's' to the property.

```pycon
>>> C50 = Chemical('pentacontane')
```

```pycon
>>> C50.rhog, C50.Cpg, C50.kg, C50.mug
(4.241646701894199, 1126.5533755283168, 0.00941385692301755, 6.973325939594919e-06)
```

Temperature dependent properties are calculated by objects which provide
many useful features related to the properties. To determine the
temperature at which nitrogen has a saturation pressure of 1 MPa:

```pycon
>>> N2.VaporPressure.solve_property(1E6)
103.73528598652341
```

To compute an integral of the ideal-gas heat capacity of nitrogen
to determine the enthalpy required for a given change in temperature.
Note the thermodynamic objects calculate values in molar units always.

```pycon
>>> N2.HeatCapacityGas.T_dependent_property_integral(100, 120) # J/mol/K
582.0121860897898
```

Derivatives of properties can be calculated as well, as may be needed by
for example heat transfer calculations:

```pycon
>>> N2.SurfaceTension.T_dependent_property_derivative(77)
-0.00022695346296730534
```

If a property is needed at multiple temperatures or pressures, it is faster
to use the object directly to perform the calculation rather than setting
the conditions for the chemical.

```pycon
>>> [N2.VaporPressure(T) for T in range(80, 120, 10)]
[136979.4840843189, 360712.5746603142, 778846.276691705, 1466996.7208525643]
```

These objects are also how the methods by which the properties are
calculated can be changed. To see the available methods for a property:

```pycon
>>> N2.VaporPressure.all_methods
set(['VDI_PPDS', 'BOILING_CRITICAL', 'WAGNER_MCGARRY', 'AMBROSE_WALTON', 'COOLPROP', 'LEE_KESLER_PSAT', 'EOS', 'ANTOINE_POLING', 'SANJARI', 'DIPPR_PERRY_8E', 'Edalat', 'WAGNER_POLING'])
```

To specify the method which should be used for calculations of a property.
In the example below, the Lee-kesler correlation for vapor pressure is
specified.

```pycon
>>> N2.calculate(80)
>>> N2.Psat
136979.4840843189
>>> N2.VaporPressure.method = 'LEE_KESLER_PSAT'
>>> N2.Psat
134987.76815364443
```

Properties may also be plotted via these objects:

```pycon
>>> N2.VaporPressure.plot_T_dependent_property() # doctest: +SKIP
>>> N2.VolumeLiquid.plot_isotherm(T=77, Pmin=1E5, Pmax=1E7) # doctest: +SKIP
>>> N2.VolumeLiquid.plot_isobar(P=1E6,  Tmin=66, Tmax=120) # doctest: +SKIP
>>> N2.VolumeLiquid.plot_TP_dependent_property(Tmin=60, Tmax=100,  Pmin=1E5, Pmax=1E7) # doctest: +SKIP
```

# Notes

:::{warning}
The Chemical class is not designed for high-performance or the ability
to use different thermodynamic models. It is especially limited in its
multiphase support and the ability to solve with specifications other
than temperature and pressure. It is impossible to change constant
properties such as a compound's critical temperature in this interface.

It is recommended to switch over to the {obj}`thermo.flash` interface
which solves those problems and is better positioned to grow. That
interface also requires users to be responsible for their chemical
constants and pure component correlations; while default values can
easily be loaded for most compounds, the user is ultimately responsible
for them.
:::

# Attributes

T

: Temperature of the chemical, \[K\]

P

: Pressure of the chemical, \[Pa\]

phase

: Phase of the chemical; one of 's', 'l', 'g', or 'l/g'.

ID

: User specified string by which the chemical's CAS was looked up.

CAS

: The CAS number of the chemical.

PubChem

: PubChem Compound identifier (CID) of the chemical; all chemicals are
  sourced from their database. Chemicals can be looked at online at
  [https://pubchem.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov](https://pubchem.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov).

MW

: Molecular weight of the compound, \[g/mol\]

formula

: Molecular formula of the compound.

atoms

: dictionary of counts of individual atoms, indexed by symbol with
  proper capitalization, \[-\]

similarity_variable

: Similarity variable, see {obj}`chemicals.elements.similarity_variable`
  for the definition, \[mol/g\]

smiles

: Simplified molecular-input line-entry system representation of the
  compound.

InChI

: IUPAC International Chemical Identifier of the compound.

InChI_Key

: 25-character hash of the compound's InChI.

IUPAC_name

: Preferred IUPAC name for a compound.

synonyms

: All synonyms for the compound found in PubChem, sorted by popularity.

Tm

: Melting temperature \[K\]

Tb

: Boiling temperature \[K\]

Tc

: Critical temperature \[K\]

Pc

: Critical pressure \[Pa\]

Vc

: Critical volume \[m^3/mol\]

Zc

: Critical compressibility \[-\]

rhoc

: Critical density \[kg/m^3\]

rhocm

: Critical molar density \[mol/m^3\]

omega

: Acentric factor \[-\]

StielPolar

: Stiel Polar factor, see {obj}`chemicals.acentric.Stiel_polar_factor` for
  the definition \[-\]

Tt

: Triple temperature, \[K\]

Pt

: Triple pressure, \[Pa\]

Hfus

: Enthalpy of fusion \[J/kg\]

Hfusm

: Molar enthalpy of fusion \[J/mol\]

Hsub

: Enthalpy of sublimation \[J/kg\]

Hsubm

: Molar enthalpy of sublimation \[J/mol\]

Hfm

: Standard state molar enthalpy of formation, \[J/mol\]

Hf

: Standard enthalpy of formation in a mass basis, \[J/kg\]

Hfgm

: Ideal-gas molar enthalpy of formation, \[J/mol\]

Hfg

: Ideal-gas enthalpy of formation in a mass basis, \[J/kg\]

Hcm

: Molar higher heat of combustion \[J/mol\]

Hc

: Higher Heat of combustion \[J/kg\]

Hcm_lower

: Molar lower heat of combustion \[J/mol\]

Hc_lower

: Lower Heat of combustion \[J/kg\]

S0m

: Standard state absolute molar entropy of the chemical, \[J/mol/K\]

S0

: Standard state absolute entropy of the chemical, \[J/kg/K\]

S0gm

: Absolute molar entropy in an ideal gas state of the chemical, \[J/mol/K\]

S0g

: Absolute mass entropy in an ideal gas state of the chemical, \[J/kg/K\]

Gfm

: Standard state molar change of Gibbs energy of formation \[J/mol\]

Gf

: Standard state change of Gibbs energy of formation \[J/kg\]

Gfgm

: Ideal-gas molar change of Gibbs energy of formation \[J/mol\]

Gfg

: Ideal-gas change of Gibbs energy of formation \[J/kg\]

Sfm

: Standard state molar change of entropy of formation, \[J/mol/K\]

Sf

: Standard state change of entropy of formation, \[J/kg/K\]

Sfgm

: Ideal-gas molar change of entropy of formation, \[J/mol/K\]

Sfg

: Ideal-gas change of entropy of formation, \[J/kg/K\]

Hcgm

: Higher molar heat of combustion of the chemical in the ideal gas state,
  \[J/mol\]

Hcg

: Higher heat of combustion of the chemical in the ideal gas state,
  \[J/kg\]

Hcgm_lower

: Lower molar heat of combustion of the chemical in the ideal gas state,
  \[J/mol\]

Hcg_lower

: Lower heat of combustion of the chemical in the ideal gas state,
  \[J/kg\]

Tflash

: Flash point of the chemical, \[K\]

Tautoignition

: Autoignition point of the chemical, \[K\]

LFL

: Lower flammability limit of the gas in an atmosphere at STP, mole
  fraction \[-\]

UFL

: Upper flammability limit of the gas in an atmosphere at STP, mole
  fraction \[-\]

TWA

: Time-Weighted Average limit on worker exposure to dangerous chemicals.

STEL

: Short-term Exposure limit on worker exposure to dangerous chemicals.

Ceiling

: Ceiling limits on worker exposure to dangerous chemicals.

Skin

: Whether or not a chemical can be absorbed through the skin.

Carcinogen

: Carcinogen status information.

dipole

: Dipole moment in debye, \[3.33564095198e-30 ampere\*second^2\]

Stockmayer

: Lennard-Jones depth of potential-energy minimum over k, \[K\]

molecular_diameter

: Lennard-Jones molecular diameter, \[angstrom\]

GWP

: Global warming potential (default 100-year outlook) (impact/mass
  chemical)/(impact/mass CO2), \[-\]

ODP

: Ozone Depletion potential (impact/mass chemical)/(impact/mass CFC-11),
  \[-\]

logP

: Octanol-water partition coefficient, \[-\]

legal_status

: Legal status information \[-\]

economic_status

: Economic status information \[-\]

RI

: Refractive Index on the Na D line, \[-\]

RIT

: Temperature at which refractive index reading was made

conductivity

: Electrical conductivity of the fluid, \[S/m\]

conductivityT

: Temperature at which conductivity measurement was made

VaporPressure

: Instance of {obj}`thermo.vapor_pressure.VaporPressure`, with data and
  methods loaded for the chemical; performs the actual calculations of
  vapor pressure of the chemical.

EnthalpyVaporization

: Instance of {obj}`thermo.phase_change.EnthalpyVaporization`, with data
  and methods loaded for the chemical; performs the actual calculations
  of molar enthalpy of vaporization of the chemical.

VolumeSolid

: Instance of {obj}`thermo.volume.VolumeSolid`, with data and methods
  loaded for the chemical; performs the actual calculations of molar
  volume of the solid phase of the chemical.

VolumeLiquid

: Instance of {obj}`thermo.volume.VolumeLiquid`, with data and methods
  loaded for the chemical; performs the actual calculations of molar
  volume of the liquid phase of the chemical.

VolumeGas

: Instance of {obj}`thermo.volume.VolumeGas`, with data and methods
  loaded for the chemical; performs the actual calculations of molar
  volume of the gas phase of the chemical.

HeatCapacitySolid

: Instance of {obj}`thermo.heat_capacity.HeatCapacitySolid`, with data and
  methods loaded for the chemical; performs the actual calculations of
  molar heat capacity of the solid phase of the chemical.

HeatCapacityLiquid

: Instance of {obj}`thermo.heat_capacity.HeatCapacityLiquid`, with data and
  methods loaded for the chemical; performs the actual calculations of
  molar heat capacity of the liquid phase of the chemical.

HeatCapacityGas

: Instance of {obj}`thermo.heat_capacity.HeatCapacityGas`, with data and
  methods loaded for the chemical; performs the actual calculations of
  molar heat capacity of the gas phase of the chemical.

ViscosityLiquid

: Instance of {obj}`thermo.viscosity.ViscosityLiquid`, with data and
  methods loaded for the chemical; performs the actual calculations of
  viscosity of the liquid phase of the chemical.

ViscosityGas

: Instance of {obj}`thermo.viscosity.ViscosityGas`, with data and
  methods loaded for the chemical; performs the actual calculations of
  viscosity of the gas phase of the chemical.

ThermalConductivityLiquid

: Instance of {obj}`thermo.thermal_conductivity.ThermalConductivityLiquid`,
  with data and methods loaded for the chemical; performs the actual
  calculations of thermal conductivity of the liquid phase of the
  chemical.

ThermalConductivityGas

: Instance of {obj}`thermo.thermal_conductivity.ThermalConductivityGas`,
  with data and methods loaded for the chemical; performs the actual
  calculations of thermal conductivity of the gas phase of the chemical.

SurfaceTension

: Instance of {obj}`thermo.interface.SurfaceTension`, with data and
  methods loaded for the chemical; performs the actual calculations of
  surface tension of the chemical.

Permittivity

: Instance of {obj}`thermo.permittivity.PermittivityLiquid`, with data and
  methods loaded for the chemical; performs the actual calculations of
  permittivity of the chemical.

Psat_298

: Vapor pressure of the chemical at 298.15 K, \[Pa\]

phase_STP

: Phase of the chemical at 298.15 K and 101325 Pa; one of 's', 'l', 'g',
  or 'l/g'.

Vml_Tb

: Molar volume of liquid phase at the normal boiling point \[m^3/mol\]

Vml_Tm

: Molar volume of liquid phase at the melting point \[m^3/mol\]

Vml_STP

: Molar volume of liquid phase at 298.15 K and 101325 Pa \[m^3/mol\]

rhoml_STP

: Molar density of liquid phase at 298.15 K and 101325 Pa \[mol/m^3\]

Vmg_STP

: Molar volume of gas phase at 298.15 K and 101325 Pa according to
  the ideal gas law, \[m^3/mol\]

Vms_Tm

: Molar volume of solid phase at the melting point \[m^3/mol\]

rhos_Tm

: Mass density of solid phase at the melting point \[kg/m^3\]

Hvap_Tbm

: Molar enthalpy of vaporization at the normal boiling point \[J/mol\]

Hvap_Tb

: Mass enthalpy of vaporization at the normal boiling point \[J/kg\]

Hvapm_298

: Molar enthalpy of vaporization at 298.15 K \[J/mol\]

Hvap_298

: Mass enthalpy of vaporization at 298.15 K \[J/kg\]

functions.name: thermo.chemical_utils.S0_basis_converter
documentation:
This function converts a liquid or gas standard entropy to the
other. This is useful, as thermodynamic packages often work with ideal-
gas as the reference state and require ideal-gas Gibbs energies of
formation.

# Parameters

c

: Chemical object, \[-\]

S0_liq

: Liquid absolute entropy of the compound at the reference temperature
  \[J/mol/K\]

S0_gas

: Gas absolute entropy of the compound at the reference temperature
  \[J/mol/K\]

T_ref

: The standard state temperature, default 298.15 K; few values are
  tabulated at other temperatures, \[-\]

# Returns

S0_calc

: Standard absolute entropy of the compound at the reference temperature
  in the other state to the one provided, \[J/mol\]

# Notes

This function relies in accurate heat capacity curves for both the liquid
and gas state.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> from thermo.chemical import Chemical
>>> S0_basis_converter(Chemical('decane'), S0_liq=425.89) # doctest:+SKIP
544.6792
>>> S0_basis_converter(Chemical('decane'), S0_gas=545.7) # doctest:+SKIP
426.9107
```

functions.name: thermo.chemical_utils.standard_state_ideal_gas_formation
documentation:
This function calculates the standard state ideal-gas heat of formation
of a compound at a specified from first principles. The entropy change and
Gibbs free energy change of formation are also returned.
This special condition is usually tabulated in thermodynamic tables, and
this function is intended to be consistent with the standard conventions.

The values returned depend on:

- The heat of formation as an ideal gas of the compound at 298.15 K (must be measured experimentally)
- The ideal gas absolute entropy of the compound at 298.15 K (calculated from heat capacity and phase transitions of the compound and its various phases)
- The chosen reference states of the elements (convention, but hardcoded)
- The heat capacity and phase transitions of the constituent elements of the compound in various phases

# Parameters

c

: Chemical object, \[-\]

T

: The temperature to perform the calculation at

Hf

: Standard enthalpy of formation of the compound in the gas phase \[J/mol\]

Sf

: Standard entropy of formation of the compound in the gas phase \[J/mol/K\]

T_ref

: The standard state temperature, default 298.15 K; if set to another
  value `Hf` and `Sf` must be provided for this temperature `T_ref`
  or the default values at 298.15 K will be used and this function will
  return nonsense, \[-\]

# Returns

H_standard_state

: The standard state ideal gas enthalpy of the compound, \[J/mol\]

S_standard_state

: The standard state ideal gas entropy of the compound, \[J/mol/K\]

G_standard_state

: The standard state ideal gas Gibbs energy of the compound, \[J/mol\]

# Notes

Not all elements are supported. The default property methods
for some phases of the elements are hardcoded in this function for
accuracy, and may change as data evolves.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> from thermo.chemical import Chemical
>>> standard_state_ideal_gas_formation(Chemical('water'), T=500.0)
(-243821.4, -49.63, -219006.)
```

functions.name: thermo.functional_groups.is_amide
documentation:
Given a `rdkit.Chem.rdchem.Mol` object, returns whether or not the
molecule has a amide RC(=O)NR\`R″ group.

# Parameters

mol

: Molecule \[-\]

# Returns

is_amide

: Whether or not the compound is a amide or not, \[-\].

# Examples

```pycon
>>> from rdkit.Chem import MolFromSmiles # doctest:+SKIP
>>> is_amide(MolFromSmiles('CN(C)C=O')) # doctest:+SKIP
True
```

functions.name: thermo.functional_groups.is_amidine
documentation:
Given a `rdkit.Chem.rdchem.Mol` object, returns whether or not the
molecule has a amidine RC(NR)NR2 group.

# Parameters

mol

: Molecule \[-\]

# Returns

is_amidine

: Whether or not the compound is a amidine or not, \[-\].

# Examples

```pycon
>>> from rdkit.Chem import MolFromSmiles # doctest:+SKIP
>>> is_amidine(MolFromSmiles('C1=CC(=CC=C1C(=N)N)OCCCCCOC2=CC=C(C=C2)C(=N)N')) # doctest:+SKIP
True
```

functions.name: thermo.functional_groups.is_primary_ketimine
documentation:
Given a `rdkit.Chem.rdchem.Mol` object, returns whether or not the
molecule is a primary ketimine.

# Parameters

mol

: Molecule \[-\]

# Returns

is_primary_ketimine

: Whether or not the compound is a primary ketimine, \[-\].

# Examples

```pycon
>>> from rdkit.Chem import MolFromSmiles # doctest:+SKIP
>>> is_primary_ketimine(MolFromSmiles('C1=CC=C(C=C1)C(=N)C2=CC=CC=C2')) # doctest:+SKIP
True
```

```pycon
True
```

is_alkene

functions.name: thermo.functional_groups.is_organic
documentation:
Given a `rdkit.Chem.rdchem.Mol` object, returns whether or not the
molecule is organic. The definition of organic vs. inorganic compounds is
arabitrary. The rules implemented here are fairly complex.

- If a compound has an C-C bond, a C=C bond, a carbon triple bond, a
  carbon attatched however to a hydrogen, a carbon in a ring, or an amide
  group.
- If a compound is in the list of canonical smiles `organic_smiles`,
  either the defaults in the library or those provided as an input to the
  function, the molecule is considered organic.
- If a compound is in the list of canonical smiles `inorganic_smiles`,
  either the defaults in the library or those provided as an input to the
  function, the molecule is considered inorganic.
- If `restrict_atoms` is provided and atoms are present in the molecule
  that are restricted, the compound is considered restricted.

# Parameters

mol

: Molecule \[-\]

restrict_atoms

: Atoms that cannot be found in an organic molecule, \[-\]

organic_smiles

: Smiles that are hardcoded to be organic, \[-\]

inorganic_smiles

: Smiles that are hardcoded to be inorganic, \[-\]

# Returns

is_organic

: Whether or not the compound is a organic or not, \[-\].

# Examples

```pycon
>>> from rdkit.Chem import MolFromSmiles # doctest:+SKIP
>>> is_organic(MolFromSmiles("CC(C)C(C)C(C)C")) # doctest:+SKIP
True
```

functions.name: thermo.functional_groups.count_ring_ring_attatchments
documentation:
Given a `rdkit.Chem.rdchem.Mol` object, count the number
of times a ring in the molecule is bonded with another ring
in the molecule.

An easy explanation is cubane - each edge of the cube is a ring uniquely
bonding with another ring; so this function returns twelve.

# Parameters

mol

: Molecule \[-\]

# Returns

ring_ring_attatchments

: The number of ring-ring bonds, \[-\].

# Examples

```pycon
>>> from rdkit.Chem import MolFromSmiles # doctest:+SKIP
>>> count_ring_ring_attatchments(MolFromSmiles('C12C3C4C1C5C2C3C45')) # doctest:+SKIP
12
```

functions.name: thermo.functional_groups.count_rings_attatched_to_rings
documentation:
Given a `rdkit.Chem.rdchem.Mol` object, count the number
of rings in the molecule that are attatched to another ring.
if `allow_neighbors` is True, any bond to another atom that is part of a
ring is allowed; if it is False, the rings have to share a wall.

# Parameters

mol

: Molecule \[-\]

allow_neighbors

: Whether or not to count neighboring rings or just ones sharing a wall, \[-\]

atom_rings

: Internal parameter, used for performance only

# Returns

rings_attatched_to_rings

: The number of rings bonded to other rings, \[-\].

# Examples

```pycon
>>> from rdkit.Chem import MolFromSmiles # doctest:+SKIP
>>> count_rings_attatched_to_rings(MolFromSmiles('C12C3C4C1C5C2C3C45')) # doctest:+SKIP
6
```

functions.name: thermo.functional_groups.is_radionuclide

functions.name: thermo.functional_groups.BVirial_Tsonopoulos_extended_ab
documentation:
Calculates the  of `a` and `b` parameters of the Tsonopoulos (extended)
second virial coefficient prediction method. These parameters account for
polarity. This function uses `rdkit` to identify the component type of the
molecule.

# Parameters

Tc

: Critical temperature of fluid \[K\]

Pc

: Critical pressure of the fluid \[Pa\]

dipole

: dipole moment, optional, \[Debye\]

# Returns

a

: Fit parameter matched to one of the supported chemical classes.

b

: Fit parameter matched to one of the supported chemical classes.

# Notes

To calculate `a` or `b`, the following rules are used:

For 'simple' or 'normal' fluids:

$$
a = 0
$$

$$
b = 0
$$

For 'ketone', 'aldehyde', 'alkyl nitrile', 'ether', 'carboxylic acid',
or 'ester' types of chemicals:

$$
a = -2.14\times 10^{-4} \mu_r - 4.308 \times 10^{-21} (\mu_r)^8
$$

$$
b = 0
$$

For 'alkyl halide', 'mercaptan', 'sulfide', or 'disulfide' types of
chemicals:

$$
a = -2.188\times 10^{-4} (\mu_r)^4 - 7.831 \times 10^{-21} (\mu_r)^8
$$

$$
b = 0
$$

For 'alkanol' types of chemicals (except methanol):

$$
a = 0.0878
$$

$$
b = 0.00908 + 0.0006957 \mu_r
$$

For methanol:

$$
a = 0.0878
$$

$$
b = 0.0525
$$

For water:

$$
a = -0.0109
$$

$$
b = 0
$$

If required, the form of dipole moment used in the calculation of some
types of `a` and `b` values is as follows:

$$
\mu_r = 100000\frac{\mu^2(Pc/101325.0)}{Tc^2}
$$

# References

[^footnote-1]: Tsonopoulos, C., and J. L. Heidman. "From the Virial to the Cubic
    Equation of State." Fluid Phase Equilibria 57, no. 3 (1990): 261-76.
    doi:10.1016/0378-3812(90)85126-U

[^footnote-2]: Tsonopoulos, Constantine, and John H. Dymond. "Second Virial
    Coefficients of Normal Alkanes, Linear 1-Alkanols (and Water), Alkyl
    Ethers, and Their Mixtures." Fluid Phase Equilibria, International
    Workshop on Vapour-Liquid Equilibria and Related Properties in Binary
    and Ternary Mixtures of Ethers, Alkanes and Alkanols, 133, no. 1-2
    (June 1997): 11-34. doi:10.1016/S0378-3812(97)00058-7.
## Calculate things using functional groups - basic

classes.name: thermo.flash.flash_vl.FlashVL

methods.name: thermo.flash.flash_vl.FlashVL.sequential_substitution_2P
documentation: stable = True
for i, trial_comp in enumerate(gen):
try:
sln = stability_iteration_Michelsen(min_phase, trial_comp, test_phase=other_phase,
maxiter=self.PT_STABILITY_MAXITER, xtol=self.PT_STABILITY_XTOL)
sum_zs_test, Ks, zs_test, V_over_F, trial_zs, appearing_zs = sln
lnK_2_tot = 0.0
for k in range(self.N):
lnK = log(Ks[k])
lnK_2_tot += lnK*lnK
sum_criteria = abs(sum_zs_test - 1.0)
if sum_criteria < 1e-9 or lnK_2_tot < 1e-7:
continue
# some stability test-driven VFs are converged to about the right solution - but just a little on the other side
# For those cases, we need to let SS determine the result
stable = V_over_F < -1e-6 or V_over_F > (1.0 + 1e-6) #not (0.0 < V_over_F < 1.0)
if not stable:
break
except UnconvergedError:
pass
stab_guess_name = self.stab.incipient_guess_name(i)

class_variables.name: thermo.flash.flash_vl.FlashVL.PT_STABILITY_MAXITER
documentation: 30 good professional default; 500 used in source DTU

class_variables.name: thermo.flash.flash_vl.FlashVL.PT_STABILITY_XTOL
documentation: 1e-12 was too strict; 1e-10 used in source DTU; 1e-9 set for some points near critical where convergence stopped; even some more stopped at higher Ts

Class for performing flash calculations on one and
two phase vapor and liquid multicomponent systems. Use {obj}`FlashVLN` for
systems which can have multiple liquid phases.

The minimum information that is needed in addition to the {obj}`Phase`
objects is:

- MWs
- Vapor pressure curve
- Functioning enthalpy models for each phase

# Parameters

constants

: Package of chemical constants; these are used as boundaries at times,
  initial guesses other times, and in all cases these properties are
  accessible as attributes of the resulting
  {obj}`EquilibriumState <thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState>` object, \[-\]

correlations

: Package of chemical T-dependent properties; these are used as boundaries at times,
  for initial guesses other times, and in all cases these properties are
  accessible as attributes of the resulting
  {obj}`EquilibriumState <thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState>` object, \[-\]

gas

: A single phase which can represent the gas phase, \[-\]

liquid

: A single phase which can represent the liquid phase, \[-\]

settings

: Object containing settings for calculating bulk and transport
  properties, \[-\]

# Attributes

PT_SS_MAXITER

: Maximum number of sequential substitution iterations to try when
  converging a two-phase solution, \[-\]

PT_SS_TOL

: Convergence tolerance in sequential substitution \[-\]

PT_SS_POLISH

: When set to True, flashes which are very near a vapor fraction of 0 or
  1 are converged to a higher tolerance to ensure the solution is
  correct; without this, a flash might converge to a vapor fraction of
  -1e-7 and be called single phase, but with this the correct solution
  may be found to be 1e-8 and will be correctly returned as two phase.\[-\]

PT_SS_POLISH_VF

: What tolerance to a vapor fraction of 0 or 1; this is an absolute
  vapor fraction value, \[-\]

PT_SS_POLISH_MAXITER

: Maximum number of sequential substitution iterations to try when
  converging a two-phase solution that has been detected to be very
  sensitive, with a vapor fraction near 0 or 1 \[-\]

PT_SS_POLISH_TOL

: Convergence tolerance in sequential substitution when
  converging a two-phase solution that has been detected to be very
  sensitive, with a vapor fraction near 0 or 1 \[-\]

PT_STABILITY_MAXITER

: Maximum number of iterations to try when converging a stability test,
  \[-\]

PT_STABILITY_XTOL

: Convergence tolerance in the stability test \[-\]

DEW_BUBBLE_VF_K_COMPOSITION_INDEPENDENT_XTOL

: Convergence tolerance in Newton solver for bubble, dew, and vapor
  fraction spec flashes when both the liquid and gas model's K values do
  not dependent on composition, \[-\]

DEW_BUBBLE_QUASI_NEWTON_XTOL

: Convergence tolerance in quasi-Newton bubble and dew point flashes, \[-\]

DEW_BUBBLE_QUASI_NEWTON_MAXITER

: Maximum number of iterations to use in quasi-Newton bubble and dew
  point flashes, \[-\]

DEW_BUBBLE_NEWTON_XTOL

: Convergence tolerance in Newton bubble and dew point flashes, \[-\]

DEW_BUBBLE_NEWTON_MAXITER

: Maximum number of iterations to use in Newton bubble and dew
  point flashes, \[-\]

TPV_HSGUA_BISECT_XTOL

: Tolerance in the iteration variable when converging a flash with one
  (`T`, `P`, `V`) spec and one (`H`, `S`, `G`, `U`, `A`) spec using a
  bisection-type solver, \[-\]

TPV_HSGUA_BISECT_YTOL

: Absolute tolerance in the (`H`, `S`, `G`, `U`, `A`) spec when
  converging a flash with one (`T`, `P`, `V`) spec and one (`H`, `S`,
  `G`, `U`, `A`) spec using a bisection-type solver, \[-\]

TPV_HSGUA_BISECT_YTOL_ONLY

: When True, the `TPV_HSGUA_BISECT_XTOL` setting is ignored and the flash
  is considered converged once `TPV_HSGUA_BISECT_YTOL` is satisfied, \[-\]

TPV_HSGUA_NEWTON_XTOL

: Tolerance in the iteration variable when converging a flash with one
  (`T`, `P`, `V`) spec and one (`H`, `S`, `G`, `U`, `A`) spec using a
  full newton solver, \[-\]

TPV_HSGUA_NEWTON_MAXITER

: Maximum number of iterations when
  converging a flash with one (`T`, `P`, `V`) spec and one (`H`, `S`,
  `G`, `U`, `A`) spec using full newton solver, \[-\]

TPV_HSGUA_SECANT_MAXITER

: Maximum number of iterations when
  converging a flash with one (`T`, `P`, `V`) spec and one (`H`, `S`,
  `G`, `U`, `A`) spec using a secant solver, \[-\]

HSGUA_NEWTON_ANALYTICAL_JAC

: Whether or not to calculate the full newton jacobian analytically or
  numerically; this would need to be set to False if the phase objects
  used in the flash do not have complete analytical derivatives
  implemented, \[-\]

# Notes

The algorithms in this object are mostly from [^footnote-1], [^footnote-2] and [^footnote-3].
Sequential substitution without acceleration is used by default to converge
two-phase systems.

Quasi-newton methods are used by default to converge bubble and dew point
calculations.

Flashes with one (`T`, `P`, `V`) spec and one (`H`, `S`, `G`, `U`, `A`)
spec are solved by a 1D search over PT flashes.

Additional information that can be provided in the
{obj}`ChemicalConstantsPackage <thermo.chemical_package.ChemicalConstantsPackage>`
object and {obj}`PropertyCorrelationsPackage <thermo.chemical_package.PropertyCorrelationsPackage>`
object that may help convergence is:

- `Tc`, `Pc`, `omega`, `Tb`, and `atoms`
- Gas heat capacity correlations
- Liquid molar volume correlations
- Heat of vaporization correlations

:::{warning}
If this flasher is used on systems that can form two or more liquid
phases, and the flash specs are in that region, there is no guarantee
which solution is returned. Sometimes it is almost random, jumping
back and forth and providing nasty discontinuities.
:::

# Examples

For the system methane-ethane-nitrogen with a composition
\[0.965, 0.018, 0.017\], calculate the vapor fraction of the system and
equilibrium phase compositions at 110 K and 1 bar. Use the Peng-Robinson
equation of state and the chemsep sample interaction parameter database.

```pycon
>>> from thermo import ChemicalConstantsPackage, CEOSGas, CEOSLiquid, PRMIX, FlashVL
>>> from thermo.interaction_parameters import IPDB
>>> constants, properties = ChemicalConstantsPackage.from_IDs(['methane', 'ethane', 'nitrogen'])
>>> kijs = IPDB.get_ip_asymmetric_matrix('ChemSep PR', constants.CASs, 'kij')
>>> kijs
[[0.0, -0.0059, 0.0289], [-0.0059, 0.0, 0.0533], [0.0289, 0.0533, 0.0]]
>>> eos_kwargs = {'Pcs': constants.Pcs, 'Tcs': constants.Tcs, 'omegas': constants.omegas, 'kijs': kijs}
>>> gas = CEOSGas(PRMIX, eos_kwargs=eos_kwargs, HeatCapacityGases=properties.HeatCapacityGases)
>>> liquid = CEOSLiquid(PRMIX, eos_kwargs=eos_kwargs, HeatCapacityGases=properties.HeatCapacityGases)
>>> flasher = FlashVL(constants, properties, liquid=liquid, gas=gas)
>>> zs = [0.965, 0.018, 0.017]
>>> PT = flasher.flash(T=110.0, P=1e5, zs=zs)
>>> PT.VF, PT.gas.zs, PT.liquid0.zs
(0.0890, [0.8688, 2.5765e-05, 0.13115], [0.9744, 0.01975, 0.00584])
```

A few more flashes with the same system to showcase the functionality
of the {obj}`flash <Flash.flash>` interface:

```pycon
>>> flasher.flash(P=1e5, VF=1, zs=zs).T
133.8
>>> flasher.flash(T=133, VF=0, zs=zs).P
515029.6
>>> flasher.flash(P=PT.P, H=PT.H(), zs=zs).T
110.0
>>> flasher.flash(P=PT.P, S=PT.S(), zs=zs).T
110.0
>>> flasher.flash(T=PT.T, H=PT.H(), zs=zs).T
110.0
>>> flasher.flash(T=PT.T, S=PT.S(), zs=zs).T
110.0
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Michelsen, Michael L., and Jørgen M. Mollerup. Thermodynamic Models:
    Fundamentals & Computational Aspects. Tie-Line Publications, 2007.

[^footnote-2]: Poling, Bruce E., John M. Prausnitz, and John P. O\`Connell. The
    Properties of Gases and Liquids. 5th edition. New York: McGraw-Hill
    Professional, 2000.

[^footnote-3]: Gmehling, Jürgen, Michael Kleiber, Bärbel Kolbe, and Jürgen Rarey.
    Chemical Thermodynamics for Process Simulation. John Wiley & Sons, 2019.

methods.name: thermo.flash.flash_pure_vls.FlashPureVLS.generate_VF_data
documentation:
Could use some better algorithms for generating better data? Some of
the solutions count on this.

Class for performing flash calculations on pure-component systems.
This class is subtantially more robust than using multicomponent algorithms
on pure species. It is also faster. All parameters are also attributes.

The minimum information that is needed in addition to the {obj}`Phase`
objects is:

- MW
- Vapor pressure curve if including liquids
- Sublimation pressure curve if including solids
- Functioning enthalpy models for each phase

# Parameters

constants

: Package of chemical constants; these are used as boundaries at times,
  initial guesses other times, and in all cases these properties are
  accessible as attributes of the resulting
  {obj}`EquilibriumState <thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState>` object, \[-\]

correlations

: Package of chemical T-dependent properties; these are used as boundaries at times,
  for initial guesses other times, and in all cases these properties are
  accessible as attributes of the resulting
  {obj}`EquilibriumState <thermo.equilibrium.EquilibriumState>` object, \[-\]

gas

: A single phase which can represent the gas phase, \[-\]

liquids

: A list of phases for representing the liquid phase; normally only one
  liquid phase is present for a pure-component system, but multiple
  liquids are allowed for the really weird cases like having both
  parahydrogen and orthohydrogen. The liquid phase which calculates a
  lower Gibbs free energy is always used. \[-\]

solids

: A list of phases for representing the solid phase; it is very common
  for multiple solid forms of a compound to exist. For water ice, the
  list is very long - normally ice is in phase Ih but other phases are Ic,
  II, III, IV, V, VI, VII, VIII, IX, X, XI, XII, XIII, XIV, XV, XVI,
  Square ice, and Amorphous ice. It is less common for there to be
  published, reliable, thermodynamic models for these different phases;
  for water there is the IAPWS-06 model for Ih, and another model
  [here](https://aip.scitation.org/doi/10.1063/1.1931662)
  for phases Ih, Ic, II, III, IV, V, VI, IX, XI, XII. \[-\]

settings

: Object containing settings for calculating bulk and transport
  properties, \[-\]

# Attributes

VL_IG_hack

: Whether or not to trust the saturation curve of the liquid phase;
  applied automatically to the
  {obj}`GibbsExcessLiquid <thermo.phases.GibbsExcessLiquid>`
  phase if there is a single liquid only, \[-\]

VL_EOS_hacks

: Whether or not to trust the saturation curve of the EOS liquid phase;
  applied automatically to the
  {obj}`CEOSLiquid <thermo.phases.CEOSLiquid>`
  phase if there is a single liquid only, \[-\]

TPV_HSGUA_guess_maxiter

: Maximum number of iterations to try when converging a shortcut model
  for flashes with one (`T`, `P`, `V`) spec and one (`H`, `S`, `G`, `U`,
  `A`) spec, \[-\]

TPV_HSGUA_guess_xtol

: Convergence tolerance in the iteration variable when converging a
  shortcut model for flashes with one (`T`, `P`, `V`) spec and one (`H`,
  `S`, `G`, `U`, `A`) spec, \[-\]

TPV_HSGUA_maxiter

: Maximum number of iterations to try when converging a flashes with one
  (`T`, `P`, `V`) spec and one (`H`, `S`, `G`, `U`, `A`) spec; this is
  on a per-phase basis, so if there is a liquid and a gas phase, the
  maximum number of iterations that could end up being tried would be
  twice this, \[-\]

TPV_HSGUA_xtol

: Convergence tolerance in the iteration variable dimension when
  converging a flash with one (`T`, `P`, `V`) spec and one (`H`, `S`,
  `G`, `U`, `A`) spec, \[-\]

TVF_maxiter

: Maximum number of iterations to try when converging a flashes with a
  temperature and vapor fraction specification, \[-\]

TVF_xtol

: Convergence tolerance in the temperature dimension when converging a
  flashes with a temperature and vapor fraction specification, \[-\]

PVF_maxiter

: Maximum number of iterations to try when converging a flashes with a
  pressure and vapor fraction specification, \[-\]

PVF_xtol

: Convergence tolerance in the pressure dimension when converging a
  flashes with a pressure and vapor fraction specification, \[-\]

TSF_maxiter

: Maximum number of iterations to try when converging a flashes with a
  temperature and solid fraction specification, \[-\]

TSF_xtol

: Convergence tolerance in the temperature dimension when converging a
  flashes with a temperature and solid fraction specification, \[-\]

PSF_maxiter

: Maximum number of iterations to try when converging a flashes with a
  pressure and solid fraction specification, \[-\]

PSF_xtol

: Convergence tolerance in the pressure dimension when converging a
  flashes with a pressure and solid fraction specification, \[-\]

# Notes

The algorithms in this object are mostly from [^footnote-1] and [^footnote-2]. They all
boil down to newton methods with analytical derivatives. The phase with
the lowest Gibbs energy is the most stable if there are multiple
solutions.

Phase input combinations which have specific simplifying assumptions
(and thus more speed) are:

- a {obj}`CEOSLiquid <thermo.phases.CEOSLiquid>` and a {obj}`CEOSGas <thermo.phases.CEOSGas>` with the same (consistent) parameters
- a {obj}`CEOSGas <thermo.phases.CEOSGas>` with the {obj}`IGMIX <thermo.eos_mix.IGMIX>` eos and a {obj}`GibbsExcessLiquid <thermo.phases.GibbsExcessLiquid>`
- a {obj}`IAPWS95Liquid <thermo.phases.IAPWS95Liquid>` and a {obj}`IAPWS95Gas <thermo.phases.IAPWS95Gas>`
- a {obj}`CoolPropLiquid <thermo.phases.CoolPropLiquid>` and a {obj}`CoolPropGas <thermo.phases.CoolPropGas>`

Additional information that can be provided in the
{obj}`ChemicalConstantsPackage <thermo.chemical_package.ChemicalConstantsPackage>`
object and {obj}`PropertyCorrelationsPackage <thermo.chemical_package.PropertyCorrelationsPackage>`
object that may help convergence is:

- `Tc`, `Pc`, `omega`, `Tb`, and `atoms`
- Gas heat capacity correlations
- Liquid molar volume correlations
- Heat of vaporization correlations

# Examples

Create all the necessary objects using all of the default parameters for
decane and do a flash at 300 K and 1 bar:

```pycon
>>> from thermo import ChemicalConstantsPackage, PRMIX, CEOSLiquid, CEOSGas, FlashPureVLS
>>> constants, correlations = ChemicalConstantsPackage.from_IDs(['decane'])
>>> eos_kwargs = dict(Tcs=constants.Tcs, Pcs=constants.Pcs, omegas=constants.omegas)
>>> liquid = CEOSLiquid(PRMIX, HeatCapacityGases=correlations.HeatCapacityGases, eos_kwargs=eos_kwargs)
>>> gas = CEOSGas(PRMIX, HeatCapacityGases=correlations.HeatCapacityGases, eos_kwargs=eos_kwargs)
>>> flasher = FlashPureVLS(constants, correlations, gas=gas, liquids=[liquid], solids=[])
>>> print(flasher.flash(T=300, P=1e5))
<EquilibriumState, T=300.0000, P=100000.0000, zs=[1.0], betas=[1.0], phases=[<CEOSLiquid, T=300 K, P=100000 Pa>]>
```

Working with steam:

```pycon
>>> from thermo import FlashPureVLS, IAPWS95Liquid, IAPWS95Gas, iapws_constants, iapws_correlations
>>> liquid = IAPWS95Liquid(T=300, P=1e5, zs=[1])
>>> gas = IAPWS95Gas(T=300, P=1e5, zs=[1])
>>> flasher = FlashPureVLS(iapws_constants, iapws_correlations, gas, [liquid], [])
>>> PT = flasher.flash(T=800.0, P=1e7)
>>> PT.rho_mass()
29.1071839176
>>> print(flasher.flash(T=600, VF=.5))
<EquilibriumState, T=600.0000, P=12344824.3572, zs=[1.0], betas=[0.5, 0.5], phases=[<IAPWS95Gas, T=600 K, P=1.23448e+07 Pa>, <IAPWS95Liquid, T=600 K, P=1.23448e+07 Pa>]>
>>> print(flasher.flash(T=600.0, H=50802))
<EquilibriumState, T=600.0000, P=10000469.1288, zs=[1.0], betas=[1.0], phases=[<IAPWS95Gas, T=600 K, P=1.00005e+07 Pa>]>
>>> print(flasher.flash(P=1e7, S=104.))
<EquilibriumState, T=599.6790, P=10000000.0000, zs=[1.0], betas=[1.0], phases=[<IAPWS95Gas, T=599.679 K, P=1e+07 Pa>]>
>>> print(flasher.flash(V=.00061, U=55850))
<EquilibriumState, T=800.5922, P=10144789.0899, zs=[1.0], betas=[1.0], phases=[<IAPWS95Gas, T=800.592 K, P=1.01448e+07 Pa>]>
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Poling, Bruce E., John M. Prausnitz, and John P. O\`Connell. The
    Properties of Gases and Liquids. 5th edition. New York: McGraw-Hill
    Professional, 2000.

[^footnote-2]: Gmehling, Jürgen, Michael Kleiber, Bärbel Kolbe, and Jürgen Rarey.
    Chemical Thermodynamics for Process Simulation. John Wiley & Sons, 2019.

This module contains classes and functions for performing flash calculations.

For reporting bugs, adding feature requests, or submitting pull requests,
please use the [GitHub issue tracker](https://github.com/CalebBell/thermo/).

```{contents}
:local: true
```

# Main Interfaces

## Pure Components

```
.. autoclass:: FlashPureVLS
   :show-inheritance:
   :members: __init__
   :exclude-members: __init__
```

## Vapor-Liquid Systems

```
.. autoclass:: FlashVL
   :show-inheritance:
   :members: __init__
   :exclude-members: __init__
```

## Vapor and Multiple Liquid Systems

```
.. autoclass:: FlashVLN
   :show-inheritance:
   :members: __init__
   :exclude-members: __init__
```

## Base Flash Class

```
.. autoclass:: Flash
   :show-inheritance:
   :members: flash, plot_TP, plot_PT, plot_ternary, plot_Txy, plot_Pxy, plot_xy
   :exclude-members:

```

# Specific Flash Algorithms

It is recommended to use the Flash classes, which are designed to have generic
interfaces. The implemented specific flash algorithms may be changed in the
future, but reading their source code may be helpful for instructive purposes.
