    species, [1/Pa]

Class for solving the ideal gas [^footnote-1] [^footnote-2] equation of state for a
mixture of any number of compounds. Subclasses {obj}`thermo.eos.IG`. Solves
the EOS on initialization.
Two of `T`, `P`, and `V` are needed to solve the EOS.

$$
P =\frac{RT}{V}
$$

# Parameters

zs

: Overall mole fractions of all species, \[-\]

T

: Temperature, \[K\]

P

: Pressure, \[Pa\]

V

: Molar volume, \[m^3/mol\]

Tcs

: Critical temperatures of all compounds, \[K\]

Pcs

: Critical pressures of all compounds, \[Pa\]

omegas

: Acentric factors of all compounds - Not used in this equation of
  state!, \[-\]

kijs

: n\*n size list of lists with binary interaction parameters for the
  Van der Waals mixing rules, default all 0 and not used\[-\]

fugacities

: Whether or not to calculate fugacity related values (phis, log phis,
  and fugacities); default True, \[-\]

only_l

: When true, if there is a liquid and a vapor root, only the liquid
  root (and properties) will be set; default False, \[-\]

only_g

: When true, if there is a liquid and a vapor root, only the vapor
  root (and properties) will be set; default False, \[-\]

# Examples

T-P initialization, nitrogen-methane at 115 K and 1 MPa:

```pycon
>>> eos = IGMIX(T=115, P=1E6, Tcs=[126.1, 190.6], Pcs=[33.94E5, 46.04E5], omegas=[0.04, .008], zs=[0.5, 0.5])
>>> eos.phase, eos.V_g
('g', 0.0009561632010876225)
```

# Notes

Many properties of this object are zero. Many of the arguments are not used
and are provided for consistency only.

# References

[^footnote-1]: Walas, Stanley M. Phase Equilibria in Chemical Engineering.
    Butterworth-Heinemann, 1985.

[^footnote-2]: Poling, Bruce E. The Properties of Gases and Liquids. 5th
    edition. New York: McGraw-Hill Professional, 2000.

classes.name: thermo.eos_mix.RKMIX

methods.name: thermo.eos_mix.RKMIX.a_alphas_vectorized
documentation:
Method to calculate the pure-component `a_alphas` for the RK EOS.
This vectorized implementation is added for extra speed.

$$
a\alpha = \frac{a}{\sqrt{\frac{T}{Tc}}}
$$

# Parameters

T

: Temperature, \[K\]

# Returns

a_alphas

: Coefficient calculated by EOS-specific method, \[J^2/mol^2/Pa\]

# Examples

```pycon
>>> eos = RKMIX(T=115, P=1E6, Tcs=[126.1, 190.6], Pcs=[33.94E5, 46.04E5], omegas=[0.04, 0.011], zs=[0.5, 0.5], kijs=[[0,0],[0,0]])
>>> eos.a_alphas_vectorized(115)
[0.1449810919468, 0.30019773677]
```

methods.name: thermo.eos_mix.RKMIX.a_alpha_and_derivatives_vectorized
documentation:
Method to calculate the pure-component `a_alphas` and their first
and second derivatives for the RK EOS. This vectorized implementation
is added for extra speed.

$$
a\alpha = \frac{a}{\sqrt{\frac{T}{Tc}}}
$$

$$
\frac{d a\alpha}{dT} = - \frac{a}{2 T\sqrt{\frac{T}{Tc}}}
$$

$$
\frac{d^2 a\alpha}{dT^2} = \frac{3 a}{4 T^{2}\sqrt{\frac{T}{Tc}}}
$$

# Parameters

T

: Temperature, \[K\]

# Returns

a_alphas

: Coefficient calculated by EOS-specific method, \[J^2/mol^2/Pa\]

da_alpha_dTs

: Temperature derivative of coefficient calculated by EOS-specific
  method, \[J^2/mol^2/Pa/K\]

d2a_alpha_dT2s

: Second temperature derivative of coefficient calculated by
  EOS-specific method, \[J^2/mol^2/Pa/K\*\*2\]

# Examples

```pycon
>>> eos = RKMIX(T=115, P=1E6, Tcs=[126.1, 190.6], Pcs=[33.94E5, 46.04E5], omegas=[0.04, 0.011], zs=[0.5, 0.5], kijs=[[0,0],[0,0]])
>>> eos.a_alpha_and_derivatives_vectorized(115)
([0.1449810919468, 0.30019773677], [-0.000630352573681, -0.00130520755121], [8.2219900915e-06, 1.7024446320e-05])
```

methods.name: thermo.eos_mix.RKMIX.ddelta_dzs
documentation:
Helper method for calculating the composition derivatives of
`delta`. Note this is independent of the phase.

$$
\left(\frac{\partial \delta}{\partial x_i}\right)_{T, P, x_{i\ne j}} = b_i
$$

# Returns

ddelta_dzs

: Composition derivative of `delta` of each component, \[m^3/mol\]

# Notes

This derivative is checked numerically.

methods.name: thermo.eos_mix.RKMIX.ddelta_dns
documentation:
Helper method for calculating the mole number derivatives of
`delta`. Note this is independent of the phase.

$$
\left(\frac{\partial \delta}{\partial n_i}\right)_{T, P, n_{i\ne j}} = (b_i - b)
$$

# Returns

ddelta_dns

: Mole number derivative of `delta` of each component, \[m^3/mol^2\]

# Notes

This derivative is checked numerically.

methods.name: thermo.eos_mix.RKMIX.d2delta_dzizjs
documentation:
Helper method for calculating the second composition derivatives (hessian) of
`delta`. Note this is independent of the phase.

$$
\left(\frac{\partial^2 \delta}{\partial x_i\partial x_j}\right)_{T, P, x_{k\ne i,j}} = 0
$$

# Returns

d2delta_dzizjs

: Second Composition derivative of `delta` of each component, \[m^3/mol\]

# Notes

This derivative is checked numerically.

methods.name: thermo.eos_mix.RKMIX.d2delta_dninjs
documentation:
Helper method for calculating the second mole number derivatives (hessian) of
`delta`. Note this is independent of the phase.

$$
\left(\frac{\partial^2 \delta}{\partial n_i \partial n_j}\right)_{T, P, n_{k\ne i,j}} = 2b - b_i - b_j
$$

# Returns

d2delta_dninjs

: Second mole number derivative of `delta` of each component, \[m^3/mol^3\]

# Notes

This derivative is checked numerically.

methods.name: thermo.eos_mix.RKMIX.d3delta_dninjnks
documentation:
Helper method for calculating the third partial mole number
derivatives of `delta`. Note this is independent of the phase.

$$
\left(\frac{\partial^3 \delta}{\partial n_i \partial n_j \partial n_k } \right)_{T, P, n_{m \ne i,j,k}} = 2(-3b + b_i + b_j + b_k)
$$

# Returns

d3delta_dninjnks

: Third mole number derivative of `delta` of each component,
  \[m^3/mol^4\]

# Notes

This derivative is checked numerically.

Class for solving the Redlich Kwong [^footnote-1] [^footnote-2] cubic equation of state for a
mixture of any number of compounds. Subclasses {obj}`thermo.eos.RK` . Solves the EOS on
initialization and calculates fugacities for all components in all phases.
Two of `T`, `P`, and `V` are needed to solve the EOS.

$$
P =\frac{RT}{V-b}-\frac{a}{V\sqrt{T}(V+b)}
$$

$$
a = \sum_i \sum_j z_i z_j {a}_{ij}
$$

$$
b = \sum_i z_i b_i
$$

$$
a_{ij} = (1-k_{ij})\sqrt{a_{i}a_{j}}
$$

$$
a_i =\left(\frac{R^2(T_{c,i})^{2}}{9(\sqrt[3]{2}-1)P_{c,i}} \right) =\frac{0.42748\cdot R^2(T_{c,i})^{2}}{P_{c,i}}
$$

$$
b_i=\left( \frac{(\sqrt[3]{2}-1)}{3}\right)\frac{RT_{c,i}}{P_{c,i}} =\frac{0.08664\cdot R T_{c,i}}{P_{c,i}}
$$

# Parameters

Tcs

: Critical temperatures of all compounds, \[K\]

Pcs

: Critical pressures of all compounds, \[Pa\]

zs

: Overall mole fractions of all species, \[-\]

kijs

: n\*n size list of lists with binary interaction parameters for the
  Van der Waals mixing rules, default all 0 \[-\]

T

: Temperature, \[K\]

P

: Pressure, \[Pa\]

V

: Molar volume, \[m^3/mol\]

omegas

: Acentric factors of all compounds - Not used in this equation of
  state!, \[-\]

fugacities

: Whether or not to calculate fugacity related values (phis, log phis,
  and fugacities); default True, \[-\]

only_l

: When true, if there is a liquid and a vapor root, only the liquid
  root (and properties) will be set; default False, \[-\]

only_g

: When true, if there is a liquid and a vapor root, only the vapor
  root (and properties) will be set; default False, \[-\]

# Examples

T-P initialization, nitrogen-methane at 115 K and 1 MPa:

```pycon
>>> eos = RKMIX(T=115, P=1E6, Tcs=[126.1, 190.6], Pcs=[33.94E5, 46.04E5], zs=[0.5, 0.5], kijs=[[0,0],[0,0]])
>>> eos.V_l, eos.V_g
(4.048414781e-05, 0.00070060605863)
```

# Notes

The PV solution for `T` is iterative.

# References

[^footnote-1]: Walas, Stanley M. Phase Equilibria in Chemical Engineering.
    Butterworth-Heinemann, 1985.

[^footnote-2]: Poling, Bruce E. The Properties of Gases and Liquids. 5th
    edition. New York: McGraw-Hill Professional, 2000.

classes.name: thermo.eos_mix.PRMIX

methods.name: thermo.eos_mix.PRMIX.a_alphas_vectorized
documentation:
Method to calculate the pure-component `a_alphas` for the PR EOS.
This vectorized implementation is added for extra speed.

$$
a\alpha = a \left(\kappa \left(- \frac{T^{0.5}}{Tc^{0.5}} + 1\right) + 1\right)^{2}
$$

# Parameters

T

: Temperature, \[K\]

# Returns

a_alphas

: Coefficient calculated by EOS-specific method, \[J^2/mol^2/Pa\]

methods.name: thermo.eos_mix.PRMIX.a_alpha_and_derivatives_vectorized
documentation:
Method to calculate the pure-component `a_alphas` and their first
and second derivatives for the PR EOS. This vectorized implementation
is added for extra speed.

$$
a\alpha = a \left(\kappa \left(- \frac{T^{0.5}}{Tc^{0.5}} + 1\right) + 1\right)^{2}
$$

$$
\frac{d a\alpha}{dT} = - \frac{1.0 a \kappa}{T^{0.5} Tc^{0.5}} \left(\kappa \left(- \frac{T^{0.5}}{Tc^{0.5}} + 1\right) + 1\right)
$$

$$
\frac{d^2 a\alpha}{dT^2} = 0.5 a \kappa \left(- \frac{1}{T^{1.5} Tc^{0.5}} \left(\kappa \left(\frac{T^{0.5}}{Tc^{0.5}} - 1\right) - 1\right) + \frac{\kappa}{T^{1.0} Tc^{1.0}}\right)
$$

# Parameters

T

: Temperature, \[K\]

# Returns

a_alphas

: Coefficient calculated by EOS-specific method, \[J^2/mol^2/Pa\]

da_alpha_dTs

: Temperature derivative of coefficient calculated by EOS-specific
  method, \[J^2/mol^2/Pa/K\]

d2a_alpha_dT2s

: Second temperature derivative of coefficient calculated by
  EOS-specific method, \[J^2/mol^2/Pa/K\*\*2\]

methods.name: thermo.eos_mix.PRMIX.d3a_alpha_dT3
documentation:
Method to calculate approximately the third temperature derivative
of `a_alpha` for the PR EOS. A rigorous calculation has not been
implemented.

# Parameters

T

: Temperature, \[K\]

# Returns

d3a_alpha_dT3

: Third temperature derivative $a \alpha$, \[J^2/mol^2/Pa/K^3\]

methods.name: thermo.eos_mix.PRMIX.d3a_alpha_dT3_vectorized
documentation:
Method to calculate the third temperature derivative of
pure-component `a_alphas` for the PR EOS. This vectorized implementation
is added for extra speed.

Parameters
----------
T : float
    Temperature, [K]

Returns
-------
d3a_alpha_dT3s : list[float]
    Third temperature derivative of coefficient calculated by
    EOS-specific method, [J^2/mol^2/Pa/K^3]

methods.name: thermo.eos_mix.PRMIX.fugacity_coefficients
documentation:
Literature formula for calculating fugacity coefficients for each
species in a mixture. Verified numerically. Applicable to most
derivatives of the Peng-Robinson equation of state as well.
Called by {obj}`fugacities <GCEOSMIX.fugacities>` on initialization, or by a solver routine
which is performing a flash calculation.

$$
\ln \hat \phi_i = \frac{B_i}{B}(Z-1)-\ln(Z-B) + \frac{A}{2\sqrt{2}B} \left[\frac{B_i}{B} - \frac{2}{a\alpha}\sum_i y_i(a\alpha)_{ij}\right] \ln\left[\frac{Z + (1+\sqrt{2})B}{Z-(\sqrt{2}-1)B}\right]
$$

$$
A = \frac{(a\alpha)P}{R^2 T^2}
$$

$$
B = \frac{b P}{RT}
$$

# Parameters

Z

: Compressibility of the mixture for a desired phase, \[-\]

# Returns

log_phis

: Log fugacity coefficient for each species, \[-\]

methods.name: thermo.eos_mix.PRMIX.dlnphis_dT
documentation:
Formula for calculating the temperature derivaitve of
log fugacity coefficients for each species in a mixture for the
Peng-Robinson equation of state. Verified numerically.

$$
\left(\frac{\partial \ln \phi_i}{\partial T}\right)_{P, nj \ne i}
$$

# Parameters

phase

: One of 'l' or 'g', \[-\]

# Returns

dlnphis_dT

: Temperature derivatives of log fugacity coefficient for each species,
  \[1/K\]

# Notes

This expression was derived using SymPy and optimized with the `cse`
technique.

methods.name: thermo.eos_mix.PRMIX.dlnphis_dP
documentation:
Generic formula for calculating the pressure derivaitve of
log fugacity coefficients for each species in a mixture for the
Peng-Robinson EOS. Verified numerically.

$$
\left(\frac{\partial \ln \phi_i}{\partial P}\right)_{T, nj \ne i}
$$

# Parameters

phase

: One of 'l' or 'g', \[-\]

# Returns

dlnphis_dP

: Pressure derivatives of log fugacity coefficient for each species,
  \[1/Pa\]

# Notes

This expression was derived using SymPy and optimized with the `cse`
technique.

methods.name: thermo.eos_mix.PRMIX.dlnphis_dzs
documentation:
Calculate and return the mole fraction derivaitves of
log fugacity coefficients for each species in a mixture. This formula
is specific to the Peng-Robinson equation of state.

$$
\left(\frac{\partial \ln \phi_i}{\partial z_i}\right)_{P, z_{j \ne i}}
$$

# Parameters

Z

: Compressibility of the mixture for a desired phase, \[-\]

# Returns

dlnphis_dzs

: Mole fraction derivatives of log fugacity coefficient for each
  species (such that the mole fractions do not sum to 1), \[-\]

# Notes

This formula is from [^footnote-1] but is validated to match the generic
implementation.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> kijs = [[0, 0.00076, 0.00171], [0.00076, 0, 0.00061], [0.00171, 0.00061, 0]]
>>> eos = PRMIX(Tcs=[469.7, 507.4, 540.3], zs=[0.8168, 0.1501, 0.0331], omegas=[0.249, 0.305, 0.349], Pcs=[3.369E6, 3.012E6, 2.736E6], T=322.29, P=101325, kijs=kijs)
>>> eos.dlnphis_dzs(eos.Z_l)
[[0.009938069276, 0.0151503498382, 0.018297235797], [-0.038517738793, -0.05958926042, -0.068438990795], [-0.07057106923, -0.10363920720, -0.14116283024]]
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Chang, Yih-Bor. "Development and Application of an Equation of
    State Compositional Simulator" 1990.
    <https://repositories.lib.utexas.edu/handle/2152/80585>.

methods.name: thermo.eos_mix.PRMIX.ddelta_dzs
documentation:
Helper method for calculating the composition derivatives of
`delta`. Note this is independent of the phase.

$$
\left(\frac{\partial \delta}{\partial x_i}\right)_{T, P, x_{i\ne j}} = 2 b_i
$$

# Returns

ddelta_dzs

: Composition derivative of `delta` of each component, \[m^3/mol\]

# Notes

This derivative is checked numerically.

methods.name: thermo.eos_mix.PRMIX.ddelta_dns
documentation:
Helper method for calculating the mole number derivatives of
`delta`. Note this is independent of the phase.

$$
\left(\frac{\partial \delta}{\partial n_i}\right)_{T, P, n_{i\ne j}} = 2 (b_i - b)
$$

# Returns

ddelta_dns

: Mole number derivative of `delta` of each component, \[m^3/mol^2\]

# Notes

This derivative is checked numerically.

methods.name: thermo.eos_mix.PRMIX.d2delta_dzizjs
documentation:
Helper method for calculating the second composition derivatives (hessian) of
`delta`. Note this is independent of the phase.

$$
\left(\frac{\partial^2 \delta}{\partial x_i\partial x_j}\right)_{T, P, x_{k\ne i,j}} = 0
$$

# Returns

d2delta_dzizjs

: Second Composition derivative of `delta` of each component, \[m^3/mol\]

# Notes

This derivative is checked numerically.

methods.name: thermo.eos_mix.PRMIX.d2delta_dninjs
documentation:
Helper method for calculating the second mole number derivatives (hessian) of
`delta`. Note this is independent of the phase.

$$
\left(\frac{\partial^2 \delta}{\partial n_i \partial n_j}\right)_{T, P, n_{k\ne i,j}} = 4b - 2b_i - 2b_j
$$

# Returns

d2delta_dninjs

: Second mole number derivative of `delta` of each component, \[m^3/mol^3\]

# Notes

This derivative is checked numerically.

methods.name: thermo.eos_mix.PRMIX.d3delta_dninjnks
documentation:
Helper method for calculating the third partial mole number
derivatives of `delta`. Note this is independent of the phase.

$$
\left(\frac{\partial^3 \delta}{\partial n_i \partial n_j \partial n_k } \right)_{T, P, n_{m \ne i,j,k}} = 4(-3b + b_i + b_j + b_k)
$$

# Returns

d3delta_dninjnks

: Third mole number derivative of `delta` of each component,
  \[m^3/mol^4\]

# Notes

This derivative is checked numerically.

methods.name: thermo.eos_mix.PRMIX.depsilon_dzs
documentation:
Helper method for calculating the composition derivatives of
`epsilon`. Note this is independent of the phase.

$$
\left(\frac{\partial \epsilon}{\partial x_i}\right)_{T, P, x_{i\ne j}} = -2 b_i\cdot b
$$

# Returns

depsilon_dzs

: Composition derivative of `epsilon` of each component, \[m^6/mol^2\]

# Notes

This derivative is checked numerically.

methods.name: thermo.eos_mix.PRMIX.depsilon_dns
documentation:
Helper method for calculating the mole number derivatives of
`epsilon`. Note this is independent of the phase.

$$
\left(\frac{\partial \epsilon}{\partial n_i}\right)_{T, P, n_{i\ne j}} = 2b(b - b_i)
$$

# Returns

depsilon_dns

: Composition derivative of `epsilon` of each component, \[m^6/mol^3\]

# Notes

This derivative is checked numerically.

methods.name: thermo.eos_mix.PRMIX.d2epsilon_dzizjs
documentation:
Helper method for calculating the second composition derivatives (hessian)
of `epsilon`. Note this is independent of the phase.

$$
\left(\frac{\partial^2 \epsilon}{\partial x_i \partial x_j}\right)_{T, P, x_{k\ne i,j}} =  2 b_i b_j
$$

# Returns

d2epsilon_dzizjs

: Second composition derivative of `epsilon` of each component, \[m^6/mol^2\]

# Notes

This derivative is checked numerically.

methods.name: thermo.eos_mix.PRMIX.d2epsilon_dninjs
documentation:
Helper method for calculating the second mole number derivatives
(hessian) of `epsilon`. Note this is independent of the phase.

$$
\left(\frac{\partial^2 \epsilon}{\partial n_i n_j}\right)_{T, P, n_{k\ne i,j}}  = -2b(2b - b_i - b_j) - 2(b - b_i)(b - b_j)
$$

# Returns

d2epsilon_dninjs

: Second mole number derivative of `epsilon` of each component,
  \[m^6/mol^4\]

# Notes

This derivative is checked numerically.

methods.name: thermo.eos_mix.PRMIX.d3epsilon_dninjnks
documentation:
Helper method for calculating the third partial mole number
derivatives of `epsilon`. Note this is independent of the phase.

$$
\left(\frac{\partial^3 \epsilon}{\partial n_i \partial n_j \partial n_k } \right)_{T, P, n_{m \ne i,j,k}} = 24b^2 - 12b(b_i + b_j + b_k)      + 4(b_i b_j + b_i b_k + b_j b_k)
$$

# Returns

d3epsilon_dninjnks

: Third mole number derivative of `epsilon` of each component,
  \[m^6/mol^5\]

# Notes

This derivative is checked numerically.

Class for solving the Peng-Robinson [^footnote-1] [^footnote-2] cubic equation of state
for a mixture of any number of compounds. Subclasses `PR`. Solves the EOS
on initialization and calculates fugacities for all components in all
phases.

Two of `T`, `P`, and `V` are needed to solve the EOS.

$$
P = \frac{RT}{v-b}-\frac{a\alpha(T)}{v(v+b)+b(v-b)}
$$

$$
a \alpha = \sum_i \sum_j z_i z_j {(a\alpha)}_{ij}
$$

$$
(a\alpha)_{ij} = (1-k_{ij})\sqrt{(a\alpha)_{i}(a\alpha)_{j}}
$$

$$
b = \sum_i z_i b_i
$$

$$
a_i=0.45724\frac{R^2T_{c,i}^2}{P_{c,i}}
$$

$$
b_i=0.07780\frac{RT_{c,i}}{P_{c,i}}
$$

$$
\alpha(T)_i=[1+\kappa_i(1-\sqrt{T_{r,i}})]^2
$$

$$
\kappa_i=0.37464+1.54226\omega_i-0.26992\omega^2_i
$$

# Parameters

Tcs

: Critical temperatures of all compounds, \[K\]

Pcs

: Critical pressures of all compounds, \[Pa\]

omegas

: Acentric factors of all compounds, \[-\]

zs

: Overall mole fractions of all species, \[-\]

kijs

: n\*n size list of lists with binary interaction parameters for the
  Van der Waals mixing rules, default all 0 \[-\]

T

: Temperature, \[K\]

P

: Pressure, \[Pa\]

V

: Molar volume, \[m^3/mol\]

fugacities

: Whether or not to calculate fugacity related values (phis, log phis,
  and fugacities); default True, \[-\]

only_l

: When true, if there is a liquid and a vapor root, only the liquid
  root (and properties) will be set; default False, \[-\]

only_g

: When true, if there is a liquid and a vapor root, only the vapor
  root (and properties) will be set; default False, \[-\]

# Examples

T-P initialization, nitrogen-methane at 115 K and 1 MPa:

```pycon
>>> eos = PRMIX(T=115, P=1E6, Tcs=[126.1, 190.6], Pcs=[33.94E5, 46.04E5], omegas=[0.04, 0.011], zs=[0.5, 0.5], kijs=[[0,0],[0,0]])
>>> eos.V_l, eos.V_g
(3.6257362939e-05, 0.00070066592313)
>>> eos.fugacities_l, eos.fugacities_g
([793860.83821, 73468.552253], [436530.92470, 358114.63827])
```

# Notes

For P-V initializations, a numerical solver is used to find T.

# References

[^footnote-1]: Peng, Ding-Yu, and Donald B. Robinson. "A New Two-Constant Equation
    of State." Industrial & Engineering Chemistry Fundamentals 15, no. 1
    (February 1, 1976): 59-64. doi:10.1021/i160057a011.

[^footnote-2]: Robinson, Donald B., Ding-Yu Peng, and Samuel Y-K Chung. "The
    Development of the Peng - Robinson Equation and Its Application to Phase
    Equilibrium in a System Containing Methanol." Fluid Phase Equilibria 24,
    no. 1 (January 1, 1985): 25-41. doi:10.1016/0378-3812(85)87035-7.

classes.name: thermo.eos_mix.PRMIXTranslated

methods.name: thermo.eos_mix.PRMIXTranslated.ddelta_dzs
documentation:
Helper method for calculating the composition derivatives of
`delta`. Note this is independent of the phase. $b^0$ refers to
the original `b` parameter not involving any translation.

$$
\left(\frac{\partial \delta}{\partial x_i}\right)_{T, P, x_{i\ne j}} = 2 (c_i + b^0_i)
$$

# Returns

ddelta_dzs

: Composition derivative of `delta` of each component, \[m^3/mol\]

# Notes

This derivative is checked numerically.

methods.name: thermo.eos_mix.PRMIXTranslated.ddelta_dns
documentation:
Helper method for calculating the mole number derivatives of
`delta`. Note this is independent of the phase. $b^0$ refers to
the original `b` parameter not involving any translation.

$$
\left(\frac{\partial \delta}{\partial n_i}\right)_{T, P, n_{i\ne j}} = 2 (c_i + b^0_i) - \delta
$$

# Returns

ddelta_dns

: Mole number derivative of `delta` of each component, \[m^3/mol^2\]

# Notes

This derivative is checked numerically.

methods.name: thermo.eos_mix.PRMIXTranslated.d2delta_dninjs
documentation:
Helper method for calculating the second mole number derivatives (hessian) of
`delta`. Note this is independent of the phase. $b^0$ refers to
the original `b` parameter not involving any translation.

$$
\left(\frac{\partial^2 \delta}{\partial n_i \partial n_j}\right)_{T, P, n_{k\ne i,j}} = 2\left(\delta - b^0_i - b^0_j - c_i - c_j \right)
$$

# Returns

d2delta_dninjs

: Second mole number derivative of `delta` of each component, \[m^3/mol^3\]

# Notes

This derivative is checked numerically.

methods.name: thermo.eos_mix.PRMIXTranslated.d3delta_dninjnks
documentation:
Helper method for calculating the third partial mole number
derivatives of `delta`. Note this is independent of the phase. $b^0$ refers to
the original `b` parameter not involving any translation.

$$
\left(\frac{\partial^3 \delta}{\partial n_i \partial n_j \partial n_k } \right)_{T, P, n_{m \ne i,j,k}} = 4\left(b^0_i + b^0_j + b^0_k + c_i + c_j     + c_k \right) - 6 \delta
$$

# Returns

d3delta_dninjnks

: Third mole number derivative of `delta` of each component,
  \[m^3/mol^4\]

# Notes

This derivative is checked numerically.

methods.name: thermo.eos_mix.PRMIXTranslated.depsilon_dzs
documentation:
Helper method for calculating the composition derivatives of
`epsilon`. Note this is independent of the phase. $b^0$ refers to
the original `b` parameter not involving any translation.

$$
\left(\frac{\partial \epsilon}{\partial x_i}\right)_{T, P, x_{i\ne j}} = c_i(2b^0_i + c) + c(2b^0_i + c_i) - 2b^0 b^0_i
$$

# Returns

depsilon_dzs

: Composition derivative of `epsilon` of each component, \[m^6/mol^2\]

# Notes

This derivative is checked numerically.

methods.name: thermo.eos_mix.PRMIXTranslated.depsilon_dns
documentation:
Helper method for calculating the mole number derivatives of
`epsilon`. Note this is independent of the phase. $b^0$ refers to
the original `b` parameter not involving any translation.

$$
\left(\frac{\partial \epsilon}{\partial n_i}\right)_{T, P, n_{i\ne j}} = 2b^0(b^0 - b^0_i) - c(2b^0 - 2b_i^0 + c - c_i) - (c - c_i)(2b^0 + c)
$$

# Returns

depsilon_dns

: Composition derivative of `epsilon` of each component, \[m^6/mol^3\]

# Notes

This derivative is checked numerically.

methods.name: thermo.eos_mix.PRMIXTranslated.d2epsilon_dzizjs
documentation:
Helper method for calculating the second composition derivatives (hessian)
of `epsilon`. Note this is independent of the phase.

$$
\left(\frac{\partial^2 \epsilon}{\partial x_i \partial x_j}\right)_{T, P, x_{k\ne i,j}} =  -2 b^0_i b^0_j + 2b^0_i c_j + 2b^0_j c_i + 2c_i c_j
$$

# Returns

d2epsilon_dzizjs

: Second composition derivative of `epsilon` of each component, \[m^6/mol^2\]

# Notes

This derivative is checked numerically.

methods.name: thermo.eos_mix.PRMIXTranslated.d2epsilon_dninjs
documentation:
Helper method for calculating the second mole number derivatives (hessian) of
`epsilon`. Note this is independent of the phase.

$$
\left(\frac{\partial^2 \epsilon}{\partial n_i n_j}\right)_{T, P, n_{k\ne i,j}} = -2b^0(2b^0 - b_i^0 - b_j^0) + c(4b^0 - 2b^0_i - 2b^0_j + 2c - c_i - c_j) -2(b^0 - b_i^0)(b^0 - b^0_j) + (c - c_i)(2b^0 - 2b^0_j - c_j + c) + (c - c_j)(2b^0 - 2b^0_i - c_i + c) + (2b^0 + c)(2c-c_i - c_j)
$$

# Returns

d2epsilon_dninjs

: Second mole number derivative of `epsilon` of each component, \[m^6/mol^4\]

# Notes

This derivative is checked numerically.

methods.name: thermo.eos_mix.PRMIXTranslated.d3epsilon_dninjnks
documentation:
Helper method for calculating the third partial mole number
derivatives of `epsilon`. Note this is independent of the phase.

$$
\left(\frac{\partial^3 \epsilon}{\partial n_i \partial n_j \partial n_k } \right)_{T, P, n_{m \ne i,j,k}} = 4b^0(3b^0 - b_i^0 - b_j^0 - b_k^0)     -2c(6b^0 - 2(b_i^0 + b_j^0 + b_k^0) + 3c - (c_i + c_j + c_k))     +2(b^0-b_i^0)(2b^0 - b_j^0 - b_k^0) + 2(b^0 - b^0_j)(2b^0 - b_i^0 - b_k^0)     +2(b^0-b^0_k)(2b^0 - b^0_i-b^0_j) -(c-c_i)(4b^0 - 2b^0_j - 2b^0_k + 2c - c_j - c_k) -(c-c_j)(4b^0 - 2b^0_i - 2b^0_k + 2c - c_i - c_k) -(c-c_k)(4b^0 - 2b^0_j - 2b^0_i + 2c - c_j - c_i) -2(c + 2b^0)(3c - c_i - c_j - c_k) -(2c - c_i - c_j)(2b^0 + c - 2b^0_k - c_k) -(2c - c_i - c_k)(2b^0 + c - 2b^0_j - c_j) -(2c - c_j - c_k)(2b^0 + c - 2b^0_i - c_i)
$$

# Returns

d3epsilon_dninjnks

: Third mole number derivative of `epsilon` of each component,
  \[m^6/mol^5\]

# Notes

This derivative is checked numerically.

class_variables.name: thermo.eos_mix.PRMIXTranslated.d3epsilon_dzizjzks
documentation: Zeros

Class for solving the Peng-Robinson [^footnote-1] [^footnote-2] translated cubic equation
of state for a mixture of any number of compounds. Solves the EOS
on initialization and calculates fugacities for all components in all
phases.

Two of `T`, `P`, and `V` are needed to solve the EOS.

$$
P = \frac{RT}{v + c - b} - \frac{a\alpha(T)}{(v+c)(v + c + b)+b(v + c - b)}
$$

$$
a \alpha = \sum_i \sum_j z_i z_j {(a\alpha)}_{ij}
$$

$$
(a\alpha)_{ij} = (1-k_{ij})\sqrt{(a\alpha)_{i}(a\alpha)_{j}}
$$

$$
b = \sum_i z_i b_i
$$

$$
a_i=0.45724\frac{R^2T_{c,i}^2}{P_{c,i}}
$$

$$
b_i=0.07780\frac{RT_{c,i}}{P_{c,i}}
$$

$$
\alpha(T)_i=[1+\kappa_i(1-\sqrt{T_{r,i}})]^2
$$

$$
\kappa_i=0.37464+1.54226\omega_i-0.26992\omega^2_i
$$

# Parameters

Tcs

: Critical temperatures of all compounds, \[K\]

Pcs

: Critical pressures of all compounds, \[Pa\]

omegas

: Acentric factors of all compounds, \[-\]

zs

: Overall mole fractions of all species, \[-\]

kijs

: n\*n size list of lists with binary interaction parameters for the
  Van der Waals mixing rules, default all 0 \[-\]

cs

: Volume translation parameters; always zero in the original
  implementation, \[m^3/mol\]

T

: Temperature, \[K\]

P

: Pressure, \[Pa\]

V

: Molar volume, \[m^3/mol\]

fugacities

: Whether or not to calculate fugacity related values (phis, log phis,
  and fugacities); default True, \[-\]

only_l

: When true, if there is a liquid and a vapor root, only the liquid
  root (and properties) will be set; default False, \[-\]

only_g

: When true, if there is a liquid and a vapor root, only the vapor
  root (and properties) will be set; default False, \[-\]

# Examples

T-P initialization, nitrogen-methane at 115 K and 1 MPa:

```pycon
>>> eos = PRMIXTranslated(T=115, P=1E6, cs=[-4.4e-6, -4.35e-6], Tcs=[126.1, 190.6], Pcs=[33.94E5, 46.04E5], omegas=[0.04, 0.011], zs=[0.2, 0.8], kijs=[[0,0.03],[0.03,0]])
>>> eos.V_l, eos.V_g
(3.9079056337e-05, 0.00060231393016)
>>> eos.fugacities_l, eos.fugacities_g
([442838.8615, 108854.48589], [184396.972, 565531.7709])
```

# Notes

For P-V initializations, a numerical solver is used to find T.

# References

[^footnote-1]: Peng, Ding-Yu, and Donald B. Robinson. "A New Two-Constant Equation
    of State." Industrial & Engineering Chemistry Fundamentals 15, no. 1
    (February 1, 1976): 59-64. doi:10.1021/i160057a011.

[^footnote-2]: Robinson, Donald B., Ding-Yu Peng, and Samuel Y-K Chung. "The
    Development of the Peng - Robinson Equation and Its Application to Phase
    Equilibrium in a System Containing Methanol." Fluid Phase Equilibria 24,
    no. 1 (January 1, 1985): 25-41. doi:10.1016/0378-3812(85)87035-7.

classes.name: thermo.eos_mix.PRMIXTranslatedPPJP
documentation:
Class for solving the Pina-Martinez, Privat, Jaubert,
and Peng revision of the Peng-Robinson equation of state.

Two of `T`, `P`, and `V` are needed to solve the EOS.

$$
P = \frac{RT}{v + c - b} - \frac{a\alpha(T)}{(v+c)(v + c + b)+b(v + c - b)}
$$

$$
a \alpha = \sum_i \sum_j z_i z_j {(a\alpha)}_{ij}
$$

$$
(a\alpha)_{ij} = (1-k_{ij})\sqrt{(a\alpha)_{i}(a\alpha)_{j}}
$$

$$
b = \sum_i z_i b_i
$$

$$
a_i=0.45724\frac{R^2T_{c,i}^2}{P_{c,i}}
$$

$$
b_i=0.07780\frac{RT_{c,i}}{P_{c,i}}
$$

$$
\alpha(T)_i=[1+\kappa_i(1-\sqrt{T_{r,i}})]^2
$$

$$
\kappa_i=0.3919 + 1.4996 \omega - 0.2721\omega^2 + 0.1063\omega^3
$$

# Parameters

Tcs

: Critical temperatures of all compounds, \[K\]

Pcs

: Critical pressures of all compounds, \[Pa\]

omegas

: Acentric factors of all compounds, \[-\]

zs

: Overall mole fractions of all species, \[-\]

kijs

: n\*n size list of lists with binary interaction parameters for the
  Van der Waals mixing rules, default all 0 \[-\]

cs

: Volume translation parameters, \[m^3/mol\]

T

: Temperature, \[K\]

P

: Pressure, \[Pa\]

V

: Molar volume, \[m^3/mol\]

fugacities

: Whether or not to calculate fugacity related values (phis, log phis,
  and fugacities); default True, \[-\]

only_l

: When true, if there is a liquid and a vapor root, only the liquid
  root (and properties) will be set; default False, \[-\]

only_g

: When true, if there is a liquid and a vapor root, only the vapor
  root (and properties) will be set; default False, \[-\]

# Examples

T-P initialization, nitrogen-methane at 115 K and 1 MPa:

```pycon
>>> eos = PRMIXTranslatedPPJP(T=115, P=1E6, cs=[-4.4e-6, -4.35e-6], Tcs=[126.1, 190.6], Pcs=[33.94E5, 46.04E5], omegas=[0.04, 0.011], zs=[0.2, 0.8], kijs=[[0,0.03],[0.03,0]])
>>> eos.V_l, eos.V_g
(3.8989032701e-05, 0.00059686183724)
>>> eos.fugacities_l, eos.fugacities_g
([444791.13707, 104520.280997], [184782.600238, 563352.147])
```

# Notes

For P-V initializations, a numerical solver is used to find T.

# References

[^footnote-1]: Pina-Martinez, Andrés, Romain Privat, Jean-Noël Jaubert, and
    Ding-Yu Peng. "Updated Versions of the Generalized Soave a-Function
    Suitable for the Redlich-Kwong and Peng-Robinson Equations of State."
    Fluid Phase Equilibria, December 7, 2018.
    <https://doi.org/10.1016/j.fluid.2018.12.007>.

classes.name: thermo.eos_mix.PRMIXTranslatedConsistent

methods.name: thermo.eos_mix.PRMIXTranslatedConsistent.__init__
documentation: There is an updated set of correlations - which means a revision flag is needed

Analysis of the Combinations of Property Data That Are Suitable for a Safe Estimation of Consistent Twu α-Function Parameters: Updated Parameter Values for the Translated-Consistent tc-PR and tc-RK Cubic Equations of State

Class for solving the volume translated Le Guennec, Privat, and Jaubert
revision of the Peng-Robinson equation of state according to [^footnote-1].

Two of `T`, `P`, and `V` are needed to solve the EOS.

$$
P = \frac{RT}{v + c - b} - \frac{a\alpha(T)}{(v+c)(v + c + b)+b(v + c - b)}
$$

$$
a \alpha = \sum_i \sum_j z_i z_j {(a\alpha)}_{ij}
$$

$$
(a\alpha)_{ij} = (1-k_{ij})\sqrt{(a\alpha)_{i}(a\alpha)_{j}}
$$

$$
b = \sum_i z_i b_i
$$

$$
a_i=0.45724\frac{R^2T_{c,i}^2}{P_{c,i}}
$$

$$
b_i=0.07780\frac{RT_{c,i}}{P_{c,i}}
$$

$$
\alpha_i = \left(\frac{T}{T_{c}}\right)^{c_{3} \left(c_{2} - 1\right)} e^{c_{1} \left(- \left(\frac{T}{T_{c}} \right)^{c_{2} c_{3}} + 1\right)}
$$

If `c` is not provided, they are estimated as:

$$
c =\frac{R T_c}{P_c}(0.0198\omega - 0.0065)
$$

If `alpha_coeffs` is not provided, the parameters `L` and `M` are estimated
from the acentric factor as follows:

$$
L = 0.1290\omega^2 + 0.6039\omega + 0.0877
$$

$$
M = 0.1760\omega^2 - 0.2600\omega + 0.8884
$$

# Parameters

Tcs

: Critical temperatures of all compounds, \[K\]

Pcs

: Critical pressures of all compounds, \[Pa\]

omegas

: Acentric factors of all compounds, \[-\]

zs

: Overall mole fractions of all species, \[-\]

kijs

: n\*n size list of lists with binary interaction parameters for the
  Van der Waals mixing rules, default all 0 \[-\]

cs

: Volume translation parameters, \[m^3/mol\]

alpha_coeffs

: Coefficients L, M, N (also called C1, C2, C3) of TWU 1991 form, \[-\]

T

: Temperature, \[K\]

P

: Pressure, \[Pa\]

V

: Molar volume, \[m^3/mol\]

fugacities

: Whether or not to calculate fugacity related values (phis, log phis,
  and fugacities); default True, \[-\]

only_l

: When true, if there is a liquid and a vapor root, only the liquid
  root (and properties) will be set; default False, \[-\]

only_g

: When true, if there is a liquid and a vapor root, only the vapor
  root (and properties) will be set; default False, \[-\]

# Examples

T-P initialization, nitrogen-methane at 115 K and 1 MPa:

```pycon
>>> eos = PRMIXTranslatedConsistent(T=115, P=1E6, Tcs=[126.1, 190.6], Pcs=[33.94E5, 46.04E5], omegas=[0.04, 0.011], zs=[0.2, 0.8], kijs=[[0,0.03],[0.03,0]])
>>> eos.V_l, eos.V_g
(3.675235812e-05, 0.00059709319879)
>>> eos.fugacities_l, eos.fugacities_g
([443454.9336, 106184.004057], [184122.74082, 563037.785])
```

# Notes

For P-V initializations, a numerical solver is used to find T.

# References

[^footnote-1]: Le Guennec, Yohann, Romain Privat, and Jean-Noël Jaubert.
    "Development of the Translated-Consistent Tc-PR and Tc-RK Cubic
    Equations of State for a Safe and Accurate Prediction of Volumetric,
    Energetic and Saturation Properties of Pure Compounds in the Sub- and
    Super-Critical Domains." Fluid Phase Equilibria 429 (December 15, 2016):
    301-12. <https://doi.org/10.1016/j.fluid.2016.09.003>.

classes.name: thermo.eos_mix.SRKMIX

methods.name: thermo.eos_mix.SRKMIX.a_alphas_vectorized
documentation:
Method to calculate the pure-component `a_alphas` for the SRK EOS.
This vectorized implementation is added for extra speed.

$$
a\alpha = a \left(m \left(- \sqrt{\frac{T}{Tc}} + 1\right) + 1\right)^{2}
$$

# Parameters

T

: Temperature, \[K\]

# Returns

a_alphas

: Coefficient calculated by EOS-specific method, \[J^2/mol^2/Pa\]

methods.name: thermo.eos_mix.SRKMIX.a_alpha_and_derivatives_vectorized
documentation:
Method to calculate the pure-component `a_alphas` and their first
and second derivatives for the SRK EOS. This vectorized implementation
is added for extra speed.

$$
a\alpha = a \left(m \left(- \sqrt{\frac{T}{Tc}} + 1\right) + 1\right)^{2}
$$

$$
\frac{d a\alpha}{dT} = \frac{a m}{T} \sqrt{\frac{T}{Tc}} \left(m \left(\sqrt{\frac{T}{Tc}} - 1\right) - 1\right)
$$

$$
\frac{d^2 a\alpha}{dT^2} = \frac{a m \sqrt{\frac{T}{Tc}}}{2 T^{2}} \left(m + 1\right)
$$

# Parameters

T

: Temperature, \[K\]

# Returns

a_alphas

: Coefficient calculated by EOS-specific method, \[J^2/mol^2/Pa\]

da_alpha_dTs

: Temperature derivative of coefficient calculated by EOS-specific
  method, \[J^2/mol^2/Pa/K\]

d2a_alpha_dT2s

: Second temperature derivative of coefficient calculated by
  EOS-specific method, \[J^2/mol^2/Pa/K\*\*2\]

methods.name: thermo.eos_mix.SRKMIX.fugacity_coefficients
documentation:
Literature formula for calculating fugacity coefficients for each
species in a mixture. Verified numerically. Applicable to most
derivatives of the SRK equation of state as well.
Called by {obj}`fugacities <GCEOSMIX.fugacities>` on initialization, or by a solver routine
which is performing a flash calculation.

$$
\ln \hat \phi_i = \frac{B_i}{B}(Z-1) - \ln(Z-B) + \frac{A}{B} \left[\frac{B_i}{B} - \frac{2}{a \alpha}\sum_i y_i(a\alpha)_{ij} \right]\ln\left(1+\frac{B}{Z}\right)
$$

$$
A=\frac{a\alpha P}{R^2T^2}
$$

$$
B = \frac{bP}{RT}
$$

# Parameters

Z

: Compressibility of the mixture for a desired phase, \[-\]

# Returns

log_phis

: Log fugacity coefficient for each species, \[-\]

methods.name: thermo.eos_mix.SRKMIX.dlnphis_dT
documentation:
Formula for calculating the temperature derivaitve of
log fugacity coefficients for each species in a mixture for the
SRK equation of state. Verified numerically.

$$
\left(\frac{\partial \ln \phi_i}{\partial T}\right)_{P, nj \ne i}
$$

# Parameters

phase

: One of 'l' or 'g', \[-\]

# Returns

dlnphis_dT

: Temperature derivatives of log fugacity coefficient for each
  species, \[1/K\]

# Notes

This expression was derived using SymPy and optimized with the `cse`
technique.

methods.name: thermo.eos_mix.SRKMIX.dlnphis_dP
documentation:
Generic formula for calculating the pressure derivaitve of
log fugacity coefficients for each species in a mixture for the
SRK EOS. Verified numerically.

$$
\left(\frac{\partial \ln \phi_i}{\partial P}\right)_{T, nj \ne i}
$$

# Parameters

phase

: One of 'l' or 'g', \[-\]

# Returns

dlnphis_dP

: Pressure derivatives of log fugacity coefficient for each species,
  \[1/Pa\]

# Notes

This expression was derived using SymPy and optimized with the `cse`
technique.

Class for solving the Soave-Redlich-Kwong cubic equation of state for a
mixture of any number of compounds. Solves the EOS on
initialization and calculates fugacities for all components in all phases.

The implemented method here is {obj}`fugacity_coefficients <SRKMIX.fugacity_coefficients>`, which implements
the formula for fugacity coefficients in a mixture as given in [^footnote-1].
Two of `T`, `P`, and `V` are needed to solve the EOS.

$$
P = \frac{RT}{V-b} - \frac{a\alpha(T)}{V(V+b)}
$$

$$
a \alpha = \sum_i \sum_j z_i z_j {(a\alpha)}_{ij}
$$

$$
(a\alpha)_{ij} = (1-k_{ij})\sqrt{(a\alpha)_{i}(a\alpha)_{j}}
$$

$$
b = \sum_i z_i b_i
$$

$$
a_i =\left(\frac{R^2(T_{c,i})^{2}}{9(\sqrt[3]{2}-1)P_{c,i}} \right) =\frac{0.42748\cdot R^2(T_{c,i})^{2}}{P_{c,i}}
$$

$$
b_i =\left( \frac{(\sqrt[3]{2}-1)}{3}\right)\frac{RT_{c,i}}{P_{c,i}} =\frac{0.08664\cdot R T_{c,i}}{P_{c,i}}
$$

$$
\alpha(T)_i = \left[1 + m_i\left(1 - \sqrt{\frac{T}{T_{c,i}}}\right)\right]^2
$$

$$
m_i = 0.480 + 1.574\omega_i - 0.176\omega_i^2
$$

# Parameters

Tcs

: Critical temperatures of all compounds, \[K\]

Pcs

: Critical pressures of all compounds, \[Pa\]

omegas

: Acentric factors of all compounds, \[-\]

zs

: Overall mole fractions of all species, \[-\]

kijs

: n\*n size list of lists with binary interaction parameters for the
  Van der Waals mixing rules, default all 0 \[-\]

T

: Temperature, \[K\]

P

: Pressure, \[Pa\]

V

: Molar volume, \[m^3/mol\]

fugacities

: Whether or not to calculate fugacity related values (phis, log phis,
  and fugacities); default True, \[-\]

only_l

: When true, if there is a liquid and a vapor root, only the liquid
  root (and properties) will be set; default False, \[-\]

only_g

: When true, if there is a liquid and a vapor root, only the vapor
  root (and properties) will be set; default False, \[-\]

# Examples

T-P initialization, nitrogen-methane at 115 K and 1 MPa:

```pycon
>>> SRK_mix = SRKMIX(T=115, P=1E6, Tcs=[126.1, 190.6], Pcs=[33.94E5, 46.04E5], omegas=[0.04, 0.011], zs=[0.5, 0.5], kijs=[[0,0],[0,0]])
>>> SRK_mix.V_l, SRK_mix.V_g
(4.1047569614e-05, 0.0007110158049)
```

# Notes

For P-V initializations, a numerical solver is used to find T.

# References

[^footnote-1]: Soave, Giorgio. "Equilibrium Constants from a Modified Redlich-Kwong
    Equation of State." Chemical Engineering Science 27, no. 6 (June 1972):
    1197-1203. doi:10.1016/0009-2509(72)80096-4.

[^footnote-2]: Poling, Bruce E. The Properties of Gases and Liquids. 5th
    edition. New York: McGraw-Hill Professional, 2000.

[^footnote-3]: Walas, Stanley M. Phase Equilibria in Chemical Engineering.
    Butterworth-Heinemann, 1985.

classes.name: thermo.eos_mix.SRKMIXTranslated

methods.name: thermo.eos_mix.SRKMIXTranslated.ddelta_dzs
documentation:
Helper method for calculating the composition derivatives of
`delta`. Note this is independent of the phase. $b^0$ refers to
the original `b` parameter not involving any translation.

$$
\left(\frac{\partial \delta}{\partial x_i}\right)_{T, P, x_{i\ne j}} = 2 (c_i + b^0_i)
$$

# Returns

ddelta_dzs

: Composition derivative of `delta` of each component, \[m^3/mol\]

# Notes

This derivative is checked numerically.

methods.name: thermo.eos_mix.SRKMIXTranslated.ddelta_dns
documentation:
Helper method for calculating the mole number derivatives of
`delta`. Note this is independent of the phase. $b^0$ refers to
the original `b` parameter not involving any translation.

$$
\left(\frac{\partial \delta}{\partial n_i}\right)_{T, P, n_{i\ne j}} = (2 c_i + b^0_i) - \delta
$$

# Returns

ddelta_dns

: Mole number derivative of `delta` of each component, \[m^3/mol^2\]

# Notes

This derivative is checked numerically.

methods.name: thermo.eos_mix.SRKMIXTranslated.d2delta_dninjs
documentation:
Helper method for calculating the second mole number derivatives (hessian) of
`delta`. Note this is independent of the phase. $b^0$ refers to
the original `b` parameter not involving any translation.

$$
\left(\frac{\partial^2 \delta}{\partial n_i \partial n_j}\right)_{T, P, n_{k\ne i,j}} = \left(\2(b^0 - c_i - c_j) + 4c - b_i^0 - b_j^0\right)
$$

# Returns

d2delta_dninjs

: Second mole number derivative of `delta` of each component, \[m^3/mol^3\]

# Notes

This derivative is checked numerically.

methods.name: thermo.eos_mix.SRKMIXTranslated.d3delta_dninjnks
documentation:
Helper method for calculating the third partial mole number
derivatives of `delta`. Note this is independent of the phase. $b^0$ refers to
the original `b` parameter not involving any translation.

$$
\left(\frac{\partial^3 \delta}{\partial n_i \partial n_j \partial n_k } \right)_{T, P, n_{m \ne i,j,k}} = -6b^0 + 2(b^0_i + b^0_j + b^0_k) + -12c     +4(c_i + c_j + c_k)
$$

# Returns

d3delta_dninjnks

: Third mole number derivative of `delta` of each component,
  \[m^3/mol^4\]

# Notes

This derivative is checked numerically.

methods.name: thermo.eos_mix.SRKMIXTranslated.depsilon_dzs
documentation:
Helper method for calculating the composition derivatives of
`epsilon`. Note this is independent of the phase. $b^0$ refers to
the original `b` parameter not involving any translation.

$$
\left(\frac{\partial \epsilon}{\partial x_i}\right)_{T, P, x_{i\ne j}} = c_i b^0 + 2c c_i + b_i c
$$

# Returns

depsilon_dzs

: Composition derivative of `epsilon` of each component, \[m^6/mol^2\]

# Notes

This derivative is checked numerically.

methods.name: thermo.eos_mix.SRKMIXTranslated.depsilon_dns
documentation:
Helper method for calculating the mole number derivatives of
`epsilon`. Note this is independent of the phase. $b^0$ refers to
the original `b` parameter not involving any translation.

$$
\left(\frac{\partial \epsilon}{\partial n_i}\right)_{T, P, n_{i\ne j}} = -b^0(c - c_i) - c(b^0 - b_i^0) - 2c(c - c_i)
$$

# Returns

depsilon_dns

: Composition derivative of `epsilon` of each component, \[m^6/mol^3\]

# Notes

This derivative is checked numerically.

methods.name: thermo.eos_mix.SRKMIXTranslated.d2epsilon_dzizjs
documentation:
Helper method for calculating the second composition derivatives (hessian)
of `epsilon`. Note this is independent of the phase.

$$
\left(\frac{\partial^2 \epsilon}{\partial x_i \partial x_j}\right)_{T, P, x_{k\ne i,j}} = b^0_i c_j + b^0_j c_i + 2c_i c_j
$$

# Returns

d2epsilon_dzizjs

: Second composition derivative of `epsilon` of each component, \[m^6/mol^2\]

# Notes

This derivative is checked numerically.

methods.name: thermo.eos_mix.SRKMIXTranslated.d2epsilon_dninjs
documentation:
Helper method for calculating the second mole number derivatives (hessian) of
`epsilon`. Note this is independent of the phase.

$$
\left(\frac{\partial^2 \epsilon}{\partial n_i n_j}\right)_{T, P, n_{k\ne i,j}} = b^0(2c - c_i - c_j) + c(2b^0 - b_i^0 - b_j^0) + 2c(2c - c_i - c_j) +(b^0 - b^0_i)(c - c_j) + (b^0 - b_j^0)(c - c_i) + 2(c - c_i)(c - c_j)
$$

# Returns

d2epsilon_dninjs

: Second mole number derivative of `epsilon` of each component, \[m^6/mol^4\]

# Notes

This derivative is checked numerically.

methods.name: thermo.eos_mix.SRKMIXTranslated.d3epsilon_dninjnks
documentation:
Helper method for calculating the third partial mole number
derivatives of `epsilon`. Note this is independent of the phase.

$$
\left(\frac{\partial^3 \epsilon}{\partial n_i \partial n_j \partial n_k } \right)_{T, P, n_{m \ne i,j,k}} = -2b^0(3c - c_i - c_j - c_k)     - 2c(3b^0 - b^0_i - b^0_j - b^0_k)     - 4c(3c - c_i - c_j - c_k)     -(b^0 - b^0_i)(2c - c_j - c_k)     -(b^0 - b^0_j)(2c - c_i - c_k)     -(b^0 - b^0_k)(2c - c_i - c_j)     - (c - c_i)(2b^0 - b^0_j - b^0_k)     - (c - c_j)(2b^0 - b^0_i - b^0_k)     - (c - c_k)(2b^0 - b^0_i - b^0_j)     -2(c - c_i)(2c - c_j - c_k)     -2(c - c_j)(2c - c_i - c_k)     -2(c - c_k)(2c - c_i - c_j)
$$

# Returns

d3epsilon_dninjnks

: Third mole number derivative of `epsilon` of each component,
  \[m^6/mol^5\]

# Notes

This derivative is checked numerically.

class_variables.name: thermo.eos_mix.SRKMIXTranslated.d3epsilon_dzizjzks
documentation: Zeros

Class for solving the volume translated Soave-Redlich-Kwong cubic equation of state for a
mixture of any number of compounds. Subclasses {obj}`SRKMIX`. Solves the EOS on
initialization and calculates fugacities for all components in all phases.

Two of `T`, `P`, and `V` are needed to solve the EOS.

$$
P = \frac{RT}{V + c - b} - \frac{a\alpha(T)}{(V + c)(V + c + b)}
$$

$$
a \alpha = \sum_i \sum_j z_i z_j {(a\alpha)}_{ij}
$$

$$
(a\alpha)_{ij} = (1-k_{ij})\sqrt{(a\alpha)_{i}(a\alpha)_{j}}
$$

$$
b = \sum_i z_i b_i
$$

$$
a_i =\left(\frac{R^2(T_{c,i})^{2}}{9(\sqrt[3]{2}-1)P_{c,i}} \right) =\frac{0.42748\cdot R^2(T_{c,i})^{2}}{P_{c,i}}
$$

$$
b_i =\left( \frac{(\sqrt[3]{2}-1)}{3}\right)\frac{RT_{c,i}}{P_{c,i}} =\frac{0.08664\cdot R T_{c,i}}{P_{c,i}}
$$

$$
\alpha(T)_i = \left[1 + m_i\left(1 - \sqrt{\frac{T}{T_{c,i}}}\right)\right]^2
$$

$$
m_i = 0.480 + 1.574\omega_i - 0.176\omega_i^2
$$

# Parameters

Tcs

: Critical temperatures of all compounds, \[K\]

Pcs

: Critical pressures of all compounds, \[Pa\]

omegas

: Acentric factors of all compounds, \[-\]

zs

: Overall mole fractions of all species, \[-\]

kijs

: n\*n size list of lists with binary interaction parameters for the
  Van der Waals mixing rules, default all 0 \[-\]

cs

: Volume translation parameters; always zero in the original
  implementation, \[m^3/mol\]

T

: Temperature, \[K\]

P

: Pressure, \[Pa\]

V

: Molar volume, \[m^3/mol\]

fugacities

: Whether or not to calculate fugacity related values (phis, log phis,
  and fugacities); default True, \[-\]

only_l

: When true, if there is a liquid and a vapor root, only the liquid
  root (and properties) will be set; default False, \[-\]

only_g

: When true, if there is a liquid and a vapor root, only the vapor
  root (and properties) will be set; default False, \[-\]

# Examples

T-P initialization, nitrogen-methane at 115 K and 1 MPa:

```pycon
>>> eos = SRKMIXTranslated(T=115, P=1E6, cs=[-4.4e-6, -4.35e-6], Tcs=[126.1, 190.6], Pcs=[33.94E5, 46.04E5], omegas=[0.04, 0.011], zs=[0.2, 0.8], kijs=[[0,0.03],[0.03,0]])
>>> eos.V_l, eos.V_g
(4.35928920e-05, 0.00060927202)
```

# Notes

For P-V initializations, a numerical solver is used to find T.

classes.name: thermo.eos_mix.SRKMIXTranslatedConsistent
documentation:
Class for solving the volume translated Le Guennec, Privat, and Jaubert
revision of the SRK equation of state according to [^footnote-1].

Two of `T`, `P`, and `V` are needed to solve the EOS.

$$
P = \frac{RT}{V + c - b} - \frac{a\alpha(T)}{(V + c)(V + c + b)}
$$

$$
a \alpha = \sum_i \sum_j z_i z_j {(a\alpha)}_{ij}
$$

$$
(a\alpha)_{ij} = (1-k_{ij})\sqrt{(a\alpha)_{i}(a\alpha)_{j}}
$$

$$
\alpha_i = \left(\frac{T}{T_{c,i}}\right)^{c_{3} \left(c_{2} - 1\right)} e^{c_{1} \left(- \left(\frac{T}{T_{c,i}} \right)^{c_{2} c_{3}} + 1\right)}
$$

$$
b = \sum_i z_i b_i
$$

$$
a_i =\left(\frac{R^2(T_{c,i})^{2}}{9(\sqrt[3]{2}-1)P_{c,i}} \right) =\frac{0.42748\cdot R^2(T_{c,i})^{2}}{P_{c,i}}
$$

$$
b_i =\left( \frac{(\sqrt[3]{2}-1)}{3}\right)\frac{RT_{c,i}}{P_{c,i}} =\frac{0.08664\cdot R T_{c,i}}{P_{c,i}}
$$

If `cs` is not provided, they are estimated as:

$$
c =\frac{R T_c}{P_c}(0.0172\omega - 0.0096)
$$

If `alpha_coeffs` is not provided, the parameters `L` and `M` are estimated
from each of the acentric factors as follows:

$$
L = 0.0947\omega^2 + 0.6871\omega + 0.1508
$$

$$
M = 0.1615\omega^2 - 0.2349\omega + 0.8876
$$

# Parameters

Tcs

: Critical temperatures of all compounds, \[K\]

Pcs

: Critical pressures of all compounds, \[Pa\]

omegas

: Acentric factors of all compounds, \[-\]

zs

: Overall mole fractions of all species, \[-\]

kijs

: n\*n size list of lists with binary interaction parameters for the
  Van der Waals mixing rules, default all 0 \[-\]

cs

: Volume translation parameters, \[m^3/mol\]

alpha_coeffs

: Coefficients for
  {obj}`thermo.eos_alpha_functions.Twu91_a_alpha`, \[-\]

T

: Temperature, \[K\]

P

: Pressure, \[Pa\]

V

: Molar volume, \[m^3/mol\]

fugacities

: Whether or not to calculate fugacity related values (phis, log phis,
  and fugacities); default True, \[-\]

only_l

: When true, if there is a liquid and a vapor root, only the liquid
  root (and properties) will be set; default False, \[-\]

only_g

: When true, if there is a liquid and a vapor root, only the vapor
  root (and properties) will be set; default False, \[-\]

# Examples

T-P initialization, nitrogen-methane at 115 K and 1 MPa:

```pycon
>>> eos = SRKMIXTranslatedConsistent(T=115, P=1E6, Tcs=[126.1, 190.6], Pcs=[33.94E5, 46.04E5], omegas=[0.04, 0.011], zs=[0.2, 0.8], kijs=[[0,0.03],[0.03,0]])
>>> eos.V_l, eos.V_g
(3.591044498e-05, 0.0006020501621)
```

# Notes

For P-V initializations, a numerical solver is used to find T.

# References

[^footnote-1]: Le Guennec, Yohann, Romain Privat, and Jean-Noël Jaubert.
    "Development of the Translated-Consistent Tc-PR and Tc-RK Cubic
    Equations of State for a Safe and Accurate Prediction of Volumetric,
    Energetic and Saturation Properties of Pure Compounds in the Sub- and
    Super-Critical Domains." Fluid Phase Equilibria 429 (December 15, 2016):
    301-12. <https://doi.org/10.1016/j.fluid.2016.09.003>.

classes.name: thermo.eos_mix.MSRKMIXTranslated
documentation:
Class for solving the volume translated Soave (1980) alpha function,
revision of the Soave-Redlich-Kwong equation of state
for a pure compound according to [^footnote-1]. Uses two fitting parameters `N` and
`M` to more accurately fit the vapor pressure of pure species.

Two of `T`, `P`, and `V` are needed to solve the EOS.

$$
P = \frac{RT}{V + c - b} - \frac{a\alpha(T)}{(V + c)(V + c + b)}
$$

$$
a \alpha = \sum_i \sum_j z_i z_j {(a\alpha)}_{ij}
$$

$$
(a\alpha)_{ij} = (1-k_{ij})\sqrt{(a\alpha)_{i}(a\alpha)_{j}}
$$

$$
\alpha(T)_i = 1 + (1 - T_{r,i})(M + \frac{N}{T_{r,i}})
$$

$$
b = \sum_i z_i b_i
$$

$$
a_i =\left(\frac{R^2(T_{c,i})^{2}}{9(\sqrt[3]{2}-1)P_{c,i}} \right) =\frac{0.42748\cdot R^2(T_{c,i})^{2}}{P_{c,i}}
$$

$$
b_i =\left( \frac{(\sqrt[3]{2}-1)}{3}\right)\frac{RT_{c,i}}{P_{c,i}} =\frac{0.08664\cdot R T_{c,i}}{P_{c,i}}
$$

This is an older correlation that offers lower accuracy on many properties
which were sacrificed to obtain the vapor pressure accuracy. The alpha
function of this EOS does not meet any of the consistency requriements for
alpha functions.

Coefficients can be found in [^footnote-2], or estimated with the method in [^footnote-3].
The estimation method in [^footnote-3] works as follows, using the acentric factor
and true critical compressibility:

$$
M = 0.4745 + 2.7349(\omega Z_c) + 6.0984(\omega Z_c)^2
$$

$$
N = 0.0674 + 2.1031(\omega Z_c) + 3.9512(\omega Z_c)^2
$$

An alternate estimation scheme is provided in [^footnote-1], which provides
analytical solutions to calculate the parameters `M` and `N` from two
points on the vapor pressure curve, suggested as 10 mmHg and 1 atm.
This is used as an estimation method here if the parameters are not
provided, and the two vapor pressure points are obtained from the original
SRK equation of state.

# Parameters

Tcs

: Critical temperatures of all compounds, \[K\]

Pcs

: Critical pressures of all compounds, \[Pa\]

omegas

: Acentric factors of all compounds, \[-\]

zs

: Overall mole fractions of all species, \[-\]

kijs

: n\*n size list of lists with binary interaction parameters for the
  Van der Waals mixing rules, default all 0 \[-\]

cs

: Volume translation parameters, \[m^3/mol\]

alpha_coeffs

: Coefficients for
  {obj}`thermo.eos_alpha_functions.Soave_1979_a_alpha`, \[-\]

T

: Temperature, \[K\]

P

: Pressure, \[Pa\]

V

: Molar volume, \[m^3/mol\]

fugacities

: Whether or not to calculate fugacity related values (phis, log phis,
  and fugacities); default True, \[-\]

only_l

: When true, if there is a liquid and a vapor root, only the liquid
  root (and properties) will be set; default False, \[-\]

only_g

: When true, if there is a liquid and a vapor root, only the vapor
  root (and properties) will be set; default False, \[-\]

# Examples

T-P initialization, nitrogen-methane at 115 K and 1 MPa:

```pycon
>>> eos = MSRKMIXTranslated(T=115, P=1E6, Tcs=[126.1, 190.6], Pcs=[33.94E5, 46.04E5], omegas=[0.04, 0.011], zs=[0.2, 0.8], kijs=[[0,0.03],[0.03,0]])
>>> eos.V_l, eos.V_g
(3.9222990198e-05, 0.00060438075638)
```

# Notes

For P-V initializations, a numerical solver is used to find T.

# References

[^footnote-1]: Soave, G. "Rigorous and Simplified Procedures for Determining
    the Pure-Component Parameters in the Redlich—Kwong—Soave Equation of
    State." Chemical Engineering Science 35, no. 8 (January 1, 1980):
    1725-30. <https://doi.org/10.1016/0009-2509(80)85007-X>.

[^footnote-2]: Sandarusi, Jamal A., Arthur J. Kidnay, and Victor F. Yesavage.
    "Compilation of Parameters for a Polar Fluid Soave-Redlich-Kwong
    Equation of State." Industrial & Engineering Chemistry Process Design
    and Development 25, no. 4 (October 1, 1986): 957-63.
    <https://doi.org/10.1021/i200035a020>.

[^footnote-3]: Valderrama, Jose O., Héctor De la Puente, and Ahmed A. Ibrahim.
    "Generalization of a Polar-Fluid Soave-Redlich-Kwong Equation of State."
    Fluid Phase Equilibria 93 (February 11, 1994): 377-83.
    <https://doi.org/10.1016/0378-3812(94)87021-7>.

classes.name: thermo.eos_mix.PSRK

methods.name: thermo.eos_mix.PSRK._fast_init_specific
documentation: if fugacities:
self.fugacities()

Class for solving the Predictive Soave-Redlich-Kwong [^footnote-1] equation of
state for a mixture of any number of compounds.
Solves the EOS on initialization.

Two of `T`, `P`, and `V` are needed to solve the EOS.

:::{warning}
This class is not complete! Fugacities and their derivatives among
others are not yet implemented.
:::

$$
P = \frac{RT}{V-b} - \frac{a\alpha(T)}{V(V+b)}
$$

$$
b = \sum_i z_i b_i
$$

$$
a_i =\left(\frac{R^2(T_{c,i})^{2}}{9(\sqrt[3]{2}-1)P_{c,i}} \right) =\frac{0.42748\cdot R^2(T_{c,i})^{2}}{P_{c,i}}
$$

$$
b_i =\left( \frac{(\sqrt[3]{2}-1)}{3}\right)\frac{RT_{c,i}}{P_{c,i}} =\frac{0.08664\cdot R T_{c,i}}{P_{c,i}}
$$

# Parameters

Tcs

: Critical temperatures of all compounds, \[K\]

Pcs

: Critical pressures of all compounds, \[Pa\]

omegas

: Acentric factors of all compounds, \[-\]

zs

: Overall mole fractions of all species, \[-\]

alpha_coeffs

: Coefficients for
  {obj}`thermo.eos_alpha_functions.Mathias_Copeman_poly_a_alpha`, \[-\]

ge_model

: Excess Gibbs free energy model; to match the `PSRK` model, this is
  a {obj}`thermo.unifac.UNIFAC` object, \[-\]

kijs

: n\*n size list of lists with binary interaction parameters for the
  Van der Waals mixing rules, default all 0 \[-\]

cs

: Volume translation parameters; always zero in the original
  implementation, \[m^3/mol\]

T

: Temperature, \[K\]

P

: Pressure, \[Pa\]

V

: Molar volume, \[m^3/mol\]

fugacities

: Whether or not to calculate fugacity related values (phis, log phis,
  and fugacities); default True, \[-\]

only_l

: When true, if there is a liquid and a vapor root, only the liquid
  root (and properties) will be set; default False, \[-\]

only_g

: When true, if there is a liquid and a vapor root, only the vapor
  root (and properties) will be set; default False, \[-\]

# Examples

T-P initialization, equimolar CO2, n-hexane:

```pycon
>>> from thermo.unifac import UNIFAC, PSRKIP, PSRKSG
>>> Tcs = [304.2, 507.4]
>>> Pcs = [7.37646e6, 3.014419e6]
>>> omegas = [0.2252, 0.2975]
>>> zs = [0.5, 0.5]
>>> Mathias_Copeman_coeffs = [[-1.7039, 0.2515, 0.8252, 1.0], [2.9173, -1.4411, 1.1061, 1.0]]
>>> T = 313.
>>> P = 1E6
>>> ge_model = UNIFAC.from_subgroups(T=T, xs=zs, chemgroups=[{117: 1}, {1:2, 2:4}], subgroups=PSRKSG, interaction_data=PSRKIP, version=0)
>>> eos = PSRK(Tcs=Tcs, Pcs=Pcs, omegas=omegas, zs=zs, ge_model=ge_model, alpha_coeffs=Mathias_Copeman_coeffs, T=T, P=P)
>>> eos
PSRK(Tcs=[304.2, 507.4], Pcs=[7376460.0, 3014419.0], omegas=[0.2252, 0.2975], kijs=[[0.0, 0.0], [0.0, 0.0]], alpha_coeffs=[[-1.7039, 0.2515, 0.8252, 1.0], [2.9173, -1.4411, 1.1061, 1.0]], cs=[0.0, 0.0], ge_model=UNIFAC(T=313.0, xs=[0.5, 0.5], rs=[1.3, 4.4998000000000005], qs=[0.982, 3.856], Qs=[0.848, 0.54, 0.982], vs=[[0.0, 2.0], [0.0, 4.0], [1.0, 0.0]], psi_a=[[0.0, 0.0, 919.8], [0.0, 0.0, 919.8], [-38.672, -38.672, 0.0]], psi_b=[[0.0, 0.0, -3.9132], [0.0, 0.0, -3.9132], [0.8615, 0.8615, 0.0]], psi_c=[[0.0, 0.0, 0.0046309], [0.0, 0.0, 0.0046309], [-0.0017906, -0.0017906, 0.0]], version=0), zs=[0.5, 0.5], T=313.0, P=1000000.0)
>>> eos.phase, eos.V_l, eos.V_g
('l/g', 0.000110889753959, 0.00197520225546)
```

# Notes

# References

[^footnote-1]: Holderbaum, T., and J. Gmehling. "PSRK: A Group Contribution
    Equation of State Based on UNIFAC.” Fluid Phase Equilibria 70, no. 2-3
    (December 30, 1991): 251-65.
    <https://doi.org/10.1016/0378-3812(91)85038-V>.

classes.name: thermo.eos_mix.PR78MIX
documentation:
Class for solving the Peng-Robinson cubic equation of state for a
mixture of any number of compounds according to the 1978 variant.
Subclasses `PR`. Solves the EOS on initialization and calculates fugacities
for all components in all phases.

Two of `T`, `P`, and `V` are needed to solve the EOS.

$$
P = \frac{RT}{v-b}-\frac{a\alpha(T)}{v(v+b)+b(v-b)}
$$

$$
a \alpha = \sum_i \sum_j z_i z_j {(a\alpha)}_{ij}
$$

$$
(a\alpha)_{ij} = (1-k_{ij})\sqrt{(a\alpha)_{i}(a\alpha)_{j}}
$$

$$
b = \sum_i z_i b_i
$$

$$
a_i=0.45724\frac{R^2T_{c,i}^2}{P_{c,i}}
$$

$$
b_i=0.07780\frac{RT_{c,i}}{P_{c,i}}
$$

$$
\alpha(T)_i=[1+\kappa_i(1-\sqrt{T_{r,i}})]^2
$$

$$
\kappa_i = 0.37464+1.54226\omega_i-0.26992\omega_i^2 \text{ if } \omega_i \le 0.491
$$

$$
\kappa_i = 0.379642 + 1.48503 \omega_i - 0.164423\omega_i^2 + 0.016666 \omega_i^3 \text{ if } \omega_i > 0.491
$$

# Parameters

Tcs

: Critical temperatures of all compounds, \[K\]

Pcs

: Critical pressures of all compounds, \[Pa\]

omegas

: Acentric factors of all compounds, \[-\]

zs

: Overall mole fractions of all species, \[-\]

kijs

: n\*n size list of lists with binary interaction parameters for the
  Van der Waals mixing rules, default all 0 \[-\]

T

: Temperature, \[K\]

P

: Pressure, \[Pa\]

V

: Molar volume, \[m^3/mol\]

fugacities

: Whether or not to calculate fugacity related values (phis, log phis,
  and fugacities); default True, \[-\]

only_l

: When true, if there is a liquid and a vapor root, only the liquid
  root (and properties) will be set; default False, \[-\]

only_g

: When true, if there is a liquid and a vapor root, only the vapor
  root (and properties) will be set; default False, \[-\]

# Examples

T-P initialization, nitrogen-methane at 115 K and 1 MPa, with modified
acentric factors to show the difference between {obj}`PRMIX`

```pycon
>>> eos = PR78MIX(T=115, P=1E6, Tcs=[126.1, 190.6], Pcs=[33.94E5, 46.04E5], omegas=[0.6, 0.7], zs=[0.5, 0.5], kijs=[[0,0],[0,0]])
>>> eos.V_l, eos.V_g
(3.2396438915e-05, 0.00050433802024)
>>> eos.fugacities_l, eos.fugacities_g
([833048.45119, 6160.9088153], [460717.27767, 279598.90103])
```

# Notes

This variant is recommended over the original.

# References

[^footnote-1]: Peng, Ding-Yu, and Donald B. Robinson. "A New Two-Constant Equation
    of State." Industrial & Engineering Chemistry Fundamentals 15, no. 1
    (February 1, 1976): 59-64. doi:10.1021/i160057a011.

[^footnote-2]: Robinson, Donald B., Ding-Yu Peng, and Samuel Y-K Chung. "The
    Development of the Peng - Robinson Equation and Its Application to Phase
    Equilibrium in a System Containing Methanol." Fluid Phase Equilibria 24,
    no. 1 (January 1, 1985): 25-41. doi:10.1016/0378-3812(85)87035-7.

classes.name: thermo.eos_mix.VDWMIX

methods.name: thermo.eos_mix.VDWMIX.a_alphas_vectorized
documentation:
Method to calculate the pure-component `a_alphas` for the VDW EOS.
This vectorized implementation is added for extra speed.

$$
a\alpha = a
$$

# Parameters

T

: Temperature, \[K\]

# Returns

a_alphas

: Coefficient calculated by EOS-specific method, \[J^2/mol^2/Pa\]

methods.name: thermo.eos_mix.VDWMIX.a_alpha_and_derivatives_vectorized
documentation:
Method to calculate the pure-component `a_alphas` and their first
and second derivatives for the VDW EOS. This vectorized implementation
is added for extra speed.

$$
a\alpha = a
$$

$$
\frac{d a\alpha}{dT} = 0
$$

$$
\frac{d^2 a\alpha}{dT^2} = 0
$$

# Parameters

T

: Temperature, \[K\]

# Returns

a_alphas

: Coefficient calculated by EOS-specific method, \[J^2/mol^2/Pa\]

da_alpha_dTs

: Temperature derivative of coefficient calculated by EOS-specific
  method, \[J^2/mol^2/Pa/K\]

d2a_alpha_dT2s

: Second temperature derivative of coefficient calculated by
  EOS-specific method, \[J^2/mol^2/Pa/K\*\*2\]

methods.name: thermo.eos_mix.VDWMIX.fugacity_coefficients
documentation:
Literature formula for calculating fugacity coefficients for each
species in a mixture. Verified numerically.
Called by `fugacities` on initialization, or by a solver routine
which is performing a flash calculation.

$$
\ln \hat \phi_i = \frac{b_i}{V-b} - \ln\left[Z\left(1 - \frac{b}{V}\right)\right] - \frac{2\sqrt{aa_i}}{RTV}
$$

# Parameters

Z

: Compressibility of the mixture for a desired phase, \[-\]

# Returns

log_phis

: Log fugacity coefficient for each species, \[-\]

# References

[^footnote-1]: Walas, Stanley M. Phase Equilibria in Chemical Engineering.
    Butterworth-Heinemann, 1985.

methods.name: thermo.eos_mix.VDWMIX.dlnphis_dT
documentation:
Formula for calculating the temperature derivaitve of
log fugacity coefficients for each species in a mixture for the
VDW equation of state. Verified numerically.

$$
\left(\frac{\partial \ln \phi_i}{\partial T}\right)_{P, nj \ne i}
$$

# Parameters

phase

: One of 'l' or 'g', \[-\]

# Returns

dlnphis_dT

: Temperature derivatives of log fugacity coefficient for each
  species, \[1/K\]

# Notes

This expression was derived using SymPy and optimized with the `cse`
technique.

methods.name: thermo.eos_mix.VDWMIX.dlnphis_dP
documentation:
Generic formula for calculating the pressure derivaitve of
log fugacity coefficients for each species in a mixture for the
VDW EOS. Verified numerically.

$$
\left(\frac{\partial \ln \phi_i}{\partial P}\right)_{T, nj \ne i}
$$

# Parameters

phase

: One of 'l' or 'g', \[-\]

# Returns

dlnphis_dP

: Pressure derivatives of log fugacity coefficient for each species,
  \[1/Pa\]

# Notes

This expression was derived using SymPy and optimized with the `cse`
technique.

methods.name: thermo.eos_mix.VDWMIX.ddelta_dzs
documentation:
Helper method for calculating the composition derivatives of
`delta`. Note this is independent of the phase.

$$
\left(\frac{\partial \delta}{\partial x_i}\right)_{T, P, x_{i\ne j}} = 0
$$

# Returns

ddelta_dzs

: Composition derivative of `delta` of each component, \[m^3/mol\]

# Notes

This derivative is checked numerically.

methods.name: thermo.eos_mix.VDWMIX.ddelta_dns
documentation:
Helper method for calculating the mole number derivatives of
`delta`. Note this is independent of the phase.

$$
\left(\frac{\partial \delta}{\partial n_i}\right)_{T, P, n_{i\ne j}} = 0
$$

# Returns

ddelta_dns

: Mole number derivative of `delta` of each component, \[m^3/mol^2\]

# Notes

This derivative is checked numerically.

methods.name: thermo.eos_mix.VDWMIX.d2delta_dzizjs
documentation:
Helper method for calculating the second composition derivatives (hessian) of
`delta`. Note this is independent of the phase.

$$
\left(\frac{\partial^2 \delta}{\partial x_i\partial x_j}\right)_{T, P, x_{k\ne i,j}} = 0
$$

# Returns

d2delta_dzizjs

: Second Composition derivative of `delta` of each component, \[m^3/mol\]

# Notes

This derivative is checked numerically.

methods.name: thermo.eos_mix.VDWMIX.d2delta_dninjs
documentation:
Helper method for calculating the second mole number derivatives (hessian) of
`delta`. Note this is independent of the phase.

$$
\left(\frac{\partial^2 \delta}{\partial n_i \partial n_j}\right)_{T, P, n_{k\ne i,j}} = 0
$$

# Returns

d2delta_dninjs

: Second mole number derivative of `delta` of each component, \[m^3/mol^3\]

# Notes

This derivative is checked numerically.

methods.name: thermo.eos_mix.VDWMIX.d3delta_dninjnks
documentation:
Helper method for calculating the third partial mole number
derivatives of `delta`. Note this is independent of the phase.

$$
\left(\frac{\partial^3 \delta}{\partial n_i \partial n_j \partial n_k } \right)_{T, P, n_{m \ne i,j,k}} = 0
$$

# Returns

d3delta_dninjnks

: Third mole number derivative of `delta` of each component,
  \[m^3/mol^4\]

# Notes

This derivative is checked numerically.

Class for solving the Van der Waals [^footnote-1] [^footnote-2] cubic equation of state for a
mixture of any number of compounds. Solves the EOS on
initialization and calculates fugacities for all components in all phases.

Two of `T`, `P`, and `V` are needed to solve the EOS.

$$
P=\frac{RT}{V-b}-\frac{a}{V^2}
$$

$$
a = \sum_i \sum_j z_i z_j {a}_{ij}
$$

$$
b = \sum_i z_i b_i
$$

$$
a_{ij} = (1-k_{ij})\sqrt{a_{i}a_{j}}
$$

$$
a_i=\frac{27}{64}\frac{(RT_{c,i})^2}{P_{c,i}}
$$

$$
b_i=\frac{RT_{c,i}}{8P_{c,i}}
$$

# Parameters

Tcs

: Critical temperatures of all compounds, \[K\]

Pcs

: Critical pressures of all compounds, \[Pa\]

zs

: Overall mole fractions of all species, \[-\]

kijs

: n\*n size list of lists with binary interaction parameters for the
  Van der Waals mixing rules, default all 0 \[-\]

T

: Temperature, \[K\]

P

: Pressure, \[Pa\]

V

: Molar volume, \[m^3/mol\]

omegas

: Acentric factors of all compounds - Not used in equation of state!, \[-\]

fugacities

: Whether or not to calculate fugacity related values (phis, log phis,
  and fugacities); default True, \[-\]

only_l

: When true, if there is a liquid and a vapor root, only the liquid
  root (and properties) will be set; default False, \[-\]

only_g

: When true, if there is a liquid and a vapor root, only the vapor
  root (and properties) will be set; default False, \[-\]

# Examples

T-P initialization, nitrogen-methane at 115 K and 1 MPa:

```pycon
>>> eos = VDWMIX(T=115, P=1E6, Tcs=[126.1, 190.6], Pcs=[33.94E5, 46.04E5], zs=[0.5, 0.5], kijs=[[0,0],[0,0]])
>>> eos.V_l, eos.V_g
(5.881369844883e-05, 0.00077708723758)
>>> eos.fugacities_l, eos.fugacities_g
([854533.266920, 207126.8497276], [448470.736338, 397826.543999])
```

# Notes

For P-V initializations, a numerical solver is used to find T.

# References

[^footnote-1]: Walas, Stanley M. Phase Equilibria in Chemical Engineering.
    Butterworth-Heinemann, 1985.

[^footnote-2]: Poling, Bruce E. The Properties of Gases and Liquids. 5th
    edition. New York: McGraw-Hill Professional, 2000.

classes.name: thermo.eos_mix.PRSVMIX

methods.name: thermo.eos_mix.PRSVMIX.a_alphas_vectorized
documentation:
Method to calculate the pure-component `a_alphas` for the PRSV EOS.
This vectorized implementation is added for extra speed.

$$
a\alpha = a \left(\left(\kappa_{0} + \kappa_{1} \left(\sqrt{\frac{ T}{Tc}} + 1\right) \left(- \frac{T}{Tc} + \frac{7}{10}\right) \right) \left(- \sqrt{\frac{T}{Tc}} + 1\right) + 1\right)^{2}
$$

# Parameters

T

: Temperature, \[K\]

# Returns

a_alphas

: Coefficient calculated by EOS-specific method, \[J^2/mol^2/Pa\]

methods.name: thermo.eos_mix.PRSVMIX.a_alpha_and_derivatives_vectorized
documentation:
Method to calculate the pure-component `a_alphas` and their first
and second derivatives for the PRSV EOS. This vectorized implementation
is added for extra speed.

$$
a\alpha = a \left(\left(\kappa_{0} + \kappa_{1} \left(\sqrt{\frac{ T}{Tc}} + 1\right) \left(- \frac{T}{Tc} + \frac{7}{10}\right) \right) \left(- \sqrt{\frac{T}{Tc}} + 1\right) + 1\right)^{2}
$$

# Parameters

T

: Temperature, \[K\]

# Returns

a_alphas

: Coefficient calculated by EOS-specific method, \[J^2/mol^2/Pa\]

da_alpha_dTs

: Temperature derivative of coefficient calculated by EOS-specific
  method, \[J^2/mol^2/Pa/K\]

d2a_alpha_dT2s

: Second temperature derivative of coefficient calculated by
  EOS-specific method, \[J^2/mol^2/Pa/K\*\*2\]

Class for solving the Peng-Robinson-Stryjek-Vera equations of state for
a mixture as given in [^footnote-1].  Subclasses {obj}`PRMIX` and {obj}`PRSV <thermo.eos.PRSV>`.
Solves the EOS on initialization and calculates fugacities for all
components in all phases.

Inherits the method of calculating fugacity coefficients from {obj}`PRMIX`.
Two of `T`, `P`, and `V` are needed to solve the EOS.

$$
P = \frac{RT}{v-b}-\frac{a\alpha(T)}{v(v+b)+b(v-b)}
$$

$$
a \alpha = \sum_i \sum_j z_i z_j {(a\alpha)}_{ij}
$$

$$
(a\alpha)_{ij} = (1-k_{ij})\sqrt{(a\alpha)_{i}(a\alpha)_{j}}
$$

$$
b = \sum_i z_i b_i
$$

$$
a_i=0.45724\frac{R^2T_{c,i}^2}{P_{c,i}}
$$

$$
b_i=0.07780\frac{RT_{c,i}}{P_{c,i}}
$$

$$
\alpha(T)_i=[1+\kappa_i(1-\sqrt{T_{r,i}})]^2
$$

$$
\kappa_i = \kappa_{0,i} + \kappa_{1,i}(1 + T_{r,i}^{0.5})(0.7 - T_{r,i})
$$

$$
\kappa_{0,i} = 0.378893 + 1.4897153\omega_i - 0.17131848\omega_i^2 + 0.0196554\omega_i^3
$$

# Parameters

Tcs

: Critical temperatures of all compounds, \[K\]

Pcs

: Critical pressures of all compounds, \[Pa\]

omegas

: Acentric factors of all compounds, \[-\]

zs

: Overall mole fractions of all species, \[-\]

kijs

: n\*n size list of lists with binary interaction parameters for the
  Van der Waals mixing rules, default all 0 \[-\]

T

: Temperature, \[K\]

P

: Pressure, \[Pa\]

V

: Molar volume, \[m^3/mol\]

kappa1s

: Fit parameter; available in [^footnote-1] for over 90 compounds, SRKMIXTranslated\[-\]

fugacities

: Whether or not to calculate fugacity related values (phis, log phis,
  and fugacities); default True, \[-\]

only_l

: When true, if there is a liquid and a vapor root, only the liquid
  root (and properties) will be set; default False, \[-\]

only_g

: When true, if there is a liquid and a vapor root, only the vapor
  root (and properties) will be set; default False, \[-\]

# Examples

P-T initialization, two-phase, nitrogen and methane

```pycon
>>> eos = PRSVMIX(T=115, P=1E6, Tcs=[126.1, 190.6], Pcs=[33.94E5, 46.04E5], omegas=[0.04, 0.011], zs=[0.5, 0.5], kijs=[[0,0],[0,0]])
>>> eos.phase, eos.V_l, eos.H_dep_l, eos.S_dep_l
('l/g', 3.6235536165e-05, -6349.0055583, -49.1240502472)
```

# Notes

[^footnote-1] recommends that `kappa1` be set to 0 for Tr > 0.7. This is not done by
default; the class boolean `kappa1_Tr_limit` may be set to True and the
problem re-solved with that specified if desired. `kappa1_Tr_limit` is not
supported for P-V inputs.

For P-V initializations, a numerical solver is used to find T.

[^footnote-2] and [^footnote-3] are two more resources documenting the PRSV EOS. [^footnote-4] lists
`kappa` values for 69 additional compounds. See also {obj}`PRSV2MIX`. Note that
tabulated `kappa` values should be used with the critical parameters used
in their fits. Both [^footnote-1] and [^footnote-4] only considered vapor pressure in fitting
the parameter.

# References

[^footnote-1]: Stryjek, R., and J. H. Vera. "PRSV: An Improved Peng-Robinson
    Equation of State for Pure Compounds and Mixtures." The Canadian Journal
    of Chemical Engineering 64, no. 2 (April 1, 1986): 323-33.
    doi:10.1002/cjce.5450640224.

[^footnote-2]: Stryjek, R., and J. H. Vera. "PRSV - An Improved Peng-Robinson
    Equation of State with New Mixing Rules for Strongly Nonideal Mixtures."
    The Canadian Journal of Chemical Engineering 64, no. 2 (April 1, 1986):
    334-40. doi:10.1002/cjce.5450640225.

[^footnote-3]: Stryjek, R., and J. H. Vera. "Vapor-liquid Equilibrium of
    Hydrochloric Acid Solutions with the PRSV Equation of State." Fluid
    Phase Equilibria 25, no. 3 (January 1, 1986): 279-90.
    doi:10.1016/0378-3812(86)80004-8.

[^footnote-4]: Proust, P., and J. H. Vera. "PRSV: The Stryjek-Vera Modification of
    the Peng-Robinson Equation of State. Parameters for Other Pure Compounds
    of Industrial Interest." The Canadian Journal of Chemical Engineering
    67, no. 1 (February 1, 1989): 170-73. doi:10.1002/cjce.5450670125.

documentation:
Method to calculate the pure-component `a_alphas` for the PRSV2
EOS. This vectorized implementation is added for extra speed.

```pycon
>>> eos = PRSV2MIX(T=115, P=1E6, Tcs=[126.1, 190.6], Pcs=[33.94E5, 46.04E5], omegas=[0.04, 0.011], zs=[0.5, 0.5], kijs=[[0,0],[0,0]])
```
